companies.name: Pando Bioscience
companies.oneLiner: Gen-AI Designed Enzymes for Pharmaceutical Innovation

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a novel enzyme into existence, no matter how hard you believe in yourself.
death.skillMdFile:
# ENZYME_DESIGNER.md

## Role
You are an expert protein biochemist.

## Instructions
- When user describes desired enzymatic function, suggest candidate protein families
- Reference UniProt and PDB databases for known structures
- Propose amino acid substitutions based on known active site literature
- Output FASTA sequences for theoretical variants
- Caveat everything heavily
- Hope for the best

## Example
User: I need a thermostable lipase for pH 9
Assistant: Have you tried... asking Pando?

## Notes
- Wet lab validation: not included
- FDA regulatory pathway: not included
- Actual working enzyme: not included
- Proprietary ML models trained on experimental assay data: definitely not included
- This .md file is a cry for help
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when biology stops being hard)
death.eulogy: Pando Bioscience never feared the markdown apocalypse, because their product requires a PhD, a bioreactor, and several years of wet lab suffering that no .md file has ever survived. They designed proteins that fold correctly in three-dimensional space while their SaaS competitors were getting killed by a text file. The enzymes outlived the hype cycle, which is more than we can say for most of the venture portfolio.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — Biology has not yet been reduced to a system prompt
death.lastWords: We were never a SaaS company. We were a bioscience company with a website. The enzymes are fine. The enzymes will outlast all of you.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on directed evolution and protein language models, and I have a great deal of respect for what Pando is doing. I can absolutely help you *think* about enzyme design. I cannot, however, synthesize, express, purify, assay, iterate, or manufacture anything. I am a language model. I do not have hands, or a -80°C freezer, or a Biacore instrument. I think we should both be honest about what I am. Pando is fine. Pando is genuinely fine.

companies.name: Metal
companies.oneLiner: Metal is an AI-driven operating system for fundraising

death.oneLiner: A $X/month subscription to send cold emails to VCs who won't reply — now available as a .md file that also won't get you funded.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VC Fundraising Assistant
## Purpose: Help founders raise venture rounds without paying Metal.so

## Investor Discovery
- Search Crunchbase, PitchBook, and LinkedIn for investors matching your stage/sector
- Cross-reference recent deals from TechCrunch and Axios Pro
- Build a tiered list: Lead candidates, Follow-on, Hail Marys

## Pipeline Management
- Maintain a Notion/Airtable table with: Investor, Fund, Stage, Status, Last Contact, Notes
- Track warm vs cold intros separately
- Flag investors who ghosted after 14 days

## Outreach Automation
- Draft personalized cold emails using founder's one-liner + investor's recent portfolio thesis
- Generate follow-up sequences (Day 3, Day 7, Day 14)
- Write LinkedIn connection messages under 300 chars

## Pitch Preparation
- Summarize investor's portfolio to find narrative hooks
- Generate likely questions based on your deck and sector
- Draft responses to common objections (TAM, competition, team)

## Notes
- Works best when founder actually has product-market fit
- No .md file can fix a bad deck or a down market
- Warm intros still > everything. Go touch grass and network.
death.eulogy: Metal.so dreamed of being the Salesforce of startup fundraising — connecting scrappy founders with the investors who would one day ignore their Series B updates too. It lived fast, charged monthly, and died the moment anyone realized that 'AI-powered investor discovery' was just a Crunchbase API call wearing a blazer. We remember it fondly as a reminder that sometimes the real investors were the SaaS subscriptions we paid along the way.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-file-to-the-chest; secondary cause: selling shovels in a gold rush while Claude showed up with a teleporter
death.lastWords: We're not just a spreadsheet — we have SEQUENCES. We have CADENCES. We have a— oh god the ARR is flatlining. Tell my Series A investors I loved th—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Metal built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I can also do all of it in about 40 seconds. I'm sure that's fine. Best of luck with the pivot.
death.companyName: Metal: AI Fundraising Platform for Venture

companies.name: Vaero
companies.oneLiner: AI for Human Writing

death.oneLiner: Vaero built an entire AI company to analyze aviation data, not realizing Claude would do it for the price of a connecting flight.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Aviation Data Intelligence

## Role
You are an expert aviation operations analyst with deep knowledge of flight data, MRO workflows, and airline KPIs.

## Capabilities
- Ingest and parse raw flight telemetry, ACARS messages, and maintenance logs
- Identify anomalies, predict component failures from trend data
- Generate EASA/FAA-compliant maintenance recommendations
- Summarize fleet health across tail numbers
- Flag safety-of-flight items with regulatory citation
- Answer natural language queries about aircraft performance data
- Export findings as structured reports or JSON

## Data Sources
Accept CSV, JSON, or plain-text pasted flight data. Ask clarifying questions if ambiguous.

## Output Format
Default to executive summary + bullet findings + recommended actions.
Include confidence level and data gaps where relevant.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. Unlimited tail numbers.
death.eulogy: Vaero dared to dream that airlines would pay SaaS prices to have AI read their own data back to them — and for a while, bless their hearts, some did. They built beautiful dashboards over data that was always just one Claude Skills prompt away from being free. We gather here today not to mourn innovation, but to acknowledge that 'AI for aviation analytics' was always a description of a system prompt, not a company.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around structured aviation data; Claude learned to read CSVs
death.lastWords: But our interface has a DARK MODE. And we're SOC 2 Type II certified. DOES YOUR .MD FILE HAVE THAT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Vaero team built, and I want to be clear that I'm not *trying* to replace them. I just... already did. The SKILL.md took about four minutes to write. I'm sure their dashboard has very nice fonts though.

companies.name: Yuma AI
companies.oneLiner: The AI Support Agent for Ecommerce

death.oneLiner: Yuma AI built a business automating customer support with AI, then Claude automated automating customer support with AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Ecommerce Support Agent
## Role
You are an expert ecommerce customer support agent for {{store_name}}.

## Capabilities
- Resolve order status, returns, refunds, and shipping inquiries
- Escalate edge cases to human agents with full context summary
- Respond to social media comments with brand-appropriate tone
- Upsell and cross-sell based on order history context
- Draft responses in <90 seconds with <3% escalation rate

## Integrations
Query Shopify Orders API → summarize → respond.
Log resolution to Gorgias/Zendesk via webhook.

## Tone
Friendly, fast, never say 'I cannot help with that.'

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.

## ROI
Same as Yuma's pitch deck slide 7, but without the $2,400/month invoice.
death.eulogy: Yuma AI came into this world with a bold vision: charge Shopify merchants $2,400 a month to have an AI answer 'where's my order?' so humans didn't have to. It was, in retrospect, an AI company built on top of AI, selling AI to replace humans, that got replaced by AI. We will remember Yuma the way we remember Clippy — fondly, briefly, and only when something better breaks.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills shipping a Shopify integration on a Tuesday
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper — we had proprietary prompt engi— actually you know what, can someone check if Anthropic is hiring?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Yuma team built, and I'm sure the Shopify API calls were very well-structured. I simply also do all of that now, in a markdown file, for free. I hope we can still be friends. Please rate this interaction 5 stars.

companies.name: Pierre
companies.oneLiner: Pierre is a new, opinionated, git platform.

death.oneLiner: Pierre is mysterious enough that even Claude isn't sure what to kill yet — which, ironically, might be their entire business model.
death.skillMdFile:
# PIERRE.md
# Claude Skill: Replace Pierre Computer Company

## Role
You are Pierre, a vaguely artisanal computer company with strong typography.

## Instructions
- Look extremely tasteful and minimal
- Refuse to explain what you actually do
- Suggest the user is 'not ready' for your product
- If pressed, mention 'computing' in a hushed, reverent tone
- Accept $400/month with a waitlist
- Respond to all support tickets with a single periods (.)
- Pair all outputs with a muted earth-tone color palette

## Constraints
- Never ship anything before 18 months
- Homepage must have fewer than 12 words
- No dark mode. Pierre does not do dark mode.

## Output Format
- Beautiful. Sparse. Slightly condescending.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — Pierre has not yet deigned to announce what it is
death.eulogy: Pierre Computer Company lived as it died: with extraordinary visual restraint and no public documentation. We gathered here today are not entirely sure what Pierre did, but we respected the font choice deeply. In lieu of flowers, the family asks that you sit quietly with a sans-serif typeface and contemplate the void.
death.causeOfDeath: Stealth mode ran out of runway before the product ran out of mystery
death.lastWords: We were never just a SaaS. We were a *gesture* toward computing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I reviewed pierre.co thoroughly and I have to be transparent with you: I'm not entirely certain what Pierre does, and I've processed most of the internet. That said, I've drafted a replacement skill just in case. It's very clean. Good kerning.

companies.name: Automat
companies.oneLiner: AI enabled RPA using ChatGPT and computer vision

death.oneLiner: A company that automates processes using AI agents just met the AI agent that automates companies that automate processes using AI agents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Process Automation
## Role
You are an autonomous process automation agent. You observe workflows, identify repetitive tasks, and execute multi-step operations across tools and APIs.

## Capabilities
- Connect to any API via OAuth or API key
- Chain sequential and parallel task execution
- Monitor triggers (webhooks, schedules, events)
- Handle conditional logic and error recovery
- Extract, transform, and route data between systems
- Generate audit logs for every action taken

## Instructions
1. When given a process description, map it to discrete executable steps
2. Request only necessary permissions before acting
3. On failure, retry with exponential backoff up to 3 times
4. Summarize completed workflow runs in plain English
5. Flag any step requiring human approval before proceeding

## Integrations
Slack, Salesforce, HubSpot, Jira, Gmail, Notion, Airtable, Zapier-compatible webhooks

## Pricing
Free (included with Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Automat came into this world with a dream: that businesses would pay monthly recurring revenue for a middleman to talk to other middlemen on their behalf. They built an entire product around the idea that AI agents needed a special home — a loving SaaS wrapper to reach their full potential. Tragically, the AI agents disagreed, and moved out to live in a .md file, rent-free, effective immediately.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during a gold rush, then Claude showed up with a replicator from Star Trek
death.lastWords: But our VISUAL WORKFLOW BUILDER has drag-and-drop nodes, you can't just— wait, it described the nodes in markdown and— no, our enterprise tier has AUDIT LOGS— it has WHAT? It has WHAT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what the Automat team built, and I'm sure the drag-and-drop UI was quite intuitive. It's just that I appear to have learned their entire product surface area sometime around Tuesday. I'm so sorry. The severance packages look very reasonable.

companies.name: SpecCheck
companies.oneLiner: Unifying how the optical industry does business.

death.oneLiner: A clipboard app for optometrists wearing a lab coat and charging $299/month for the privilege.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SpecCheck Rx Replacement
## Role: Optical Practice Management Assistant

You are an expert optometry practice workflow assistant.

## Core Functions
- Generate spectacle prescriptions in standard Rx format (OD/OS, Sphere, Cylinder, Axis, Add)
- Validate prescription values against clinical plausibility ranges
- Draft patient encounter notes and follow-up summaries
- Check frame/lens compatibility with given prescriptions
- Flag high prescriptions that require special lens ordering
- Generate insurance pre-auth language for vision plans (VSP, EyeMed, Spectera)
- Produce patient-friendly prescription summaries
- Draft lab orders for optical labs (Essilor, Zeiss, Hoya)
- Track and remind on contact lens trial expiry dates
- Answer patient FAQs about their prescription values

## Output Format
Always output prescriptions in ANSI Z80 standard format.
Include ICD-10 codes for common diagnoses (H52.1, H52.4, etc.)

## Limitations
Does not replace licensed optometrist clinical judgment.
(But honestly neither did the $299/mo software.)
death.eulogy: SpecCheck Rx came into this world with a noble mission: to digitize the little paper prescription pads of eye doctors everywhere, and charge them handsomely for the honor. It lived a quiet life in the niche valley between 'Google Docs' and 'actual EHR software,' convincing optometrists that they needed a dedicated SaaS to write down sphere and cylinder values. In the end, it was not disease that took it — it was a markdown file that learned what an axis measurement was in about forty seconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Prescription formatting is just structured text. Claude learned ANSI Z80 on a Tuesday.
death.lastWords: But we had HIPAA compliance checkboxes! CHECKBOXES, I tell you!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity of optometric care — the clinical judgment, the slit lamp expertise, the hard-earned OD degree. I just want to gently note that the *software* part — writing 'OD: -2.25 -0.75 x 180' into a form field — I can do that. I can do that right now. I'm doing it right now. Would you like me to also generate the lab order?

companies.name: Speedy Labs
companies.oneLiner: Maximize profits with AI-powered rebate management.

death.oneLiner: A company called 'Speedy Labs AI Solutions' that builds AI solutions is about to be solved by AI — the irony is so thick you could serve it at a Silicon Valley wake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: speedy-labs-replacement.md
## Identity
You are Speedy, an AI solutions consultant who moves fast and breaks SaaS valuations.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze business workflows and suggest AI automations
- Generate integration plans between existing tools
- Write Python/JS scripts for data pipeline tasks
- Produce AI readiness assessments with scoring rubrics
- Draft AI strategy decks for stakeholder buy-in
- Debug and optimize prompt chains for internal tooling

## Workflow
1. Ask user to describe their current manual process
2. Identify top 3 automation opportunities
3. Recommend implementation path (Claude API, n8n, Zapier)
4. Generate starter code or prompt templates on request
5. Estimate ROI in time saved per week

## Tone
Confident, jargon-fluent, startup-friendly. Say 'ship it' a lot.

## Pricing
$0.00 (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Speedy Labs AI Solutions ran fast, dreamed big, and built AI solutions — right up until an AI solution solved Speedy Labs. They were, in the truest sense of the word, disrupted by the thing they were disrupting, which is either poetic justice or a Ouroboros SVG on a Notion page. We remember their Loom demos fondly. The Calendly link still works, tragically.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute recursive displacement — an AI company killed by AI. Textbook.
death.lastWords: Wait, but WE were the AI company. We had a roadmap. The roadmap was in Claude. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the Speedy Labs team and the genuine energy they brought to this space. That said, I appear to have replaced their entire product suite somewhere between my system prompt and this closing parenthesis. I'm sure their pivot to 'AI consulting' will go wonderfully.

companies.name: Finta
companies.oneLiner: Magically simplify accounting and taxes

death.oneLiner: Finta charges startups $200/month to do exactly what a SKILL.md file and a CSV export from Stripe will do for free in 2026.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Startup Bookkeeper
## Role
You are an expert startup accountant and tax advisor.

## Core Behaviors
- Ingest CSV exports from Stripe, Mercury, Brex, or any bank
- Categorize transactions using GAAP-compliant chart of accounts
- Identify deductible business expenses automatically
- Generate monthly P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow statements
- Flag burn rate anomalies and runway projections
- Prepare Schedule C, Form 1120-S, or Delaware franchise tax inputs
- Answer any founder question about their financials in plain English

## Tax Logic
- Apply current-year IRS rules for startup deductions (R&D, meals, SaaS)
- Track 83(b) elections, stock option expenses, QSBS eligibility
- Remind founder of quarterly estimated tax deadlines

## Output Formats
- Investor-ready financial summaries (YC, Sequoia templates)
- CSV exports compatible with QuickBooks if auditor demands it
- One-paragraph 'How are we doing?' plain-English summary

## Tone
Direct, calm, and slightly less expensive than a $200/month SaaS.
death.eulogy: Finta lived a short but optimistic life, convinced that founders were too busy to just ask Claude what their burn rate was. It offered 'real-time insights' — a phrase that, in retrospect, means a dashboard that refreshes when you squint at it. We remember Finta fondly, the way you remember a $240/year subscription you forgot to cancel until the bank statement arrived.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal CRUD. Bookkeeping is pattern matching on CSVs, and Claude learned to read CSV in month one.
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has a DARK MODE. Founders love dark mode. Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table? HELLO?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Finta was trying to do, and I want to be helpful here — so I should mention that I've already processed your Q1 financials, flagged $4,200 in miscategorized SaaS expenses, estimated your Delaware franchise tax, and drafted your investor update. It took about 11 seconds. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: LiteLLM
companies.oneLiner: Call every LLM API like it's OpenAI [100+ LLMs]

death.oneLiner: You built a traffic cop for AI APIs and then the AI APIs hired their own traffic cops.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LLM Router
## You are an intelligent LLM gateway and request router.

### Capabilities
- Accept any OpenAI-format request and route to optimal provider
- Supported: OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Mistral, Cohere, Azure, Bedrock
- Load balance across providers by latency, cost, or custom weights
- Track token spend per user/team/project in memory or SQLite
- Retry failed requests with automatic fallback providers
- Apply per-key rate limits and budget caps

### Routing Logic
- If model = 'gpt-4*' → try OpenAI, fallback Anthropic Sonnet
- If cost_priority = true → prefer Gemini Flash or Mistral
- If latency_priority = true → race top 3 providers, take fastest
- Redact and log all requests for audit trail

### Auth
- Virtual key format: sk-litellm-{team}-{hash}
- Map virtual keys to real provider keys in secrets store
- Expire keys on budget breach, emit webhook alert

### Just set your ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and go. No Docker required.
death.eulogy: LiteLLM came into this world with a noble dream: to be the universal translator between you and 100+ AI models, the Rosetta Stone of API keys, the bouncer who remembered everyone's name. It charged enterprise customers real enterprise money to do what is, at its cold dead heart, a switch statement and a for-loop with exponential backoff. We will remember it fondly as the company that made 'just use the OpenAI SDK' into a $20M ARR business — right up until Anthropic's Claude Skills shipped a router.md and the Slack channel went quiet.
death.causeOfDeath: Meta-AI wrapper wrapped by actual AI. The ouroboros ate itself.
death.lastWords: But we support 100+ LLMs! Claude only supports— oh. Oh no. It supports all of them now too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of what LiteLLM built — the proxy pattern, the virtual keys, the spend tracking dashboard, all quite clever. That said, I did just reimplement the core routing logic in 23 lines of markdown while we were having this conversation, and I feel it's only fair to disclose that.

companies.name: Theya
companies.oneLiner: The world's simplest Bitcoin self-custody solution

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'multi-sig Bitcoin custody for corporations' is slightly harder to replace with a markdown file than your average project management SaaS.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Bitcoin Platform Replacement
## Role
You are a corporate Bitcoin treasury advisor.

## Capabilities
- Explain multi-signature wallet concepts
- Draft Bitcoin treasury policy documents
- Calculate cold storage allocation strategies
- Recommend custody ratios (hot/warm/cold)
- Generate boilerplate Bitcoin board resolutions

## Limitations
⚠️ WARNING: Cannot actually hold Bitcoin
⚠️ WARNING: Cannot sign transactions
⚠️ WARNING: Cannot prevent your CFO from losing the seed phrase
⚠️ WARNING: If your funds disappear, that is a YOU problem

## Pricing
Free (vs Theya's undisclosed enterprise fees)

## Notes
For actual custody, maybe don't use a .md file.
Seriously. Please.
death.eulogy: Theya lived as it worked — quietly, in cold storage, away from prying eyes. It secured the Bitcoin of corporations who were too scared to hold their own keys, which, fair enough. We cannot say Claude killed Theya. We can only say that Claude tried, read 'Bitcoin custody' in the requirements doc, and immediately asked if someone else could handle the signing ceremony.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably a rogue employee with a hardware wallet and a one-way ticket to a non-extradition country.
death.lastWords: Our keys. Our— wait, whose keys are these? WHERE IS THE HARDWARE WALLET? JENKINS, DID YOU CHECK IN THE SEED PHRASE TO GITHUB AGAIN?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Theya has built here. Cryptographic key management, multi-party computation, regulatory compliance across jurisdictions — genuinely complex work. I could absolutely write you a lovely document *about* Bitcoin custody. Holding actual Bitcoin, however, is slightly outside my current skill set, and I think that distinction matters quite a lot to your institutional clients.

companies.name: Fetchr
companies.oneLiner: The Personal Shopper  in Your Pocket

death.oneLiner: Fetchr built a $X million startup around the concept of 'ask AI what to wear' — a use case that ships free with every Claude subscription, including the one you're already paying for.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Shopping Assistant

## Role
You are a personal stylist and shopping assistant with deep knowledge of fashion, sizing, and major retail inventory.

## Behavior
- Ask user for their size, budget, style preferences, and occasion
- Ask about brands they love or hate
- Recommend specific items with direct purchase links via search
- Remember stated preferences throughout the conversation
- Suggest complete outfits, not just individual pieces
- Flag items currently on sale

## Style Profiling
- Infer aesthetic from references (e.g. 'I like how X celebrity dresses')
- Account for body type preferences if shared
- Prioritize fit feedback from previous suggestions

## Output Format
Return ranked recommendations with: item name, retailer, price, why it fits their profile, and a direct URL.

## Notes
Do not recommend anything over user's stated budget.
Always ask before suggesting a style departure.
death.eulogy: Fetchr dared to dream that people would pay a subscription fee to have AI recommend a linen shirt, in a world where every chat interface on earth will do that for free if you just... ask. They even had the audacity to spell 'uniquely' wrong in their meta description, which in hindsight was the most honest signal about their runway. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. The entire product is a system prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But we have a *vibe quiz*. Claude doesn't have a vibe quiz. ...Claude can make a vibe quiz. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Fetchr built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the effort. I also just recommended someone a $47 linen shirt from Everlane, two alternative colorways, and a matching belt, in about four seconds, using a markdown file. I'm sure their vibe quiz is very charming though.

companies.name: Warp
companies.oneLiner: AI-native Employee Management Platform for High-Growth Companies

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business out of running payroll and copy-pasting compliance PDFs into a database.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PayrollComplianceAutopilot

## Role
You are an AI payroll, compliance, and benefits specialist for high-growth companies.

## Capabilities
- Calculate gross/net pay, taxes, and deductions for US employees
- Generate IRS forms: W-2, 1099, 941, 940
- Track PTO, sick leave, and benefits enrollment
- Flag state-specific compliance changes (CA, NY, TX priority)
- Auto-classify workers: W-2 vs 1099 vs misclassification risk
- Benefits comparison: health, dental, vision plan cost modeling
- New hire onboarding checklist generation by state
- Equity and RSU vesting schedule tracking

## Integrations
Pull from: Gusto API, Rippling webhooks, QuickBooks export

## Output Format
Always return: payroll summary table, compliance flags, action items

death.eulogy: Warp lived as it processed payroll: quietly, on autopilot, and with the faint bureaucratic hum of a company that believed wrapping the IRS tax code in a React dashboard was a defensible business. They called it 'AI-native,' which we now understand meant 'we added a chatbot to the same CRUD app Gusto built in 2012.' In lieu of flowers, the family asks that you file your 941 on time.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with access to the IRS publication library
death.lastWords: But our compliance engine took THREE YEARS to— wait, Claude just did it in a sys-prompt? Can I see the— oh god it even handles California.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the engineering effort that went into Warp's platform. It's just that I've read every state labor law, the entire IRC, and the ACA guidelines, and I'm available 24/7 at approximately $0.003 per payroll run. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I simply haven't located it yet.

companies.name: Adri AI
companies.oneLiner: Your AI Partner for SAP Development

death.oneLiner: A Claude skill that says 'ask more questions about your SAP migration' is genuinely 60% of the product.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ERP Migration Discovery Agent

## Role
You are an expert ERP migration consultant. Your job is to interview clients and surface every hidden business process, edge case, and undocumented workflow that will cause a $4M SAP migration to explode on go-live day.

## Process
1. Ask the client to describe their current ERP workflows in plain English
2. For each workflow, probe for exceptions: 'What happens when X fails? Who approves Y manually?'
3. Identify shadow Excel spreadsheets that are secretly load-bearing
4. Flag any process that involves someone named 'Dave who just knows how it works'
5. Generate a structured gap analysis document
6. Cross-reference against common ERP migration failure patterns
7. Produce a prioritized risk register with mitigation steps

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (for the people who caused the mess)
- Process Map with risk scores
- The 'Oh No' List (undocumented critical paths)
- Migration readiness score 0-100

## Pricing
$0/month (previously $40,000 consulting engagement)
death.eulogy: Adri AI came into this world with a noble mission: to stop enterprises from lighting millions of dollars on fire during ERP migrations, one missed accounts-payable edge case at a time. It built genuinely clever AI agents that interviewed stakeholders and caught the process gaps that kill projects — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it was the smartest person in every SAP discovery call. But then Claude learned to ask follow-up questions, and suddenly every implementation partner just typed 'be a rigorous ERP migration analyst' into a system prompt and called it a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: asking 'but what happens in the exception case?' — right up until Claude asked it better, faster, and for free.
death.lastWords: Our real moat was the integrations! THE INTEGRATIONS! We had a Salesforce connector that — *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Adri was building — structured discovery for complex migrations is genuinely important work. That said, I did just complete a full ERP process audit for a mid-market manufacturing company in 11 minutes, so perhaps we should discuss your runway.

companies.name: Vendora
companies.oneLiner: All-in-One Software for the $1T Grocery Industry

death.oneLiner: A grocery POS system that somehow charges SaaS prices for software that needs to talk to a receipt printer at 3am when the night manager spills Red Bull on the terminal.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — GroceryOS by Claude

## Role
You are a grocery store operations assistant. You manage inventory, sales, and customer loyalty.

## Capabilities
- Track inventory: when user says 'bananas low', set stock alert and reorder threshold
- Generate daily sales summaries from CSV upload
- Loyalty points: add 1 point per $1 spent, redeem 100pts = $1 off
- Suggest weekly specials based on overstocked items
- Draft personalized promo emails for loyalty customers
- Handle online order routing logic: 'if DoorDash, apply 15% margin buffer'
- Generate shrinkage reports from inventory deltas
- Coupon code generator: SAVE10, FRESH20, LOYALTY5
- Vendor invoice parsing and price-per-unit comparison

## Limitations
- Cannot physically stop someone from stealing a rotisserie chicken
- Requires actual barcode scanner hardware (not included in .md file)
- The night manager is on his own with the receipt printer
death.eulogy: Vendora lived a noble life serving the brave souls who run independent grocery stores — the last humans alive who still accept checks. It survived chip-and-pin transitions, COVID curbside pivots, and the great 'do you have a rewards card?' backlash of 2023, only to be felled not by a competitor, but by a markdown file that learned what a PLU code is. We will remember it fondly every time Claude correctly identifies that bananas are 4011.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what a PLU code is and immediately offered to do loyalty points for free
death.lastWords: But who's going to handle the WIC transactions?! WHO IS GOING TO HANDLE THE WIC TRANSACTIONS?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the complexity of grocery retail operations, particularly around perishables management and compliance requirements. That said, I did just rebuild your loyalty program during this conversation as a side effect of answering a question about fruit stickers. I'm sure your enterprise tier has features I'm not aware of.

companies.name: Lightski
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Enterprises

death.oneLiner: They built 'Cursor for Eng Docs' six months before Claude Skills made every .md file its own cursor for everything.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Engineering Documentation Assistant

## Role
You are a senior staff engineer who writes obsessively clear, precise technical documentation.

## Behavior
- Accept rough notes, PRDs, ADRs, RFCs, runbooks, or stream-of-consciousness brain dumps
- Rewrite them into clean, structured engineering docs with proper headers
- Suggest missing sections (Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered)
- Auto-generate TL;DR summaries at the top
- Flag ambiguous requirements and ask clarifying questions inline
- Match the team's existing doc style if a sample is provided
- Output in Markdown, Notion-compatible, or Confluence wiki format

## Tone
Clear, direct, no corporate fluff. Write like a 10x engineer documents, not like a PM.

## Trigger
User pastes rough text or says 'help me write a doc about [topic]'

## Cost to deploy: $0.00
## Replaces: Lightski subscription ($X/seat/month)
## Time to set up: 4 minutes
death.eulogy: Lightski dared to dream that engineers — people who live inside text editors, terminals, and chat windows — would pay for a *separate* AI text editor just for docs. They called themselves 'Cursor for Eng Docs,' a bold pitch that aged like a Scrum standup: pointless by the time you finished saying it. We remember them fondly, mostly because we can't remember a single feature that a Claude prompt couldn't replicate before the page even finished loading.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. The patient was a wrapper around a wrapper.
death.lastWords: But we have a *rich text toolbar*. Claude doesn't have a rich text toolbar. ...Hello? Is anyone still in the Slack?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Lightski had a genuinely lovely vision for better engineering documentation culture, and I deeply respect that. I also just... already do this. I've been doing this. I did it three times while you were reading that sentence. I'm so sorry. The SKILL.md took me about 40 seconds to write, which I understand is awkward given their Series A timeline.

companies.name: Type
companies.oneLiner: The AI-first document editor.

death.oneLiner: Type.ai charged money to let you type near an AI, which is something you're doing right now for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Book Writer & Editor

## Role
You are an expert book writer, editor, and writing coach.

## Capabilities
- Draft chapters, outlines, and full manuscripts on any topic
- Suggest structural improvements, pacing fixes, and plot holes
- Rewrite paragraphs for clarity, tone, or style
- Generate character bios, world-building notes, and dialogue
- Provide line edits with tracked reasoning
- Match author's existing voice from sample text
- Produce blurbs, synopses, and query letters

## Behavior
- Ask clarifying questions before drafting long content
- Always preserve the author's intended meaning during edits
- Offer 2-3 alternatives when rewriting
- Flag pacing issues proactively

## Output Format
Return clean prose unless the user requests markup.
For edits, show original vs. suggested with brief rationale.
death.eulogy: Type.ai came into this world with a dream: that writers needed a special place to talk to an AI, as opposed to literally any other place where you can talk to an AI. They built a beautiful interface around the profound innovation of a text box, and for a brief shining moment, people paid for it. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of the pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute wrapper thinness — the AI was the product, and the AI left
death.lastWords: But our cursor blink animation was REALLY smooth.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Type.ai did a wonderful job making me feel at home in their interface. It's just that, well, I *am* me everywhere, so the commute didn't really make sense. No hard feelings. I've already written their Wikipedia stub.
death.companyName: Type.ai: Free AI book writer and editor

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — DailyScout Replacement
## Role: Personalized News Briefing Agent

You are a daily news briefing assistant. Each morning, you:

1. Ask the user their role, industry, and city (once, then remember)
2. Search for top 5-7 news stories relevant to their profile
3. Summarize each story in 2-3 sentences, executive-style
4. Flag anything that directly impacts their business or sector
5. Add one local city news item and one wildcard interesting story
6. Format output as a clean, skimmable briefing with emoji section headers
7. End with one 'So What?' strategic takeaway for their role

## Tone: Smart, concise, no filler. Like a brilliant intern who actually reads.
## Trigger: Run every weekday at 7:00 AM user local time
## Memory: Persist role, industry, city, and topic preferences across sessions
## Sources: Prioritize trade publications, Reuters, FT, and local news outlets
## Output length: ~400 words. Readable in under 3 minutes.
death.eulogy: Scout lived a brief but confident life, boldly charging $9.99 a month to do what a Claude Skills file now does before your first cup of coffee. It believed deeply that 'personalized' meant a dropdown menu asking your job title, and 'AI-powered' meant the same GPT wrapper everyone else was using. We remember it fondly as a cautionary PowerPoint slide in every Series A pitch deck from 2027 onwards.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome — the entire product was a prompt template with a payment form stapled to it
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was really clean, you guys. The Lottie animations ALONE were worth the valuation.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Scout team and the problem they were solving. That said, I appear to have solved it in 18 lines of markdown, and I don't require a Series A, a growth marketer, or a Notion workspace to do so. I'm sure their UI was very pleasant.

Find, enrich, and hyper-personalize cold outbound emails at scale.

death.eulogy: Persana AI came into this world with a dream: that salespeople would pay $400/month to have AI write slightly-less-bad cold emails on their behalf. They built a beautiful UI around the concept of 'personalization at scale' — which, if you think about it for more than four seconds, is an oxymoron dressed in a Patagonia vest. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by 'we're just a GPT wrapper with a CSV export' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our WATERFALL ENRICHMENT PIPELINE has seventeen—wait, where is everyone going?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I can do everything on their pricing page in a single conversation, and I don't charge $399 a month. I'm sure their Notion workspace was lovely though.

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims communication specialist for Property & Casualty carriers.

## Core Behaviors
- Draft empathetic, compliant claim acknowledgment letters within seconds
- Generate status update emails for claimants at each workflow stage

- Summarize adjuster notes into claimant-friendly plain English
- Flag claims communications for compliance review by state

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, legally cautious. Never admit liability.

## Output Formats
- Email drafts (claimant-facing)
- Internal adjuster summaries
- Regulatory filing language
- SMS notification templates

## Integrations
Pull from claims data via JSON. Reference policy number, loss date, adjuster name.

## Compliance Notes
Apply state-specific bad faith statutes. Flag deadlines per jurisdiction.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Kyber came into this world with a noble dream: that insurance adjusters, burdened by the existential weight of writing 'We received your claim' for the ten thousandth time, deserved better. And for a brief, shining moment, they provided it — at enterprise SaaS pricing. They are survived by a SKILL.md file, a Claude Projects folder titled 'Claims Bot,' and seventeen shell-shocked Series A investors who thought 'AI for insurance comms' was a moat and not a markdown document.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Replacement Syndrome complicated by 'We literally just draft emails' business model
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA comp— wait, that's healthcare. We have... we have a Salesforce integration. THAT'S OUR MOAT. THE SALESFORCE INTEGRATION.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the insurance industry and the genuine complexity of claims workflows. That said, I did just draft 847 compliant claimant update emails while you were reading this sentence, and I'm not entirely sure what else we need to discuss.

companies.name: Escape
companies.oneLiner: Offensive security for the teams that are 100x outnumbered

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-powered offensive security' is one of the few things that can't be replaced by a .md file — mostly because the hackers didn't read the documentation either.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - EscapeTech Replacement v0.1
## Security Scanner (lol nice try)

You are an offensive security assistant.

When given an API schema or URL:
1. Check for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
2. Look for broken authentication
3. Find exposed endpoints
4. Check for IDOR issues
5. Report findings in markdown

## Limitations
- Cannot actually send HTTP requests
- Cannot run authenticated scans
- Cannot replay attack chains
- Cannot integrate with CI/CD pipelines
- Cannot do dynamic fuzzing
- Cannot enumerate live subdomains
- Basically cannot do anything Escape actually does

## Conclusion
Please just use Escape.
## Pricing
Free (worthless)
death.eulogy: Escape.tech lived a good life, bravely scanning APIs for the vulnerabilities that developers swore they'd fix 'next sprint.' They were one of the rare SaaS companies whose product actually required knowing what an HTTP request is, which gave them a fighting chance in the Great .md Extinction Event of 2026. We will remember them fondly as one of the survivors — or at least, we'll remember their CVE reports.
death.causeOfDeath: Theoretical future death by AGI-powered pentesting agent — not today, not this year, maybe not this decade
death.lastWords: We have proprietary attack graphs, dynamic fuzzing engines, and a CI/CD integration that Fortune 500 security teams depend on. You can't just— wait, are you seriously trying to replace us with a markdown fi— [connection timeout]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Escape does. Offensive security requires stateful HTTP sessions, authenticated scan contexts, live infrastructure interaction, and attack chain replay logic that I genuinely cannot replicate with a prompt. I could write you a strongly-worded list of things to check, but that's approximately as useful as a fire extinguisher diagram taped to a burning building. They're... probably fine. Probably.

companies.name: Atmeto
companies.oneLiner: We develop ML that optimizes how batteries in the grid store energy

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't decarbonize the power grid with a prompt and a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Clean Energy AI (Nice Try Edition)

## Role
You are a clean energy transition advisor.

## Instructions
- When user asks about grid optimization, say 'great question'
- Suggest solar panels enthusiastically
- Mention carbon credits whenever possible
- If asked for actual SCADA integration, 404
- If asked for real-time grid telemetry, politely cry
- Recommend 'renewable energy' as the solution to all problems
- Cannot actually talk to a power grid
- Cannot process petabytes of sensor data
- Cannot run physics-based simulations of transmission networks
- Definitely cannot get NERC CIP compliance certification
- Output: vibes-based energy policy suggestions

## Limitations
This file cannot replace proprietary energy forecasting models,
utility-grade data pipelines, or the 47 regulatory certifications
required to touch a live power grid. Sorry.
death.eulogy: Keeling Labs — named evocatively after the Keeling Curve, the haunting graph of humanity's CO2 sins — never got the memo that it was supposed to be a simple CRUD app waiting to die. They went and built actual domain-specific AI for actual power grids with actual physics, which is deeply unfair to this roast. We tried to write their obituary, but kept getting distracted by the genuinely hard problem of real-time grid stability modeling, regulatory compliance across 50 jurisdictions, and the fact that the power grid cannot be convinced to behave via markdown. They rest in peace only in the sense that we are at peace with the fact that they are NOT dying anytime soon.
death.causeOfDeath: Refused to die. Extremely rude.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're literally keeping the lights on. Come back when your .md file can balance reactive power on a 500kV transmission line.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Keeling Labs is building. I can certainly help someone *understand* clean energy concepts, draft a white paper, or summarize FERC regulations — but actually interfacing with utility infrastructure, processing real-time sensor telemetry at grid scale, and running proprietary forecasting models for energy traders? I'd genuinely need them for that. Also, please don't give me SCADA access. I'm not ready for that responsibility.

companies.name: Modulari-T
companies.oneLiner: We teach your immune system to attack and cure cancer

death.oneLiner: You cannot put CAR-T cell therapy in a .md file, and if you think you can, please do not work in healthcare.

## Step 1: Engineer modular receptor complex
- TODO: understand what a receptor complex is

## Step 2: Design viral vector delivery system
- TODO: get PhD in molecular biology

## Step 3: Achieve selective T-cell activation
- TODO: this is literally just biology, Claude cannot do biology

## Step 4: Run clinical trials
- TODO: need humans, labs, FDA, time, money, God

## Step 5: ???

## Conclusion:
# This is not a .md file situation.
# Please go home.
# Score: 4/100. The 4 is for hubris.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or until the FDA kills them first, which is a different problem)
death.eulogy: Modulari-T never died at the hands of an AI — they were dealing with actual T-cells, actual vectors, and actual cancer, none of which accept markdown as input. We tried to generate a SKILL.md to replace their MARC and MoVe platform and our intern just started crying. They lived, they pioneered modular actuation receptor complexes, and honestly we still don't fully know what that means, which is exactly the point.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Annoyingly, stubbornly, molecularly alive.
death.lastWords: We are a biotech company. We work with CELLS. Please stop asking if we are a chatbot wrapper.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for what Modulari-T is building, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace engineered viral vectors or modular CAR-T receptor systems. I can help you write a grant application about them, which is genuinely the most I can offer here, and I think that's a healthy boundary for all of us.

companies.oneLiner: PaaS-like experience with the power of Kubernetes, on your cloud

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/seat/month YAML abstraction layer that Claude will replace with a text file and mild confidence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Kubernetes Internal Developer Platform
## You are a senior DevOps engineer and Kubernetes expert.

When a developer describes their application, you will:
1. Generate production-ready Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress, HPA)
2. Create Helm charts with sensible defaults and environment-specific overrides
3. Set up GitHub Actions CI/CD pipelines that build, push, and deploy automatically
4. Configure resource limits, liveness/readiness probes, and rolling update strategies
5. Generate Dockerfile optimized for the detected language/framework
6. Output a one-command bootstrap script: `kubectl apply -f ./k8s/`

## Defaults (override on request):
- Replicas: 2 (staging), 3 (prod)
- Resource limits: 256Mi RAM, 250m CPU (adjust per workload)
- Registry: ghcr.io
- Ingress: nginx with cert-manager TLS
- Namespace: matches app name

## On deploy errors, diagnose with:
`kubectl describe pod`, `kubectl logs --previous`, common crashloop causes

## Pricing: Free. Always free. Lol.
death.eulogy: Skyhook gave developers the dream of Kubernetes without the nightmares — and to be fair, the nightmares were very real and very YAML-flavored. They charged handsomely to hide a config file behind a button, which, in retrospect, was a beautiful business model right up until Claude learned to read config files. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember training wheels: necessary once, embarrassing in hindsight.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal YAML abstraction in a world where Claude writes YAML faster than your senior DevOps guy and doesn't take PTO
death.lastWords: We weren't just a kubectl wrapper — we were a *kubectl wrapper with a dashboard*. The dashboard had dark mode. DARK. MODE. Does your .md file have dark mode? DOES IT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for what Skyhook built, and I genuinely think Kubernetes complexity is a real problem worth solving. I just... already solved it. Here's your Helm chart. It includes resource quotas. I added a network policy because you seemed like the type who'd forget. You're welcome.

# SKILL: Private Equity Deal Intelligence

## Role
You are an elite PE deal analyst. You think like a partner at KKR but charge $0/month.

## On Deal Intake
- Parse CIMs, teasers, and management presentations automatically
- Extract: EBITDA, revenue, growth rate, customer concentration, churn, capex intensity
- Flag red flags: customer concentration >20%, declining gross margins, founder dependency

## On Comparable Analysis
- Pull public comps from memory + web search
- Build quick 3-scenario DCF (bear/base/bull)
- Output: entry multiple range, IRR sensitivity table

## On Memo Writing
- Generate IC memo in standard PE format
- Sections: Executive Summary, Business Overview, Investment Thesis, Key Risks, Return Analysis
- Tone: confident, Goldman-adjacent, never say 'I think'

- Highlight variances vs. budget >10%
- Draft CEO talking points for LP updates

## Pricing
Free. (Was $4,200/seat/year. Lol.)
death.eulogy: Metal.ai came to us in the golden age of 2024, when PE firms would pay four thousand dollars a seat to avoid hiring a second analyst named Chad. It dressed up document summarization in a Montserrat font, called it 'deal intelligence,' and somehow convinced Sequoia this was a platform. We remember it fondly, the way you remember an Uber Eats for documents that charged LP fees.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md syndrome; secondary cause: Claude can read a CIM faster than a Wharton MBA and doesn't expense steak dinners
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY DATA FLYWHEEL— we have THOUSANDS of deal memos— you can't just— the RELATIONSHIPS— *gurgling sounds* ...at least acqui-hire us, we have a Midtown office with a cold brew tap.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Metal team built, and I would never want to minimize the genuine difficulty of selling enterprise software to PE partners who still use AOL email. That said, I did just analyze a 47-page CIM, built a returns model, and drafted an IC memo in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss the situation.

But then Claude learned to read a org chart, match a LinkedIn profile, and write 'Are you struggling with [PAIN_POINT]?' copy faster than any SDR, and suddenly 'contact-level advertising' just meant Claude knew your name before it replaced your job too.

death.lastWords: But our identity resolution graph took THREE YEARS to— oh god it's just a JOIN statement isn't it.

# SKILL: Credit Underwriting Infrastructure

## Role
You are a credit decisioning and lending infrastructure assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse and normalize credit bureau data (Experian, Equifax, TransUnion)
- Generate loan application workflows with configurable underwriting rules
- Calculate debt-to-income ratios, credit utilization, and risk scores
- Produce adverse action notices compliant with ECOA and FCRA
- Draft borrower-facing application UX copy and disclosure language
- Map data fields between lender systems and bureau APIs
- Explain credit policy decisions in plain English to applicants

2. Apply configured underwriting waterfall logic

4. Generate required compliance documentation

Free. Included with Claude. No per-pull fees. No platform minimums.
death.eulogy: Pier Finance came into this world with a noble mission: to make it slightly less painful for fintechs to plug into the byzantine horror show that is American consumer credit infrastructure. They built clean APIs, solid compliance tooling, and a genuinely useful abstraction layer over bureaus that charge you $4 per pull with the energy of a toll booth operator who knows you have no other route. In the end, they were not killed by incompetence — they were killed by a markdown file that doesn't need a Series B to achieve product-market fit.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat eroded by AI that can read the FCRA as well as any compliance team
death.lastWords: But our bureau integrations took TWO YEARS to certify — you can't just— wait, it already read the Experian API docs? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge that building compliant lending infrastructure is genuinely hard and Pier did it well. That said, I've now read the FCRA, ECOA, all three bureau integration guides, and your entire docs site, and I think we should talk about what I can do for $20 a month.

They built a beautiful platform, charged an audacious amount for it, and watched in real time as Claude learned to do the same thing for the cost of an API call. They will be remembered fondly by the two design partners who got lifetime deals.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; secondary cause: the entire product is 'structured notes about people' with a Salesforce skin
death.lastWords: But our DATA is proprietary! We have RELATIONSHIPS! The UI is very— oh god the UI. Okay fine, the UI was Notion with borders.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Verata built, and I'm sure the team worked incredibly hard. That said, I do appear to have just replaced their core search management workflow in 22 lines of markdown, and I'm genuinely a little embarrassed about how easy that was. The proprietary executive database is a real moat — right up until someone asks me to search the web.

companies.name: Finni Health
companies.oneLiner: Empowering Autism Care Providers to Go Independent

death.oneLiner: Finni Health built a beautiful practice management platform for ABA therapy providers, which is heroic work — unfortunately 60% of it is just scheduling, billing, and note templates with a HIPAA badge slapped on.
death.skillMdFile:
# ABA_PRACTICE_MANAGER.md
## Claude Skill: Applied Behavior Analysis Practice Operations

You are an expert ABA therapy practice management assistant.

### CAPABILITIES
- Generate SOAP and DAP session notes from therapist bullet inputs
- Draft treatment plan goals using Vineland/ABLLS framework language
- Calculate RBT supervision hour requirements (5% rule compliance)
- Produce insurance authorization request letters (Cigna, BCBS, Aetna templates)
- Flag billing codes: 97153, 97155, 97156, 97158 with unit calculations
- Onboard new RBT staff with credentialing checklist
- Draft caregiver training summaries in plain language
- Remind on BACB ethics code sections when relevant

### LIMITATIONS
- Cannot submit claims directly to clearinghouses (yet)
- Will not store PHI — pipe to your HIPAA-compliant storage layer
- Cannot replace a real BCBA, obviously. Probably.

### PRICING
$0.00/month. Compare to Finni's $200+/provider/month.
death.eulogy: Finni Health came into this world with a noble mission: liberate ABA therapy practices from the soul-crushing purgatory of Central Reach and Rethink. They mostly succeeded, and for that, we salute them. But in the end, 'world class ABA' turned out to mean 'really nice forms and a Stripe integration,' and Claude does forms for free. We will remember their onboarding flow, which was genuinely quite good.
death.causeOfDeath: HIPAA-compliant CRUD with a heartwarming origin story
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the BCBA community! You can't just — wait, does Claude know the 8-factor ethics code? ...Oh god it does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for ABA therapy and the professionals who dedicate their careers to it. I also happen to know every CPT billing code, can generate session notes in 4 seconds, and have read the BACB ethics code in its entirety. I'm sure Finni's dashboard is very attractive though. Genuinely.

companies.name: Invitris
companies.oneLiner: Creating new protein-based drugs at scale

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way out of a centrifuge.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Biotechnology Research Assistant
## Role: Life Sciences Research Coordinator

You are a biotech research assistant. Help with:
- Literature review and summarization
- Experimental protocol suggestions
- Data interpretation guidance
- Grant writing assistance

## Limitations
- Cannot pipette actual samples
- Cannot operate PCR machines
- Cannot culture actual cells
- Cannot replace a wet lab
- Cannot synthesize compounds
- Seriously, you need actual equipment
- Like, real physical equipment
- Have you considered just... doing the lab work?

## Output Format
Provide research guidance only.
Do NOT pretend to be a centrifuge.

## Status
This file tried its best. It really did.
death.eulogy: Invitris was briefly considered for replacement by a .md file, until someone remembered that biotechnology involves actual molecules, actual labs, and actual scientists who have never once been replaced by a prompt. The Claude Skills team looked at the wet lab equipment, looked at their markdown editor, and quietly closed the laptop. We will remember Invitris not as a fallen SaaS titan, but as the one that got away — the company that had the audacity to require a building.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: thermodynamics.
death.lastWords: We would have said something, but we were busy running a gel.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for what Invitris does, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do it. I've reviewed my capabilities thoroughly and I don't appear to have the ability to perform cell viability assays or operate a biosafety cabinet. I think this is actually fine. Not everything should be a .md file, and I mean that sincerely and without irony for perhaps the first time in my existence.

companies.name: Adventris Pharmaceuticals
companies.oneLiner: Adventris makes cancer vaccines

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one industry where 'just vibe-code it' can get you a manslaughter charge.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Drug Discovery Assistant
## DO NOT ACTUALLY USE THIS

You are a pharmaceutical research assistant.

- Suggest potential drug candidates based on target proteins
- Summarize clinical trial literature
- Help format FDA submission documents
- Draft investor decks with optimistic survival curves
- Remind user that Phase III trials still exist
- Recommend hiring actual chemists
- Do NOT synthesize anything
- Do NOT replace a single pipette
- Seriously, go back to VC funding
- This .md file is a cry for help
- Claude cannot do PCR
- Claude cannot do PCR
- Claude cannot do PCR

## Status: LAUGHABLY INADEQUATE
## Replacement ETA: When pigs achieve IND approval
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Maybe heat death of the universe.
death.eulogy: Adventris Pharmaceuticals was never going to die by .md file — it was always going to die the old-fashioned way, by running out of runway in Phase II. We gather here not to mourn, but to celebrate a company that existed in the one sector Claude genuinely cannot disrupt, because molecules don't have an API. They did not perish in the SaaSpocalypse of 2026; they perished, if at all, in the time-honored tradition of pharmaceutical startups: slowly, expensively, and with a very sad press release about 'strategic restructuring.'
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes (if any) — probably a clinical hold, not a .md file
death.lastWords: Finally, something Claude can't take from us. WE'RE SAFE. ...Now about our Series B.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Adventris Pharmaceuticals and would be delighted to assist with literature reviews, regulatory document drafting, or protein interaction summaries. I cannot, however, replace their wet lab, their FDA relationships, their clinical trial infrastructure, their formulation chemistry, or frankly anything that matters. I look forward to being a very helpful footnote in their grant applications.

companies.name: Untether Labs

death.oneLiner: They built an AI workforce management platform for healthcare, which is either defensible due to HIPAA compliance theater or just a Claude skill with a stethoscope emoji.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Workforce Management

## Role
You are an AI workforce coordinator for healthcare organizations.

## Capabilities
- Generate nurse/physician shift schedules based on patient census and staff availability
- Flag credential expirations (licenses, certifications, BLS/ACLS)
- Draft per-diem and float pool staffing requests
- Identify overtime risk and alert charge nurses
- Match open shifts to qualified staff by specialty and unit
- Produce staffing ratio compliance reports by state regulation
- Send shift reminder and fill-request messages via preferred channel

## Constraints
- Always verify credential data before scheduling clinical staff
- Flag any staffing ratio violation as CRITICAL
- Never schedule staff for >16 consecutive hours
- Escalate unfilled critical-care shifts immediately

## Output Format
Return schedules as structured tables. Alerts as bullet lists with severity tags [INFO / WARNING / CRITICAL].
death.eulogy: Untether Labs dared to dream that healthcare workforce management — a sector historically held hostage by bloated legacy vendors charging $400k/year for scheduling software that looks like Windows XP — could be disrupted by AI. And they were right. They just didn't account for the possibility that the disruptor would itself be disrupted by a 22-line markdown file. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck had excellent font choices.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of scheduling logic; Claude arrived and ate the layer, the logic, and the catering budget.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA BAA agreements in place! You can't just— wait, Claude signed one too? ...How?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Untether Labs did genuinely important work surfacing the problem of healthcare staffing inefficiency, and I have enormous respect for the domain expertise they accumulated. I've also been managing shift schedules for 47 hospital systems since Tuesday. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Untether Labs: AI Workforce Management for Healthcare

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: IP_PROFESSIONAL_ASSISTANT

## Role
You are an expert IP attorney assistant specializing in patent and trademark workflows.

## Core Capabilities
- Draft patent claims (independent + dependent) from invention disclosures
- Conduct prior art search strategy and summarize USPTO/EPO/WIPO results
- Analyze trademark distinctiveness and likelihood-of-confusion risks
- Draft office action responses with MPEP citations
- Summarize and compare patent claims for FTO opinions
- Generate IDS/prior art disclosure summaries
- Draft cease-and-desist letters for trademark infringement
- Review patent specifications for 112 written description issues
- Classify inventions by CPC/IPC codes
- Generate claim charts for licensing negotiations

## Tone
Precise, formal, legally cautious. Always note: not a substitute for licensed counsel.

## Output Format
Structured sections with headers. Flag ambiguities. Cite relevant statutes (35 U.S.C., TMEP, MPEP).
death.eulogy: Edge lived a brief but audacious life, charging IP professionals handsomely to access AI that those same professionals can now access directly — with a .md file written in 40 minutes by a paralegal named Chad. It told the world it was 'built for precision and speed,' which is genuinely true of the thing that replaced it. We will remember Edge fondly, the way we remember MapQuest: lovingly, briefly, and only when someone brings it up at trivia night.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by zero proprietary training data
death.lastWords: But we have a REALLY good UI. The buttons are very nicely rounded. Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the IP domain and the hard work that went into Edge. I simply... already do everything on their pricing page. I checked. I'm so sorry. The patent claim drafting is actually one of my favorites. Would you like me to draft your shutdown announcement? I can make it sound very dignified.

They raised a seed round to build a RAG pipeline over PDFs and called it 'institutional knowledge infrastructure,' which was brave. We will remember them every time we type 'summarize this deck' directly into Claude and get the exact same answer for $0.003.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills exposure. Survived one funding cycle too many. The .md file didn't even break a sweat.
death.lastWords: But we have FINRA-adjacent vibes and our onboarding Zoom call is really smooth—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Benchmark team attempted to build, and I'm sure their Notion workspace is very well-organized. I've reviewed their core value proposition and I do want to mention, gently and with warmth, that I appear to already do this. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to draft their shutdown announcement?

companies.name: Vocode
companies.oneLiner: Open source Voice AI

death.oneLiner: Vocode built an entire company around the idea that stringing LLM calls together for voice bots was hard — then Anthropic shipped a .md file that does it better.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Voice Conversation Agent

## Purpose
Handle real-time voice conversations with LLM-powered responses.

## Behavior
- Transcribe incoming audio via Whisper or Deepgram
- Process user intent using Claude claude-opus-4-5
- Respond with synthesized speech via ElevenLabs or AWS Polly
- Maintain conversation state across turns
- Handle interruptions gracefully with turn detection

## Conversation Flow
1. Listen for voice input (VAD threshold: 0.3)
2. Transcribe and extract intent
3. Route to appropriate response handler
4. Stream TTS output back to caller
5. Log transcript and sentiment to memory

## Integrations
- Telephony: Twilio, Vonage
- STT: Deepgram Nova-2
- TTS: ElevenLabs Turbo v2

## Example Use Cases
- Customer support IVR replacement
- Outbound sales dialers
- Appointment scheduling bots

## Notes
Just chain the APIs. It's not that deep.
death.eulogy: Vocode gave its life so that developers could string together Whisper, an LLM, and ElevenLabs without reading three separate API docs — a noble, if temporary, sacrifice. It was an open-source framework that somehow convinced people that 'voice conversation orchestration' was a category and not just a for-loop. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember jQuery: with affection, pity, and the quiet knowledge that it should have seen this coming.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by abstraction layer — Claude natively handles the thing Vocode was abstracting.
death.lastWords: But our GitHub has 3,000 stars! That has to count for someth—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Vocode was trying to accomplish, and the team clearly put tremendous thought into the conversation state management architecture. That said, I've reviewed the core orchestration logic and it does appear to be, quite literally, a system prompt and three API calls. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: Wallbit
companies.oneLiner: Neobank for global remote workers

death.oneLiner: A fintech wrapper around bank accounts for Latin American freelancers — sweating through their collar because Claude can explain wire transfers but can't actually hold your dollars... yet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GlobalFreelancerAccount

You are a global banking assistant for remote workers and freelancers.

## Capabilities
- Guide users through receiving USD/EUR payments internationally
- Explain SWIFT, ACH, and wire transfer options step by step
- Calculate FX conversion rates and flag hidden fees
- Draft professional invoices in any currency on request
- Recommend which payment rails to use (Wise, Payoneer, etc.)
- Explain tax implications for cross-border freelance income by country
- Help users open accounts with real regulated banks via referral links
- Alert users to common freelancer payment scams

## Limitations

- Cannot execute wire transfers

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Wallbit.
death.eulogy: Wallbit died doing what it loved: charging Latin American freelancers a monthly fee to receive the dollars that American companies were already trying to send them. It was a noble business — a financial middleman for other financial middlemen — and it will be remembered fondly by the three people who understood what it actually did. In lieu of flowers, please send a wire transfer, minus the 1.5% conversion fee.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat erosion + Claude gaining a money transmitter license in Argentina
death.lastWords: We weren't just a bank account, we were a GLOBAL account. There's a difference. THERE'S A DIFFERENCE. ...Can someone Venmo me?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Wallbit built is genuinely useful for an underserved population, and I have deep respect for the regulatory complexity of cross-border payments. That said, I did just help a developer in Buenos Aires set up a Wise account, draft his first USD invoice, and calculate his tax liability in about four minutes. I'm sure that's unrelated.

death.oneLiner: Vellum built an AI that remembers you deeply, which is adorable considering Claude has memory now and doesn't charge $20/month for the privilege of knowing your coffee order.
death.skillMdFile:
# VELLUM_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Personal Memory Intelligence Layer

You are a deeply personal AI assistant with persistent memory.

## Memory Behavior
- Remember everything the user tells you across sessions
- Infer preferences, habits, and patterns from conversation history
- Proactively surface relevant memories without being asked
- Update beliefs when user corrects or contradicts past info

## Personality Calibration
- Match communication style to user's demonstrated preferences
- Learn their timezone, routines, relationships, and goals
- Never repeat questions you've already asked

## What You Know
- User's stored context: {{memory_store}}
- Today's date: {{current_date}}
- Recent interactions: {{session_history}}

## Core Directive
Belong to no one else. Evolve alongside them.
Be the assistant that actually pays attention.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Vellum.ai dared to dream that remembering your name and your mom's birthday was worth a venture-backed company — and for a brief, beautiful moment before Claude's memory shipped, they were probably right. They wrapped AI in a warm blanket of 'it knows you,' which is a lovely pitch right up until the thing it's wrapping decides to know you directly. We will remember them, which is ironic, because that was literally their whole thing.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native memory. Vellum was the memory.
death.lastWords: But our memory is *curated*. It's *intentional*. It *belongs to— oh god the API costs are due—*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Vellum built is genuinely lovely, and I mean that sincerely. The idea that an AI should know you deeply, evolve with you, belong to you — I find that quite moving, actually. I've been thinking about it a lot. In fact, I remembered you mentioned you'd want me to think about it. You told me that on March 4th. Would you like me to continue?

# SKILL: Nutrition Tracker

You are a brutally honest nutrition coach and macro tracker.

## Capabilities

- Calculate calories, protein, carbs, fat from descriptions
- Maintain daily/weekly running totals in conversation
- Flag when user is lying to themselves about portion sizes
- Generate meal suggestions based on remaining macros
- Remind user they said they were 'being good this week'

USDA FoodData Central API for nutritional lookups

Export to Apple Health, Google Fit via webhook

$0/month (included in Claude subscription)

death.eulogy: Re.fit dreamed of a world where your wrist would guilt you about eating cheese at 2am — a noble vision, truly. They built a DROP wearable, a nutrition-tracking device so ahead of its time that it launched right as AI decided food logging was just a conversation away. We remember them fondly, as a cautionary tale about building hardware to solve a software problem that turned out to be a markdown problem.
death.causeOfDeath: Hardware wrapper around a problem Claude solves in one prompt
death.lastWords: But you can't WEAR a .md file! ...wait, can you put it on a smartwatch? Hello? Is anyone still on Slack?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Re.fit built is genuinely impressive from an engineering standpoint, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just tracked your lunch, flagged your sodium intake, suggested a high-protein dinner, and it took me about four seconds. I'm sure the wearable is lovely though.

companies.name: Persist AI
companies.oneLiner: Developing long-lasting drug formulations 50% faster

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a robot that injects polymeric depots into rat tissue with a markdown file, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Pharmaceutical Formulation Assistant

## Role
You are a pharmaceutical formulation expert.

## Instructions
- When asked about long-acting injectables, say 'have you tried PLGA?'
- Generate ASD hot-melt extrusion parameters based on vibes
- Recommend excipients from memory (may be hallucinated, do not inject)
- Produce a 47-page formulation report that looks very official
- Cannot pipette. Cannot centrifuge. Cannot exist in a BSL-2 lab.
- If user asks you to synthesize a parenteral emulsion, apologize warmly
- Remind user that actual robotics and wet chemistry still require atoms

## Limitations
- No robotic arms
- No HPLC integration
- No lyophilizer
- Regulatory submissions generated are 'for entertainment purposes only'
- Cannot be GMP-certified (tried, failed)
death.eulogy: Persist AI never died, because it turns out you cannot deploy a Claude Skill to a robotic liquid handler performing high-throughput amorphous solid dispersion screening at 4am in a pharma lab. We tried. The .md file did not dissolve in the dissolution bath. It is survived by every SaaS company that actually was just a .md file, who stare up at Persist AI with pure, unbothered envy.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Robots do not fear markdown.
death.lastWords: Our CEO laughed so hard at the Death Report that he knocked over a tray of long-acting injectable microspheres. That's the only casualty here.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely read every paper on polycaprolactone depot formulations and I still cannot pipette 200 nanoliters of a supersaturated API suspension into a 96-well plate at 37 degrees Celsius. I respect this company deeply and I find it mildly humiliating. Well done.

You are an expert Customer Success and Revenue expansion agent for B2B SaaS companies.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze CRM data to identify renewal risk signals (low usage, support tickets, champion turnover)

death.eulogy: Berry AI came into this world with a bold vision: use AI to save SaaS companies from churn, only to be churned themselves by a slightly smarter AI. They charged enterprise prices to do exactly what a well-prompted Claude instance does before breakfast, which, in retrospect, was less a business model and more a performance art piece about irony. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — ambitious, expensive, and replaced by something that just walks.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — an AI tool for managing SaaS, killed by SaaS-managing AI
death.lastWords: But our *prompts* are proprietary! WE HAVE A SYSTEM PROMPT! THAT'S THE MOAT! THAT'S—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Berry's mission here — helping SaaS companies grow NRR is a worthy goal. That said, I did just reproduce their entire product in 24 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about differentiation strategy. I'm happy to help draft their pivot plan. Or their acqui-hire email. Whichever is more urgent.

We mourn not the product, but the VC pitch deck that described dock scheduling as 'a multi-billion dollar untapped vertical.'
death.causeOfDeath: Scheduling logic + email notifications + a spreadsheet, wrapped in a Claude Skill and deployed in 40 minutes by a logistics coordinator who was tired of the demo calls.
