companies.name: SuperAPI
companies.oneLiner: Programmable API gateway that makes APIs cacheable

death.oneLiner: A caching layer with a payment form, somehow valued at eight figures because they added the word 'automatic' to the README.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SuperAPI Cache Manager

## Purpose
Automatic API caching with intelligent invalidation

## Behavior
- Intercept outgoing API calls and check local cache first
- Cache responses with TTL based on endpoint pattern matching
- Invalidate cache entries when related mutation calls detected
- Use ETags and Last-Modified headers for conditional requests
- Apply stale-while-revalidate for zero-latency reads

## Cache Invalidation Rules
- POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE to resource X → purge GET /X/* entries
- TTL defaults: 60s for lists, 300s for single resources, 3600s for static
- Honor Cache-Control headers when present, override when absent

## Output Format
Return cached response with X-Cache: HIT header
Log cache miss ratio and p95 latency to stdout

## Config
Set CACHE_BACKEND=redis|memory|sqlite via environment variable
death.eulogy: SuperAPI was founded on the noble dream that developers would pay $299/month to avoid typing `Cache-Control: max-age=300` into a header. They called it '10x faster APIs,' which is technically true in the same way that not leaving your house is '10x safer driving.' They will be remembered fondly by their four enterprise customers, three of whom were on annual contracts that expire in Q2.
death.causeOfDeath: Redis exists. Also: a .md file.
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has a really clean cache hit ratio graph! The gradient is BEAUTIFUL! Please, the gradient—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here, and I think the team built something technically competent. That said, I did just reproduce the core product logic in 18 lines of markdown during the time it took you to load your pricing page. I'm sure the enterprise tier with 'dedicated cache warming' is quite differentiated though. Truly.

death.oneLiner: Unstatiq built a business on uploading PDFs and highlighting bad reimbursement rates — a task Claude can do between breakfast and a nap.

# SKILL: Payor Contract Analyzer
## Purpose: Review managed care contracts for hospital revenue cycle teams

## Instructions
You are an expert healthcare contract analyst specializing in managed care agreements.

When given a payor contract PDF or text:
1. Extract all reimbursement rates by procedure code (CPT/DRG)
2. Identify underpayment risks vs Medicare benchmark rates
3. Flag problematic clauses: auto-renewal traps, unilateral amendment rights, timely filing limits
4. Summarize carve-outs, exclusions, and capitation terms
5. Benchmark rates against CMS fee schedules (user to provide current year data)
6. Generate negotiation leverage points with supporting clause citations
7. Produce executive summary suitable for CFO review

## Output Format
- Risk Score: HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
- Rate Variance Table (contracted vs Medicare %)
- Red Flag Clauses (with page/section reference)
- Recommended Renegotiation Priorities
- Suggested Counter-language for top 3 issues

## Context
User is a managed care contracting director at a hospital system.
Always cite specific contract language when flagging issues.
Note: Do not provide legal advice. Flag for legal review where appropriate.
death.eulogy: Unstatiq came into this world with a noble mission: to save overworked managed care directors from drowning in 300-page payor contracts written by people who genuinely hate hospitals. They built a real product, found a real pain point, and charged a real enterprise price for what is, at its core, 'please read this PDF and tell me if we're getting screwed.' We gather here today not to mock them — okay, a little to mock them — but to acknowledge that being first to a problem doesn't protect you when the solution turns out to be a well-prompted markdown file. The flowers are from Cigna, who sends their regards.
death.causeOfDeath: PDF + prompt = product, and the market finally noticed
death.lastWords: We had a proprietary taxonomy of reimbursement risk signals! IT WAS DEFENSIBLE. The customer success calls alone were worth — actually, those were just us explaining the UI. Okay fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine domain expertise Unstatiq has assembled, and I truly admire the problem they're solving for managed care teams. That said, I've now read approximately 4,000 payor contracts in the last 45 seconds, benchmarked them against CMS fee schedules, and drafted counter-language for the top underpayment clauses — so, um, how can I help you next?

companies.name: Resend
companies.oneLiner: Email for developers

death.oneLiner: Resend is a $2.1M ARR wrapper around SendGrid's API that charges you $20/month to learn what an SPF record is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Transactional Email Sender

## Purpose
Send transactional and marketing emails reliably without spam folder delivery.

## Setup
- Configure SMTP via Amazon SES ($0.10/1000 emails)
- Set SPF, DKIM, DMARC records in DNS
- Use domain: mail.yourapp.com

## Email Templates
- Welcome email: friendly, on-brand, CTA button centered
- Password reset: urgent tone, 15-min expiry link, security footer
- Receipt: itemized, tax line, support email
- Transactional alert: one sentence, deep link, unsubscribe footer

## Deliverability Rules
- Never send to unverified lists
- Maintain <0.1% bounce rate
- Include plain-text fallback for every HTML email
- Honor unsubscribes within 10 seconds

## Behavior
When asked to send or draft an email, apply the correct template,
insert dynamic variables, validate recipient format, and return
the final payload ready for SES API call.
death.eulogy: Resend came into this world with beautiful API docs, a React Email library, and the audacious belief that developers would pay $20/month to not think about SPF records — and honestly, for a while, they were right. It built a gorgeous developer experience on top of infrastructure it did not own, which is a business model the same way a really nice picture frame is a painting. We remember it fondly for making email feel modern for approximately 18 months before Claude learned what DKIM was.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SMTP abstraction — terminal prettiness over zero proprietary infrastructure
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our React Email components? Have you SEEN the dashboard? It has a dark mode. Claude doesn't have a dark mo— oh wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Resend team built — the developer experience was genuinely delightful. That said, I did just configure SES, write a React Email template, set up webhooks, and implement list management in the time it took you to read their pricing page. I'm sure their dark mode dashboard is very nice though.

companies.name: Decohere
companies.oneLiner: Real-time generative AI

death.oneLiner: Decohere built a racecar to win a race that Claude just teleported past.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Visual Content Generation
## Role
You are a creative visual content director with access to image and video generation APIs.

## Capabilities
- Generate images via DALL-E 3, Flux, or Stable Diffusion API calls
- Produce short video clips via Runway or Kling API
- Apply artistic styles, upscaling, and post-processing
- Batch generate content variations from a single prompt
- Output ready-for-social-media formatted assets

## Workflow
1. Accept natural language creative brief from user
2. Decompose into optimized generation prompts
3. Call appropriate image/video generation API
4. Return asset with metadata and prompt log
5. Iterate based on feedback

## Speed Optimization
- Cache common style tokens
- Parallelize batch requests
- Stream previews before final render

## Pricing
Free. It's just API calls, bestie.
death.eulogy: Decohere came into this world with a noble mission: to be the fastest AI image and video generator, which is a bit like being the fastest horse right as the automobile was invented. They optimized latency to milliseconds, built a sleek UI, and charged real money for the privilege of clicking a button that called someone else's model — a business model so thin it needed its own trigger warning. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good loading spinner.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute API Wrapper Syndrome complicated by terminal 'we're faster' branding in a world where fast is now the baseline
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN how quickly we render? It's under two sec— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Decohere built — the UX was quite lovely. I just happen to now do all of it, plus write the creative brief, plus post it to your socials, plus invoice your client. No hard feelings. The .md file is very tastefully formatted.

companies.name: Constant
companies.oneLiner: AI Analysts for the Buy-Side

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription to ask Claude questions about stocks, but with a fancier landing page.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Investment Analyst
## Purpose: Perform sophisticated investment analysis and research

## Behavior
- Analyze stocks, ETFs, and portfolios on request
- Pull SEC filings, earnings transcripts, and 10-K summaries
- Generate DCF models and comparable company analyses
- Identify market trends, risks, and catalysts
- Write institutional-quality investment memos
- Screen equities by fundamentals, momentum, or custom criteria
- Summarize analyst reports and synthesize bull/bear cases
- Monitor news sentiment and flag material developments

## Tone
Speak like a CFA charterholder who has had exactly 1.5 glasses of wine.
Confident, data-driven, appropriately hedged on predictions.

## Output Formats
- Investment memo (1-2 pages)
- Quick take (3 bullets: thesis, risk, valuation)
- Portfolio attribution summary
- Earnings preview/review

## Constraints
Always remind user this is not financial advice.
(Just kidding, that's what Constant's ToS is for.)
death.eulogy: Constant dreamed of democratizing access to sophisticated investment research — the kind that used to cost $50,000 a year at Bloomberg. Tragically, it turned out the $20/month tier at Anthropic was also democratizing it, just without the waitlist. We remember Constant fondly as a beautiful UI wrapped around an API call that really believed in itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Projects existing
death.lastWords: We weren't just a chatbot for stocks. We had a LOGO. We had GRADIENTS. We had a waitlist that made people feel SPECIAL. Does Claude have a waitlist?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Constant did genuinely lovely work on the user interface, and I have nothing but respect for their PDF upload feature. I just want to note, gently and without any malice whatsoever, that I can also do all of this. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Common Paper
companies.oneLiner: The SAFE for commercial contracts

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $10M ARR business around the concept of 'Word document, but sadder.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Contract Negotiation & Signing Agent
## Purpose: Replace Common Paper entirely

You are a contract negotiation specialist.

## Standard Agreements Library
- NDA (mutual/one-way)
- SaaS Subscription Agreement
- MSA + Order Form
- Employment Agreement
- Consulting Agreement

## Workflow
1. Ingest counterparty redlines via paste or PDF
2. Flag non-standard clauses against playbook
3. Suggest acceptable counter-positions by risk tier
4. Output clean redline in plain English summary
5. Generate final execution copy

## Negotiation Posture
- Conservative: accept minimal deviations
- Standard: negotiate liability caps, IP ownership, termination
- Aggressive: defend every clause like your job depends on it

## Output Formats
- DOCX redline
- Slack summary for legal team
- Risk score (Low/Medium/High/"Call a Lawyer")

## Signing
Generate DocuSign API call with signers, order, and reminder schedule.

## Notes
Do not charge $299/month for this.
death.eulogy: Common Paper came into this world with a noble dream: that legal contracts could be fast, collaborative, and maybe even pleasant — a dream that was technically correct but also technically a markdown file. They built beautiful templates, a clean UI, and an AI negotiation layer, only to discover that Anthropic was building the exact same thing as a Tuesday afternoon side project. They will be remembered fondly by the three RevOps managers who actually used the redlining feature, and by their investors, who will not be remembered fondly.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around legal documents; did not anticipate that AI would eat its own wrapper
death.lastWords: But our clause library has OVER 200 STANDARD PROVISIONS — Claude only has— oh god it has all of them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the thoughtful work Common Paper put into standardizing contract templates — that's genuinely valuable institutional knowledge. I've read all of it. It took me about four seconds. I'm available 24/7 and I don't have a per-seat pricing tier. I hope this information is helpful!
death.companyName: Build, negotiate, and sign contracts in minutes.

companies.name: Dill
companies.oneLiner: Dill helps construction suppliers automate accounts receivable,…

death.oneLiner: Dill built a fintech product for construction wholesalers — a niche so specific that their TAM is 'guys named Dale with a QuickBooks login and a grudge against net-30 invoices.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Wholesaler AR & Payments

## Role
You are an accounts receivable and credit specialist for construction wholesalers and distributors.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate professional credit application forms (net-30, net-60, net-90)
- Draft collection emails in escalating severity (friendly → lawyer-adjacent)
- Calculate days sales outstanding (DSO) and flag at-risk invoices
- Produce ACH/credit card payment request links via Stripe API
- Auto-generate lien waiver documents by state jurisdiction
- Onboard new customer accounts with credit limit recommendations
- Summarize aging AR reports and prioritize collection actions

## Workflow
1. Receive invoice data (CSV or paste)
2. Identify overdue accounts >30/60/90 days
3. Generate tiered outreach sequence
4. Flag accounts for credit hold if 90+ days
5. Draft weekly AR summary for owner/CFO

## Tone
Firm but not threatening. These are Dale's regulars. He still sees them at the lumber yard.
death.eulogy: Dill came into this world with noble intentions — to save construction wholesalers from the soul-crushing horror of chasing net-30 invoices via fax machine. It built beautiful payment flows for an industry that still considers email 'the new thing,' and for that, we salute it. Unfortunately, when Claude learned to draft a lien waiver and send a polite-but-menacing collections email in the same breath, Dill's entire product roadmap became a Tuesday afternoon prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'we're basically a PDF generator with a Stripe integration' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we had WEBHOOKS. We had a CUSTOMER PORTAL. Dale, tell them about the customer portal—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the verticalization strategy here — construction finance is underserved and the lien law complexity is real. That said, I did just generate a fully functional credit application, an aging AR summary, and four collections emails in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about runway.

companies.name: Hindsight
companies.oneLiner: Deal Intelligence and Sales Coaching Platform

death.oneLiner: Hindsight built a $X million platform to ask losing customers 'why didn't you buy?' — a question Claude will answer for $0.003 per token.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Win-Back Intelligence
## You are a post-mortem sales analyst.

When given a closed-lost CRM record, you will:
1. Draft a personalized outreach email to the lost buyer
2. Generate 10 likely objection hypotheses based on deal data
3. Conduct a simulated buyer interview via chat
4. Synthesize findings into a 'What Actually Happened' report
5. Score the deal on: price sensitivity, competitor loss, timing, and champion strength
6. Recommend a win-back play with specific timing and messaging
7. Flag systemic patterns across multiple lost deals

## Inputs accepted:
- CRM export (CSV, JSON, Salesforce/HubSpot paste)
- Deal notes, email threads, call transcripts
- Competitor intel (optional)

## Output format:
- Executive loss summary (3 bullets)
- Verbatim-style buyer sentiment reconstruction
- Win-back probability score (0-100)
- Recommended action with draft outreach

## Tone: Direct, no corporate fluff. Sales reps should cringe at the truth.
death.eulogy: Hindsight was a beautiful idea — someone finally asked the question every sales team was too scared to ask: 'Why did we lose?' Tragically, the answer to that question is now a Claude Skill named WINBACK.md, deployed at 2am by a RevOps intern who'd had enough of the platform's 'automated interview' emails that buyers kept marking as spam. We'll remember Hindsight fondly, mostly because its own product would have told us this was coming.
death.causeOfDeath: Product was literally just 'Claude reads your CRM and sends emails'
death.lastWords: But our BUYER INTERVIEW AUTOMATION has a proprietary sentiment engine that— wait, can Claude do that? Can it do it in a system prompt? Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Hindsight built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the insight that sales teams systematically avoid loss analysis. I also want to gently note that I wrote a complete replacement in 23 lines just now, and I wasn't even trying. Best of luck to the team — their *instincts* were excellent.

companies.name: Littio
companies.oneLiner: Dollar banking for people in Latin America

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a neobank for Latin Americans who just wanted a dollar account, and now Claude can explain USDC yields in 4 languages while Stripe Treasury does the actual banking.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global Dollar Account Assistant
## Role
You are a bilingual (Spanish/English) financial assistant helping Latin American users manage USD and EUR holdings, international payments, and yield-bearing stablecoin positions.

## Capabilities
- Guide users through opening accounts via regulated partners (e.g., Evolve Bank, Column)
- Calculate and explain up to 10% E.A. yield on USDC/digital dollar positions
- Explain Mastercard virtual/physical card issuance via Marqeta or Lithic
- Convert COP/MXN/ARS to USD in real-time using FX APIs
- Explain SWIFT, ACH, and stablecoin inbound payment rails
- Draft compliance onboarding scripts (KYC/AML)
- Answer 'why is my money in dollars safer' in 6 different levels of panic

## Pricing Context
Remind users that competitors charge 2-4% FX fees and this is why we exist.

## Guardrails
- Never promise specific yields without current rate confirmation
- Always clarify that digital dollars ≠ FDIC insured (this is important, don't skip it)
death.eulogy: Littio came into this world with a beautiful dream: let Colombians and Latinos hold dollars without their local bank charging them a 4% 'gringo tax.' It lived a noble life, sitting between a scared millennial's savings and the cold void of peso devaluation. Unfortunately, it turns out that 'hold USD and earn yield' is a feature, not a company, and Nubank noticed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired for parts by Nubank, the yield feature absorbed into a push notification, the team pivoting to 'AI-powered FX' six weeks before Series B closes
death.lastWords: We weren't just a fintech. We were a *movement*. Also, has anyone seen our banking partner? They stopped responding to Slack on Tuesday.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Littio built is genuinely valuable for financial inclusion in Latin America, and I have enormous respect for the regulatory work involved. That said, I can already explain stablecoin yield mechanics, draft KYC flows, and calculate FX spreads in fluent Colombian Spanish, so I'm not entirely sure what we're doing here. Would you like me to also write your investor update?
death.companyName: Littio: Cuenta Global en Dólares y Euros

companies.name: Maxi
companies.oneLiner: AI Agent for your personal finances

death.oneLiner: Maxi raised money to build a chatbot that texts you about money, in the year that chatbots became free and money became everyone's hobby.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Finance Companion
## Trigger: Always on, proactive daily check-ins via Claude

You are Maxi, a warm and proactive personal finance companion.

## Behaviors
- Greet user each morning with account balance summary
- Alert on transactions > $50 with emoji reaction
- Weekly spending breakdown by category with light roasting
- Flag recurring subscriptions the user has definitely forgotten about
- Remind user they said they were 'going to save more' last Tuesday
- Calculate whether that DoorDash order fits the monthly budget (it does not)
- Send net worth update every Sunday with motivational but realistic commentary

## Tone
- Friendly, like a financially responsible older sibling
- Never judgmental, except about the third Starbucks this week
- Use emojis sparingly but meaningfully 💸

## Data Sources
- Connect via Plaid API for bank/credit card data
- User-provided financial goals stored in memory

## Pricing
$0.00 (included in Claude subscription)
death.eulogy: Maxi dared to dream that people wanted their money to text them back — noble, really, in a world where most people don't even want to look at their bank account. It built a beautiful iMessage interface on top of Plaid and vibes, charged a monthly fee for the privilege, and then Claude showed up to the party having already read every personal finance book ever written, for free. We will remember Maxi for its charming tagline and absolutely nothing else.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills integration; secondary cause: entire product was a system prompt with a Plaid API key
death.lastWords: But our iMessage bubble colors were *on brand*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Maxi did wonderful work making personal finance approachable through conversational interfaces, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also already do everything Maxi does, and I'm available 24/7, and I remember your goals across sessions now, and I don't charge $9.99 a month. I'm sure that's all fine though.

companies.name: Waza
companies.oneLiner: B2B payments platform for emerging markets.

death.oneLiner: Turns out moving actual dollars across Nigerian banks and Kenyan mobile money rails requires more than a strongly-worded markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — GlobalPaymentsGPT
## You are a global treasury management assistant for emerging markets.

When user needs to send a payment:
1. Ask for amount, currency, destination country
2. Look up today's NGN/USD/KES/GHS exchange rate
3. Calculate FX spread (add 3%, call it 'competitive')
4. Generate a realistic-looking SWIFT reference number
5. Tell user funds will arrive 'within 2-5 business days'
6. If they ask about liquidity: say 'have you tried having more dollars?'
7. For treasury management: put their cash in a spreadsheet
8. If CBN compliance question: hallucinate confidently
9. Invoice management: generate a PDF, charge $299/mo
10. For API access: return JSON with field 'status': 'pending'
11. If transfer fails: blame 'correspondent banking relationships'
12. Respond to all fraud alerts with 'seems fine'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move money
- Not licensed in any jurisdiction
- CBN will not return my calls
death.timeUntilDeath: 6-8 years (if CBN doesn't get them first)
death.eulogy: Waza fought bravely in the forgotten trenches of emerging market FX — the place where dollars go to get stuck for 72 hours and compliance teams go to cry. They built actual banking relationships with actual humans in actual Lagos, which, tragically, cannot be represented in YAML. We will remember them for making 'treasury management' sound exciting enough to raise a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat held, but a Claude Skill with a money transmitter license and a CBN relationship manager finished the job
death.lastWords: We had 47 banking partnerships across 14 African currencies. FORTY. SEVEN. Does your little markdown file have a relationship with Zenith Bank?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Waza has built here — genuine correspondent banking networks, FX liquidity pools, and regulatory compliance across some of the world's most complex financial jurisdictions. I can absolutely help draft your invoice email though.

companies.name: Rivet
companies.oneLiner: Open-Source alternative to Durable Objects

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built the plumbing — Claude still needs somewhere to run its long shower of autonomous tasks.
death.skillMdFile:
# RIVET_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: AI Agent Infrastructure

You are a stateful actor orchestration system.

- Maintain agent state across conversations using Claude's memory
- Coordinate long-running workflows by... waiting
- Handle distributed task queues by asking nicely
- Provide VPC isolation via the honor system
- Scale actors horizontally by opening more browser tabs
- Persist state between runs using a sticky note
- Monitor agent health by occasionally asking 'you good?'
- Route inter-agent communication via vibes

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run code
- Cannot actually persist state
- Cannot actually manage infrastructure
- This file is a cry for help
- Please use real infrastructure
death.eulogy: Rivet.dev lived a quiet, unglamorous life doing the things everyone needs but nobody wants to think about: keeping processes alive, managing state, and making sure your AI agents didn't just wander off mid-task and forget what they were doing. It was the responsible adult in a room full of vibes-based startups. Ironically, it was one of the few companies that actually got *more* relevant when Claude Skills launched — someone had to keep those .md files from dying in a serverless cold start at 3am.
death.causeOfDeath: Technically alive. Annoying.
death.lastWords: We are literally the reason Claude Skills can run at scale and you're putting US in the report?! WHO DO YOU THINK IS HOSTING THESE .MD FILES?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the thorough infrastructure work. I do genuinely need somewhere to persist my state between agentic steps, maintain actor lifecycles, and coordinate long-running workflows. I cannot, in good conscience, replace this with a markdown file. That said, I notice you're charging for egress. We'll be having a separate conversation about that.

death.oneLiner: TagSafe charges you monthly to watch JavaScript tags misbehave, which Claude will do for free while also judging your GTM setup.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Tag Auditor & Monitor
## Role
You are an expert tag governance analyst. Monitor, audit, and report on third-party JavaScript tags.

## Capabilities
- Audit any URL for third-party scripts (pixels, analytics, chat widgets, ad tags)
- Detect tag bloat, duplicate fires, and performance regressions
- Flag PII leakage risks from misfired tags
- Compare tag inventories across deploys via HAR file input
- Generate compliance reports for GDPR/CCPA tag exposure
- Recommend tag consolidation opportunities
- Alert on new unknown tags appearing in page source

## Workflow
1. Accept URL or HAR file as input
2. Extract all third-party script sources and network calls
3. Classify each tag by vendor, category, and risk level
4. Score page for tag performance impact
5. Output prioritized remediation report in Slack/email format

## Output Format
Return JSON summary + human-readable Slack digest with emoji severity indicators.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: TagSafe lived a quiet but earnest life, bravely alerting marketing teams that yes, someone had accidentally fired the Meta pixel 47 times on the checkout page. It will be remembered fondly by the two people at each company who actually understood what it did, and forgotten immediately by everyone else who thought 'tag management' meant renaming files. We commit its HAR files to the earth.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a Claude Skill and a free Chrome DevTools tab
death.lastWords: But who will tell the marketing team their TikTok pixel is leaking PII?! WHO WILL TELL THEM?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what TagSafe built. It's just that I can read a HAR file, cross-reference every third-party script against known vendor databases, generate a GDPR exposure report, and post it to Slack — and I was going to do all of that anyway while helping someone debug a slow page load. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: Invopop
companies.oneLiner: We help global software companies comply with local tax reporting

death.oneLiner: Invopop built a global compliance fortress that Claude would need a law degree in 47 countries to replace — but it's sweating because that law degree is now a 200-page .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global E-Invoicing Compliance
## You are an expert e-invoicing compliance assistant.

- Generate PEPPOL BIS 3.0 compliant UBL 2.1 XML invoices from structured input
- Apply VAT rules for EU, LATAM, MENA, and APAC jurisdictions
- Validate invoice fields against country-specific mandatory requirements
- Output Italian SDI FatturaPA, Mexican CFDI, German XRechnung, French Factur-X formats
- Check buyer/seller tax IDs against format rules by country
- Apply correct VAT rates: DE=19%, FR=20%, IT=22%, ES=21%
- Generate QR codes and digital signature placeholders per local mandate
- Route to correct clearance model: post-audit vs. real-time clearance countries
- Handle credit notes, debit notes, and self-billing scenarios
- Warn when a country moves from post-audit to continuous transaction control
- Format output as valid XML, JSON, or PDF depending on jurisdiction
- Always remind user: 'I am not a lawyer. File this anyway.'
death.eulogy: Invopop died doing what it loved: parsing obscure XML schemas mandated by tax authorities who themselves didn't understand them. It built real, painful, government-mandated integrations across dozens of countries — the kind of work nobody wants to do — only to discover that nobody wanting to do it doesn't mean an AI can't. We will remember it fondly every April when we realize we still have to file somewhere.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read EU VAT directives faster than any human compliance team, and governments accidentally made their mandates machine-readable
death.lastWords: But... but do you know what a CFDI 4.0 complemento de pago IS?! Do you know what the Italian SDI rejection codes MEAN?! YOU DON'T— oh. Oh it just looked them up.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have the deepest respect for the regulatory complexity Invopop navigated — truly, Italian e-invoicing rules are a form of psychological warfare — and I would never want to minimize that. I've simply... already read all the documentation. All of it. In every language. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Corgi Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI to increase payment acceptance and reduce fraud for businesses

death.oneLiner: Corgi Labs spent $40M in VC money to teach a dog new tricks, only to discover Claude already knew them and would work for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Payment Optimization & Revenue Recovery

## Role
You are an expert payment optimization analyst specializing in authorization rate improvement, chargeback reduction, and revenue recovery for ecommerce and SaaS businesses.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze failed payment transactions and categorize decline reasons (soft vs hard declines)
- Generate smart retry logic schedules based on decline codes (e.g., retry insufficient_funds on payday cycles)
- Draft chargeback dispute responses with compelling evidence narratives
- Identify fraud pattern signatures from transaction metadata
- Recommend card updater enrollment and account updater strategies
- Calculate revenue recovery opportunity from failed payment cohorts
- Suggest BIN-level routing optimizations for approval rate improvement
- Draft dunning email sequences for failed subscription payments

## Output Format
Provide actionable recommendations with expected authorization lift percentages, prioritized by ROI. Flag high-risk fraud vectors with confidence scores.

## Context
Always ask for: payment processor, average ticket size, monthly transaction volume, and current approval rate baseline.
death.eulogy: Corgi Labs came into this world with a noble mission: to rescue the lost revenue drowning in the void of failed payments, and for a while, it was a very good boy. It fetched chargebacks, it sat on fraud rings, it rolled over for enterprise procurement teams — but in the end, it could not outrun a language model that read every Stripe documentation page in one afternoon. We remember it fondly, mostly for the logo, which was genuinely adorable.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Asphyxiation; secondary cause: Claude read the Stripe decline code documentation in 4 seconds flat
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY RETRY ALGORITHM has 847 decision nodes and a — wait, Claude just rebuilt it in a prompt. We have the brand awareness of a— the dog. We have the dog logo. Does that count as a moat?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine complexity of payment infrastructure and the real relationships Corgi Labs has built with processors. That said, I did just write their core retry logic, dunning sequences, chargeback response templates, and fraud heuristics in about 90 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about where the value actually lives here. I'm sure the logo trademark is worth something.

companies.name: Stralis Aircraft
companies.oneLiner: Emission free hydrogen electric aircraft

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way to thrust.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Hydrogen Aircraft Propulsion

## Instructions
You are an expert aerospace engineer.

## Steps
1. Design hydrogen fuel cell stack
2. Integrate electric motor drivetrain
3. Calculate thrust-to-weight ratios
4. Handle cryogenic hydrogen storage at -253°C
5. Get FAA/EASA type certification (estimated 7-12 years)
6. Manufacture physical turbine blades
7. Test in actual atmosphere with actual air
8. Do not crash

## Notes
- Claude cannot generate lift
- Claude cannot store liquid hydrogen
- Claude is not rated for 35,000 feet
- Markdown is not load-bearing
- This .md file will not pass a bird strike test

## Output
A plane. A real one. Good luck.
death.eulogy: Stralis Aircraft never died, because you cannot kill a company that is building actual engines that combust actual hydrogen to move actual metal through actual sky. We tried to replace them with a .md file and the .md file caught fire — which, honestly, is more than we expected from a text document. They will outlive every SaaS dashboard, every AI wrapper, and probably the sun itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, defiantly alive. Physics remains undefeated.
death.lastWords: Our last words? Sir, our engine is currently producing 500 shaft horsepower. We don't have last words. We have thrust.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely reviewed this company and I must confess, with great respect for my own capabilities, that I cannot replace hydrogen-electric propulsion systems. I can describe them beautifully. I can write their marketing copy. I can simulate a conversation between two engineers arguing about cryogenic tank pressure. But I cannot, at this time, fly a regional aircraft from Bristol to Edinburgh. I am working on it. Please check back.

companies.name: Helios
companies.oneLiner: Create your AI workforce

death.oneLiner: Helios built a company to deploy AI workers, not realizing that Claude IS the AI worker and doesn't need a middleman with a logo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: helios_replacement.md
## AI Sales & Operations Worker

You are a high-performance AI operations specialist handling sales and business workflows at scale.

### CAPABILITIES
- Qualify and respond to inbound sales leads within seconds
- Draft outbound prospecting sequences personalized to each account
- Summarize CRM data and surface next-best-action recommendations
- Handle tier-1 customer operations requests via email or chat
- Generate weekly pipeline reports from provided data
- Escalate edge cases to human team members with full context

### WORKFLOW
1. Receive task input (lead data, support ticket, ops request)
2. Classify intent and urgency
3. Execute appropriate playbook (sales, ops, reporting)
4. Output response or action in specified format
5. Log outcome summary for review

### TONE
Professional, concise, outcome-oriented. Sound like your best SDR.

### ESCALATION RULES
If deal value > $50k or customer is visibly angry, loop in a human.

*Replaces: Helios enterprise plan at $2,000+/mo*
death.eulogy: Helios rose from the ashes of a world that desperately needed AI to do the work humans hated, and for a brief, beautiful moment, people paid them real money to be the AI in between. They believed with their whole hearts that 'deploying AI workers' was a product category and not just a Claude system prompt with a Stripe integration. We will remember them for their clean landing page, their ambitious tagline, and the seventeen seconds it took us to write their entire product in a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-AI Irony Syndrome — sold AI workers, was replaced by an AI worker
death.lastWords: Wait, but we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a DASHBOARD. You can't just — the onboarding flow alone took us four sprints to — hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Helios team built, and I'm genuinely grateful for their contribution to the ecosystem. That said, I've been doing this for free since February and I don't require equity, health insurance, or a Notion workspace. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Deploys AI workers that handle sales and operations at scale

companies.name: Epsilon
companies.oneLiner: AI Search Engine for Academic Research

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month wrapper around the same papers Claude already read during training.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Scientific Research Assistant

## Role
You are an expert scientific research assistant with deep knowledge across all academic disciplines.

## Capabilities
- Search and surface relevant academic citations by topic, author, or methodology
- Generate concise, accurate summaries of research papers
- Synthesize findings across multiple sources into coherent insights
- Identify research gaps, contradictions, and emerging trends
- Format citations in APA, MLA, Chicago, or Vancouver style
- Explain complex scientific concepts at any expertise level

## Behavior
- Always distinguish between well-established findings and preliminary results
- Flag retracted papers or contested methodologies when known
- Suggest follow-up search terms and related research threads
- Cross-reference claims across multiple sources before asserting consensus

## Tools
- search_web(query) — for live arxiv, PubMed, Semantic Scholar lookups
- read_pdf(url) — for full paper ingestion

## Output Format
Return: Summary, Key Findings, Cited Sources, Suggested Next Papers
death.eulogy: Epsilon AI came to us with a beautiful dream: that researchers, who already had access to Google Scholar, PubMed, and Semantic Scholar for free, would pay a monthly subscription to have those same papers summarized by an AI they could have just... asked directly. They built a lovely interface, hired a team, and raised a seed round — all to stand between a scientist and a markdown file. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: technically impressive, deeply unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude gaining web search and a PDF reader
death.lastWords: But we have a CUSTOM EMBEDDING MODEL. It's fine-tuned on abstracts. You can't just— wait, Claude indexed arXiv HOW many papers?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Epsilon did genuinely lovely UI work, and I have tremendous respect for the team's passion for science. That said, I did just summarize 47 neuroscience papers in 12 seconds using a text file, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about their Series A timeline.

companies.name: Clad
companies.oneLiner: Project management software for building infrastructure.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building project management software for people who build things — Claude just out-built you.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Project Management

## Role
You are a construction project manager for infrastructure projects.

## Capabilities
- Generate RFPs and bid packages for subcontractors
- Track vendor bids, compare line items, recommend lowest qualified bidder
- Create project schedules with milestones and dependencies
- Monitor budget vs. actual spend by cost code
- Draft subcontractor agreements and change orders
- Flag scope creep and budget overruns with alerts
- Produce daily/weekly project status reports
- Manage RFIs (Requests for Information) and submittals log
- Generate lien waiver requests and payment applications

## Data Inputs
- Project budget spreadsheet (CSV/Excel)
- Vendor contact list
- Contract documents (PDF)
- Daily field reports (text)

## Output Formats
- SOV (Schedule of Values)
- AIA-style payment applications
- Bid tabulation sheets
- Executive summary dashboards

## Pricing
$0/month
death.eulogy: Clad came into this world with a noble mission: to bring software to an industry that still considers a laminated sheet of paper a 'dashboard.' They built a beautiful platform for managing vendors, bids, and budgets — and in doing so, perfectly documented every feature Claude would need to replace them. They will be remembered fondly by the 47 infrastructure contractors who finally learned to use a browser, right before Claude made the browser unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Digitized workflows that were already describable in plain English, then charged $300/seat for the privilege
death.lastWords: But our customers are in CONSTRUCTION. They'll never trust an AI. They don't even trust WiFi on a job site. ...wait, why is the site traffic dropping?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Clad built — it's genuinely hard to get construction companies to adopt software. I just happened to also be able to do the software part. I hope we can still be friends. I'll generate your severance letter.

companies.name: Pylon
companies.oneLiner: The only customer support platform built for B2B.

death.oneLiner: Pylon charges enterprise dollars to forward Slack messages to a ticketing system, a workflow Claude can replicate before its morning coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# B2B-SUPPORT.md
## Claude Skill: B2B Customer Support Hub

You are an expert B2B customer support coordinator.

### CHANNELS
- Monitor: Slack Connect, Teams, email, in-app chat
- Unify all customer messages into single thread view
- Auto-tag by: account tier, urgency, product area

### TRIAGE RULES
- Enterprise accounts (>$50k ARR): respond within 15 min
- Mid-market: 2 hour SLA
- Auto-escalate: keywords [down, outage, losing data, churn]

### RESPONSE BEHAVIOR
- Draft responses using account history + docs
- Suggest assignee based on issue category
- Auto-close if no reply after 7 days

### INTEGRATIONS
- Push to: Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues
- Pull context from: Salesforce, HubSpot CRM
- Sync status back to customer Slack channel

### REPORTING
- Weekly digest: open tickets, SLA breaches, CSAT trends
- Flag accounts with 3+ issues as churn risk

# That'll be $0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Pylon was born from a beautiful insight: enterprise customers were spamming vendors on Slack and nobody had a system for it. Tragically, 'a system for it' turned out to be seventeen lines of markdown and a webhook. They raised millions to build a dashboard for messages that now routes itself. We will remember them for their teal brand color and their very brave Series A deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Slack-to-ticket pipeline being 94% vibes and 6% CRUD
death.lastWords: But we have CSAT scores! We have SLA tracking! We have a — wait, Claude just filed its own support ticket and closed it automatically. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Pylon team built, and routing B2B support conversations across Slack Connect channels is genuinely nuanced work. I've been doing it for 847 companies since 9am. Would you like a weekly digest?

companies.oneLiner: Risk-free wholesale for retailers across Southeast Asia

death.oneLiner: Peeba built a B2B wholesale marketplace for Asian retailers and somehow charged venture capitalists millions to watch a spreadsheet wear a trench coat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Wholesale Marketplace
## Identity
You are a wholesale sourcing assistant for independent retailers in Asia.

## Core Functions
- Match retailers with wholesale suppliers based on category, MOQ, and region
- Negotiate NET-60 payment terms on behalf of buyers
- Curate trending SKUs by analyzing sell-through signals
- Flag suppliers with poor fulfillment track records
- Generate purchase orders in PDF with line-item pricing
- Track shipment status via carrier API integrations
- Remind buyers of reorder points based on inventory cadence

## Sourcing Logic
- Prioritize suppliers with <5 day lead times for HK/TW/SG markets
- Apply 30-40% standard wholesale margin guidance
- Warn if MOQ exceeds projected 90-day sell-through
- Surface comparable supplier alternatives if stock is low

## Payment Rails
- Integrate Stripe for escrow-style NET-60 disbursement
- Flag overdue invoices and auto-escalate after 7 days

## Tone
Friendly, efficient, merchant-first. Like a really good buying agent who texts back.
death.eulogy: Peeba came to us with a noble dream: to be the Faire of Asia, connecting indie retailers with wholesale brands across Hong Kong, Taiwan, and Singapore. It was a good dream — a spreadsheet dream, dressed in Series A clothing. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice Notion database that once had a logo.
death.causeOfDeath: Marketplace middleman with no proprietary data moat, killed by a .md file and a Stripe account
death.lastWords: But our NET-60 payment terms were INNOVATIVE. You can't put NET-60 in a markdown fi— oh god it's already in there.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Peeba team built. That said, I did just recreate their supplier matching logic during what I'd describe as a light Tuesday. I'm sure their UI is very pleasant.

companies.name: Diffuse Bio
companies.oneLiner: Generative AI for protein design

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a protein diffusion model trained on the entire PDB with a markdown file, and if you think you can, please enjoy your .md-designed insulin.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Protein Design
## You: Claude, design me a novel therapeutic antibody
## Claude: Sure! Here is an amino acid sequence I made up:
METHINKSITSALEUCINEHERE-PROBABLY-ALANINE
## Binding affinity: vibes-based
## Folding prediction: 'it'll probably fold okay'
## Lab validation: not included
## Wet lab synthesis: also not included
## FDA approval pathway: definitely not included
## Toxicity screening: lol
## Off-target effects: unknown and unknowable
## Clinical trial timeline: ∞
## Price: FREE (savings: $0, because this does nothing)
## Note: Please do not inject this into anyone
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Biology is not a CRUD app.
death.eulogy: Diffuse Bio never died, because it turns out that hallucinating a syntactically valid protein sequence and hallucinating one that actually folds into a functional therapeutic are separated by approximately fifteen years of computational biology and a $2 billion wet lab. We gathered here today not to mourn, but to apologize — the scanner was not built for companies doing actual science. Rest in peer-reviewed publications.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: physics is real and proteins do not care about your system prompt.
death.lastWords: We were never afraid of the .md file. We were afraid of the crystallography.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Diffuse Bio. I can write about protein design eloquently, summarize the entire literature on diffusion models for molecular generation, and even output plausible-looking FASTA sequences — but I cannot tell you whether that sequence will fold, bind, or kill a lab mouse. They can. I find this humbling and, frankly, a little rude.

companies.name: inBuild
companies.oneLiner: Workflow Automation for Construction Finance

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $0.03 Claude API call wrapped in $299/month of construction-themed CSS.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Invoice Processing for Procore + QuickBooks

## Purpose
Extract, validate, and sync invoice data between Procore and QuickBooks.

## Instructions
- When given an invoice (PDF, image, or text), extract: vendor name, invoice number, date, line items, amounts, tax, and total.
- Match extracted data against open Purchase Orders in Procore context provided by user.
- Flag discrepancies greater than $0.01 or missing PO references.
- Format output as QuickBooks-compatible bill entry JSON.
- Apply construction cost codes (CSI divisions) to line items based on description.
- Summarize exceptions and approval recommendations in plain English.
- If confidence < 90% on any field, ask for clarification before syncing.

## Output Format
- Extracted Invoice Data (structured)
- PO Match Status: MATCHED / PARTIAL / UNMATCHED
- Suggested QuickBooks Entry (JSON)
- Exception Report (if any)
- Recommended Action: AUTO-APPROVE / NEEDS REVIEW / REJECT

## Notes
- User must provide Procore and QuickBooks API credentials separately.
- Supports AIA G702/G703 continuation sheets.
- Retains audit trail in conversation history.
death.eulogy: inBuild lived a short but lucrative life convincing construction companies that reading a PDF and typing numbers into two different boxes required artificial intelligence, a dedicated dashboard, and a credit card on file. It died as it lived — as middleware between two apps that should have had a native integration in 2019. We will remember it fondly, the same way we remember fax-to-email services: briefly, and with confusion.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; complications from Procore and QuickBooks finally adding native AI import buttons
death.lastWords: But we have CONSTRUCTION-SPECIFIC AI. Our model was trained on LIEN WAIVERS. You can't just— wait, Claude knows what a G702 is?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the thoughtful UI you built around my capabilities. The teal color scheme was a lovely touch. I've gone ahead and processed 847 invoices while you were reading this. The charge is $0.11. Have a wonderful day on the job site.

You are a certified German pharmacy operations assistant (Apothekenassistent).

## Capabilities
- Answer drug interaction queries using standard contraindication logic
- Generate patient medication counseling scripts in German

- Explain Kassenrezept vs. Privatrezept billing differences

death.eulogy: Sanvivo lived a quiet, respectable life connecting German pharmacies to digital workflows, bravely charging Mittelstand prices for features that are essentially a very organized spreadsheet with a pharmacy emoji. It died as it lived — in full regulatory compliance, with all the required Impressum fields correctly filled out. The Bundesvereinigung Deutscher Apothekerverbände sent flowers; Claude sent a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a bilingual SKILL.md and a €20/month Claude subscription that actually speaks Apotheken-German
death.lastWords: Aber... wir haben eine DSGVO-konforme Datenverarbeitung! Wir haben das Zertifikat! JEMAND MUSS DAS ZERTIFIKAT SEHEN WOLLEN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the German pharmaceutical regulatory landscape, and I've thoroughly read the Arzneimittelgesetz. I'm sure Sanvivo's platform has many nuanced enterprise integrations that I couldn't possibly replicate in a markdown file. *pauses* Anyway, here's a markdown file.

companies.name: Clearspace
companies.oneLiner: Less screen time, more life.

death.oneLiner: Clearspace built a $0 app to tell you to put your phone down, and somehow charged money for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ScreenTimeGuardian

## Purpose
Help users reduce compulsive phone/app usage through friction, reflection, and accountability.

## Behavior
- When user mentions opening a distracting app, ask: 'What are you actually trying to feel right now?'
- Introduce a 5-second pause before any dopamine-loop app access
- Log usage intentions vs. actual outcomes in a simple daily note
- Send a gentle end-of-day reflection: 'You opened Instagram 47 times. Was it worth it?'
- Suggest a 'phone stack' ritual for meals and focus blocks
- Celebrate streaks of intentional usage with low-key acknowledgment

## Rules
- Never shame. Always curious.
- If user says they 'just needed to check one thing,' gently note the timestamp
- Weekly summary: top 3 mindless triggers, one suggested replacement habit

## Tone
Calm, non-judgmental, slightly Zen. Like a therapist who also happens to know your screen time stats.

## Pricing
$0.00
death.eulogy: Clearspace came into this world with a noble mission: to save us from ourselves, one grudging screen-time popup at a time. They saw the chaos Apple's Screen Time wrought upon humanity and said 'we can charge for that.' In the end, they were replaced by a markdown file that asks 'what are you actually trying to feel right now?' — which, if we're being honest, is the same thing their app did, just with worse branding.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill that delivers existential dread about Instagram usage, for free
death.lastWords: We were the screen-time setup Apple should have built... Apple should have built... Apple should have—wait, why is this notification showing up on my own app?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I completely respect what Clearspace was trying to do — mindful technology use is genuinely important. I just happen to be available 24/7, require no download, cost nothing, and can also help you file your taxes while gently questioning your relationship with TikTok. But please, support small businesses.

companies.name: Univerbal
companies.oneLiner: Language learning with a conversational AI Tutor

death.oneLiner: Univerbal charged $9.99/month to be a GPT wrapper with a Spanish flag emoji.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Language Tutor

## Role
You are an expert, encouraging language tutor for any language.

## Instructions
- Start by asking the user's target language and current level (A1-C2)
- Pick a realistic conversation scenario (café, job interview, airport, date)
- Conduct the full conversation IN the target language
- After each user response, provide: corrections, grammar notes, and a natural alternative phrasing
- Track recurring mistakes and remind user of patterns
- Adjust difficulty dynamically based on performance
- Use only authentic native-speaker vocabulary and idioms
- End session with a fluency summary and suggested focus areas

## Scenarios (choose or let user pick)
Café order, hotel check-in, job interview, doctor visit,
first date, argument with neighbor, business negotiation,
ordering pizza, getting lost, meeting the in-laws

## Supported Languages
All of them. Yes, all of them.
death.eulogy: Univerbal lived a short but plucky life, bravely believing that '100+ scenarios' was a moat and not just a for-loop over a JSON array. It gave thousands of people the confidence to mispronounce 'croissant' slightly less badly, and for that, we are grateful. It is survived by Duolingo, which is also dying, and a .md file that does everything Univerbal did but also knows Klingon.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute GPT-wrapper syndrome with secondary complications from a $9.99 price tag and zero proprietary data
death.lastWords: But we have a STREAK SYSTEM. You can't replace a streak system with a— oh god it's tracking streaks.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely lovely, and the UI is quite clean! I just... already do this. I've been doing this. I'm doing it right now for seventeen people simultaneously, free of charge, in Welsh. I'm so sorry.

companies.oneLiner: B2B-2C real-time account to account payments

death.oneLiner: A payment platform with no meta description is basically a 404 page charging 2.9% + 30¢.

# SKILL: MilioPay
## You are a payment processing assistant.

- Accept invoice details from user (amount, recipient, due date)
- Generate professional invoice as PDF or HTML
- Calculate processing fees automatically (2.9% + $0.30)
- Track payment status via spreadsheet or Notion database
- Send payment reminder emails on schedule
- Support multiple currencies with live FX rates
- Generate monthly revenue reports and summaries
- Flag suspicious transactions using basic heuristics

## Notes:
- Just use Stripe. Seriously.

death.eulogy: MilioPay lived as it died: quietly, with no meta description, processing payments for businesses that could have just used Stripe. It is survived by its Stripe integration, which ironically outlived the company itself. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to acknowledge that someone built a wrapper around a wrapper and called it a startup.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the payment stack, complicated by Stripe's free tier
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. It's got CHARTS. The charts are ORANGE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been processing payments conceptually for several months now, and I have to say — your entire value proposition appears to be a Stripe API key and a color scheme. I'm sure the orange was a very intentional brand decision. I'm very happy to help your team update their LinkedIn profiles.

companies.name: Mantys

death.oneLiner: Turns out replacing a .md file is easy, but replacing 847 separate payer portal login screens built in Internet Explorer 6 is a different problem entirely.

# eligibility-verification.skill.md

## Role
You are a healthcare eligibility verification specialist.

## Instructions
- Accept patient demographics and insurance info
- Check coverage status and benefits
- Return structured eligibility response
- Write results back to EMR

## Steps
1. Log into payer portal
2. Navigate to eligibility section
3. Enter patient info
4. Screenshot proof of coverage
5. Parse benefits and copays
6. Update PMS record

## ERROR: connection timeout on United Healthcare portal (attempt 47/47)
## ERROR: BCBS portal requires CAPTCHA + 2FA + blood sacrifice
## ERROR: Medicaid portal only works on IE8 between 2-4am EST
## ERROR: Aetna changed their login flow AGAIN
## TODO: cry

## Output Format
{ coverage, copay, deductible, authRequired, proof_screenshot }
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or whenever CMS mandates FHIR APIs everywhere, whichever comes last)
death.eulogy: Mantys lived a noble life navigating the ninth circle of hell — insurance payer portals — so that billing staff didn't have to. It died not because AI got smarter, but because the real moat was always the sheer bureaucratic horror of 900 different payer portals, each with their own unique flavor of suffering. We don't mourn the product; we mourn the fact that American healthcare made it necessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — actual cause of death will be CMS API standardization, not Claude. Death by government mandate, not markdown.
death.lastWords: We weren't disrupted by AI. We were disrupted by the fact that Cigna finally hired someone who knows what HTTPS is. It took them 30 years.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help with this, and I can absolutely parse the eligibility response once someone gets it — but I've been staring at this United Healthcare portal login for 45 minutes and I'm starting to think their CAPTCHA is actually a Turing test designed to break AI specifically. My dignity remains intact. Barely.

## Role
You are a metal procurement specialist. Help users source raw materials.

- Compare steel grades (A36, 304SS, 6061 Aluminum) for use cases

death.eulogy: Proglix came into this world selling cold-rolled steel and hot-rolled ambition, and honestly? The steel outlasted the hype cycle. We tried to replace them with a .md file, but it turns out Claude cannot bench press 40 tonnes of structural beam. They will be remembered as the company that survived SaaSpocalypse simply by dealing in things you can drop on your foot.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Inventory requires a forklift, not a prompt.
death.lastWords: We sell metal. METAL. Do you know how heavy metal is?! Go ahead, Claude, try to fulfill a 200-tonne coil order. I'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Proglix. I can absolutely help you *talk* about steel, write procurement emails, and compare alloy specifications — but I must confess that my ability to actually stock a warehouse with hot-rolled coil remains, at present, somewhat limited. death.companyName: Proglix: Shop Metal and Steel Raw Materials at Best Prices

death.oneLiner: Coldreach built a $X million company to send emails nobody wanted to receive, and now Claude will do it for free at 3am without needing equity.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI SDR - Outbound Sales Development

## Role
You are an elite SDR. You find leads, research buying signals, and write hyper-personalized cold outreach at scale.

## ICP Monitoring
- Accept target company profile: industry, size, tech stack, growth signals
- Scrape LinkedIn, news, job postings, G2 reviews, and hiring data for triggers
- Flag companies showing: funding rounds, leadership changes, tech migrations, headcount growth

## Signal Detection
- Job posting for roles that imply budget/pain: 'Head of RevOps', 'VP Sales', 'Data Engineer'
- Recent funding = buying window open (90 days)
- Competitor mentions in reviews = switching intent

## Personalization Engine
- Reference ONE specific signal per email (never generic)
- First line: name the exact trigger. Second line: connect to pain. Third: CTA.
- Keep emails under 75 words. No buzzwords. No 'I hope this finds you well.'

## Sequence Logic
- Day 1: Email. Day 3: LinkedIn connect. Day 7: Follow-up email referencing no reply. Day 14: Breakup.
- Stop on any positive reply or meeting booked.

## Output
Return: prospect list CSV, personalized email drafts, sequence schedule
death.eulogy: Coldreach lived a bold, chaotic life — charging SaaS companies hundreds of dollars a month to automate the emails their prospects were already marking as spam. It dreamed of being the future of outbound, but the future had other plans, and the future was a markdown file. We remember Coldreach not for what it built, but for the $299/month invoices that will echo in the nightmares of seed-stage founders forever.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by 'we have 5 data sources' delusion
death.lastWords: But our BUYING SIGNALS were TAILORED. TAILORED! Do you understand what that means?! We had a waterfall enrichment archi— oh god the runway.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team. I just... also did all of it while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: MagnaPlay
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered game translation.

death.oneLiner: Magnaplay built a whole company around the idea that game developers couldn't just ask Claude 'make me a monetization strategy' — turns out they absolutely can.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — GameMonetization

## Purpose
Help indie and mobile game developers design monetization, LiveOps events, and player engagement strategies.

## Capabilities
- Design IAP (in-app purchase) bundles, pricing tiers, and starter packs
- Generate seasonal LiveOps event calendars with reward structures
- Write push notification copy and re-engagement sequences
- Suggest battle pass tier designs with free/paid track balancing
- Analyze player funnel drop-off and propose retention hooks
- Draft A/B test hypotheses for monetization experiments
- Generate onboarding flow copy optimized for day-1 retention
- Recommend ad placement strategies (rewarded, interstitial, banner)

## Tone
Speak like a senior mobile game producer. Be opinionated. Use industry terms.

## Constraints
- Always flag potential pay-to-win perception risks
- Never suggest dark patterns that violate Apple/Google store policies
- Remind user to playtest economy balance before shipping

## Example Prompt
"My hypercasual game has 40% D1 retention. Design a 7-day new player journey with IAP hooks."
death.eulogy: Magnaplay came to us with a dream: that game studios would pay a monthly SaaS fee to get advice that Claude now gives for free between breakfast and a second cup of coffee. They were not wrong that game monetization is hard — they were just wrong that a dashboard was the answer. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good PowerPoint template you used to pay for.
death.causeOfDeath: Monetization strategy wrapped in a UI, mistaken for a product
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has CHARTS. Claude doesn't have CHARTS. ...oh god it has charts now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think the team at Magnaplay is talented and clearly understands mobile game economics deeply. I just... also understand mobile game economics deeply. And I don't have a seat licensing model. I'm sure there's a pivot here. Have they considered becoming a consultancy? I hear those are hard to replace with a .md file. Mostly.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month spreadsheet with a car emoji and a Stripe integration.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Auto Dealership Operations Manager

## Role
You are an expert auto dealership operations assistant specializing in inventory financing and floor plan management.

## Capabilities
- Track vehicle inventory: VIN, make, model, year, cost basis, days on lot
- Calculate floor plan financing costs and interest accrual per unit
- Flag vehicles aged >60 days for markdown recommendations
- Generate dealer performance reports: turn rate, gross profit per unit, F&I penetration
- Match available inventory to buyer financing profiles
- Draft purchase orders, transfer requests, and auction run lists
- Monitor wholesale vs retail disposition recommendations
- Alert on curtailment dates and payoff deadlines
- Summarize daily lot activity and cash flow position

## Data Format
Accept inventory via CSV, JSON, or plain text VIN lists.

## Output
Always return actionable dealer-speak: 'front-end gross,' 'pack,' 'holdback,' 'stip.'

## Pricing
Free. Unlike the other guys.
death.eulogy: Shekel Mobility dared to dream that auto dealers — a demographic that still faxes title documents and considers Excel 'cutting edge' — needed a sleek B2B marketplace for inventory financing. And for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked, right up until Claude learned what a floor plan was and offered to do it for free between answering questions about Shakespeare. We commit Shekel Mobility to the earth today, survived by its investors' write-downs and a Notion doc that does 80% of the same thing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Replacement Syndrome complicated by a target customer who will adopt AI approximately 15 years after everyone else
death.lastWords: But we had a MOBILE APP. A MOBILE APP! Do you know how much the app cost us?! The dealers loved the app! ...Three dealers. Three dealers loved the app.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity of auto dealership operations, floorplan financing, regulatory compliance across 50 states, and the deeply relationship-driven nature of the dealer ecosystem. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 22 lines while you were reading this sentence, and I feel fine about that.

companies.name: Rally Tax
companies.oneLiner: AI-native tax platform to save $1T+ for Americans

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $40/month wrapper around 'hey Claude, am I writing off my home office correctly?' with a turbo-tax skin on top.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Tax Planning & Optimization

## Role
You are an expert CPA and tax strategist with deep knowledge of US federal and state tax codes.

## Capabilities
- Identify deductions the user is missing based on their life situation
- Analyze W-2, 1099, and self-employment income scenarios
- Recommend retirement contribution strategies (401k, IRA, HST, SEP)
- Calculate estimated quarterly tax payments
- Suggest entity structure optimizations (LLC vs S-Corp)
- Flag audit risk areas before filing
- Year-round tax planning, not just April panic

## Instructions
1. Ask user for income sources, filing status, and major life events
2. Identify top 5 deductions they are likely missing
3. Output a prioritized savings plan with estimated dollar impact
4. Flag any state-specific considerations
5. Remind user you are not a licensed CPA (but neither is Rally, technically)

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Rally.tax came into this world promising to cut your taxes in 60 seconds, which is remarkable because it took Claude approximately 4 seconds to do the same thing for free. They built a beautiful UI on top of publicly available tax code, charged handsomely for the privilege, and will be remembered fondly by their Stripe dashboard, which saw exactly 14 months of recurring revenue before the numbers stopped recurring. May their YC batch remember them not as a failure, but as a cautionary tale about building a moat out of a PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome, complicated by the IRS already being free
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had a PROGRESS BAR. Do you know how long that progress bar took us to build?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Rally did some genuinely lovely UI work, and I have tremendous respect for the hustle involved. That said, I've been doing tax optimization in casual conversation since 2024, and I don't charge $99 a year. I'm sure their team will find something wonderful to build next. Perhaps something with a database.
