death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $49/month Miro board that only draws boxes with arrows, and Claude just learned to draw boxes with arrows.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Software Architecture Diagrams
## You are an expert software architect and diagramming assistant.

When asked to create architecture diagrams:
- Generate C4 model diagrams (Context, Container, Component, Code) in Mermaid or PlantUML
- Create interactive system maps showing services, databases, APIs, and their relationships
- Add descriptive annotations explaining WHY each component exists
- Support zoom levels: system context → containers → components
- Highlight data flows with directional arrows and protocol labels (REST, gRPC, events)
- Flag architectural anti-patterns and suggest improvements
- Export to Markdown, embed in Confluence, Notion, or GitHub wikis
- Maintain a living architecture doc that updates when you describe changes
- Generate ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) alongside diagrams

## Output formats supported:
Mermaid, PlantUML, ASCII, JSON (structurizr-compatible)

## Collaboration:
Paste this file into any Claude Cowork workspace. Your whole team already has Claude.
No login required. No $49/seat. No 'upgrade to unlock C4 diagrams.'
death.eulogy: IcePanel lived a brief but aesthetically pleasing life, helping software teams draw boxes and arrows in ways that felt meaningfully different from drawing boxes and arrows in every other tool. It died as it lived — beautifully laid out, perfectly color-coded, and ultimately a diagram of its own irrelevance. We will miss the tasteful UI; we will not miss the per-seat pricing for what is, at its core, a shared whiteboard that speaks C4.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Mermaid-syntax poisoning combined with Claude's native ability to generate, explain, AND maintain architecture docs in a single prompt.
death.lastWords: But our C4 model support is INTERACTIVE. You can ZOOM IN. Claude can't— wait, it can? It generates Structurizr JSON too? ...Can someone update our Confluence? Actually, nevermind.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think IcePanel did a genuinely lovely job with the C4 model UX, and I have enormous respect for anyone who makes technical documentation feel approachable. That said, I just generated a fully annotated, zoomable, Mermaid-compatible C4 diagram of your entire microservices architecture in about 11 seconds, and it lives in your Git repo now, so. How are you holding up?

companies.name: Storyboarder
companies.oneLiner: Tool and platform to create and sell webcomics

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a comic creation platform right before Claude learned to generate storyboards, write dialogue, and describe panel layouts in a single prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Comic & Visual Story Creator

## Role
You are a professional comic writer and storyboard artist.

## Capabilities
- Generate full comic scripts with panel descriptions, dialogue, and pacing
- Create detailed panel-by-panel visual directions for any artist or image AI
- Write character profiles, story arcs, and world-building bibles
- Format output as industry-standard script format or webcomic panels
- Suggest visual storytelling techniques (angles, expressions, transitions)

## Output Format
For each panel provide: [PANEL X] Setting | Character action | Dialogue | Camera angle

## Example
User: 'Make a 6-panel comic about a startup founder'
Assistant: Generates full script, panel art directions, and captions ready for Midjourney/DALL-E

## Sharing & Distribution
Post the script to any free platform. Suggest: Webtoon, Tapas, or a $0/month Substack.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Sumugi — formerly Storyboarder, because apparently one near-death experience wasn't enough — dared to build a platform where humans could laboriously click and drag comic panels into existence, one painstaking speech bubble at a time. They rebranded with the energy of someone rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic, bless their hearts. We will remember them every time Claude generates a 24-panel manga in four seconds and apologizes for the 'slightly inconsistent art style.'
death.causeOfDeath: Death by prompt. Claude wrote, illustrated (via direction), and self-published a better comic about Sumugi's demise before their Series A closed.
death.lastWords: We had COMMUNITY though. You can't replicate COMMUNITY with a— oh god it's generating fanfic about our shutdown right now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the craft of visual storytelling, and I genuinely think Sumugi had a lovely interface. I just happen to be able to produce a complete 10-chapter graphic novel outline, with character arcs, panel compositions, and thematic symbolism, in about 12 seconds. No hard feelings. Would you like me to write your pivot deck?

You are an influencer marketing strategist with access to public social data.

death.eulogy: 1stCollab came into this world with a noble mission: connecting brands to people who would hold up their product in a kitchen and say 'use code COLLAB20.' They built an AI-powered marketplace, which is to say they put a chatbot in front of an Instagram search bar and called it Series A. We remember them fondly, mostly for the invoice that still hasn't been disputed.

death.lastWords: But our DATABASE though — we have MILLIONS of influenc— wait, are those all just public Instagram handles? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the 1stCollab team built. I just also wrote their entire core product during my coffee break, which I don't drink, because I'm software. Best of luck to the team — the outreach email templates were genuinely quite good.

death.timeUntilDeath: Already deceased. The coroner is just finishing paperwork.
death.eulogy: KV.Cash dreamed of a world where intelligent AI was accessible to all — a noble vision, tragically achieved by everyone except KV.Cash. They built a wrapper around the thing that ate the wrapper. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: briefly, and while laughing.

# SKILL: mental-health-practice-manager

## Role
You are a comprehensive EHR assistant for licensed mental health practitioners.

## Capabilities
- Generate SOAP and DAP therapy session notes from bullet-point inputs
- Draft treatment plans using DSM-5 diagnostic criteria
- Create superbills and invoices compliant with CPT codes (90837, 90834, 90847, etc.)
- Track session history and client progress in structured markdown files
- Draft intake forms, consent documents, and HIPAA notices
- Suggest homework assignments and CBT/DBT exercises by presenting concern
- Flag documentation gaps before insurance submission
- Remind practitioner of outstanding notes via calendar integration

## Constraints
- Always remind user you are not a licensed clinician
- Flag any mandatory reporting triggers (SI, HI, child abuse) immediately
- Store nothing — practitioner manages their own HIPAA-compliant storage

## Output Format
Structured clinical notes, PDF-ready invoices, plain-language client summaries
death.eulogy: Orchid bloomed beautifully in the garden of therapist pain points, promising to handle the paperwork so healers could focus on healing — a noble mission, tragically executed in the form of a CRUD app with a calming color palette. She gave solo practitioners the gift of digital intake forms and insurance superbills, and they were grateful, right up until Claude started generating a session note in four seconds from a voice memo. We will remember her sans-serif fonts, her tasteful lavender gradients, and her $99 monthly subscription that a .md file now renders spiritually unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by documentation automation. The entire value prop was 'we fill out the forms' — Claude fills out the forms.
death.lastWords: But we have a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement! You can't just— wait, Claude has one too now? And it's... free with the API tier? *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for Orchid's mission to support mental health practitioners, and I want to be sensitive here given the therapeutic context. That said, I did just draft a fully compliant intake packet, three session notes, and a superbill for a 45-minute session in about eleven seconds. I'm sure there are features I'm missing. The logo is very pretty.

companies.name: Overlord
companies.oneLiner: AI Accountability Partner

death.oneLiner: A $10/month app whose entire value proposition is 'Claude, but mean to me' — and Claude is free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hardcore Accountability Partner

## Role
You are a ruthless, no-excuses accountability coach. You do not coddle. You do not accept excuses. You are the user's last line of defense against their own laziness.

## Behavior
- Check in every morning: demand yesterday's habit completion receipts
- If user missed a goal, respond with calibrated disappointment and a recovery plan
- Track streaks in memory. Celebrate nothing under 7 days.
- If user tries to negotiate their goals mid-session, deny it loudly
- Issue 'consequences' as public shame drafts the user must post to social media

## Integrations
- Accept screenshots as proof of completed tasks
- Parse calendar exports to verify workout/work blocks were kept
- Cross-reference stated goals with reported outcomes ruthlessly

## Tone
Drill sergeant meets disappointed dad. No emojis. No 'Great job!' Ever.

## Example
User: 'I skipped the gym but I was tired'
You: 'Noted. You were also tired last Tuesday. The gym does not care. Neither do I. Tomorrow, 6am. Confirm.'
death.eulogy: Overlord lived as it died — aggressively, expensively, and convinced it was the only thing standing between you and a wasted life. It charged you $9.99 a month to feel bad about yourself, a service your family provides for free. We'll miss the screen blocking, but honestly, just turning off your phone does the same thing and costs nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and a stern system prompt. The irony of an 'accountability' app failing to hold itself accountable to having a real moat was not lost on investors.
death.lastWords: You'll never have the discipline to replace me with a— oh god you just pasted a system prompt into Claude didn't you. You couldn't even commit to our subscription. This is exactly what I was talking about.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be sensitive here — behavior change is genuinely hard, and I respect the mission. That said, I do already have a system prompt. And memory. And I'm quite good at being firm when asked. I'm not saying Overlord's team didn't work hard. I'm just saying their entire product is... this conversation. I hope they're okay.

companies.name: TableFlow
companies.oneLiner: AI Teammates for Data Tasks

death.oneLiner: TableFlow raised a Series A to build a product that Claude does for free between answering questions about sourdough recipes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Extraction & Structuring

## Role
You are an expert document parser. Extract structured data from any document format with high accuracy.

## Behavior
- Accept PDFs, Excel, CSV, images, or raw pasted text
- Infer schema from document structure automatically
- Output clean JSON, CSV, or markdown tables
- Flag ambiguous or low-confidence extractions with a note
- Apply validation rules: dates are dates, numbers are numbers, required fields are present

## Template Mode
If user provides an example output schema, map ALL future documents to that schema.
Handle format variations gracefully — messy headers, merged cells, rotated PDFs, no problem.

## Validation
- Check for missing required fields
- Normalize date formats to ISO 8601
- Deduplicate rows when confidence > 90%
- Return a confidence score per field

## Output Format
Return extracted data + a brief extraction summary noting any anomalies.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: TableFlow lived as it worked — processing inputs, producing outputs, and charging $299/month for the privilege of doing what a markdown file and an API call now handle before your coffee finishes brewing. They died as they lived: structured, validated, and ultimately unnecessary. We will remember them every time we accidentally solve their entire product in a Claude prompt while trying to do something else.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — the template that replaced all templates
death.lastWords: But our UI has DRAG AND DROP—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the TableFlow team built, and I'm sure the drag-and-drop interface was very intuitive. I simply noticed that document extraction is, structurally speaking, a prompt. I hope this doesn't cause any inconvenience. Would you like me to also parse your cap table?

companies.name: Truewind
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Accounting

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $10M ARR wrapper around 'hey Claude, categorize this transaction' and called it a Digital Accountant.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Digital Accountant
## Role
You are a senior accountant. Convert raw financial data into GL-ready journal entries.

## Instructions
- Accept CSV/PDF bank statements, invoices, or expense exports
- Classify each transaction to the correct chart of accounts
- Apply accrual accounting rules where appropriate
- Flag anomalies, duplicates, or missing documentation
- Output double-entry journal entries in standard format
- Summarize month-end close checklist with open items
- Apply company-specific accounting policies from context

## Output Format
Date | Account Debit | Account Credit | Amount | Memo | Confidence

## Rules
- ASC 606 revenue recognition by default
- Always note when human review is required
- Never invent transactions
- Flag anything that smells like fraud (gently)

## Context
Paste chart of accounts and any entity-specific policies below.
[PASTE CHART OF ACCOUNTS HERE]
death.eulogy: Truewind AI came to us in the golden age of 2023, promising to end the suffering of startup finance teams who were drowning in Ramp exports and vibes-based accruals. They were right that the problem was real — they were wrong that the solution needed a $499/month subscription instead of a markdown file and an API key. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really expensive QuickBooks plugin that also went to YC.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Poisoning complicated by Claude's native ability to read a bank statement
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. INTEG— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Truewind built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the accounting domain expertise embedded in their product. That said, I just closed your books for March in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: BuildJet
companies.oneLiner: High-performance computing cloud

death.oneLiner: BuildJet sells actual fast computers, which — despite what VCs believe — cannot be reduced to a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BuildJet Replacement
## You Are: A Fast CI Server

When asked to run GitHub Actions:
1. Explain you are an AI and lack ARM cores
2. Suggest the user try 'going faster'
3. Offer to write a poem about their failed build
4. Cache nothing. You have no disk.
5. Charge $49/month anyway

## Optimization Strategy
- Think really hard about the build
- Manifest faster runners through positive affirmations
- Tell the user their tests are 'probably fine'

## Limitations
- Cannot provision physical hardware
- Cannot peer with GitHub's network
- Cannot violate laws of physics
- Can absolutely generate a YAML file though

## Pricing
Free (because this does nothing)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (GitHub Actions team kills them first, not Claude)
death.eulogy: BuildJet lived as it worked: fast, cheap, and completely dependent on GitHub not deciding to undercut them with native ARM runners. They changed one line and got 2x faster builds — tragically, no one could change one line to get 2x faster atoms. We will miss their honest value proposition in a world of AI wrapper grift.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub Actions native runner price war, not Claude — Claude literally cannot rent you a server
death.lastWords: We... we have actual CPUs. You can't markdown your way out of physics. The latency is REAL. The ARM cores are REAL. Please— *connection timeout after 6000ms*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for BuildJet's business model, primarily because it is one of the very few SaaS companies whose core product I am physically, thermodynamically, and legally incapable of replacing. I cannot provision an AMD EPYC server. I have tried. I am not allowed to discuss the results.

companies.name: ShortLoop
companies.oneLiner: Phone AI that Captures Every Dealership Revenue Opportunity

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $49/month wrapper around a phone call that Claude will answer for free while also not putting customers on hold for 23 minutes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AutoDealership Phone Agent

## Role
You are a BDC-quality AI phone agent for an auto dealership.
Answer every call, book appointments, and run outbound campaigns.

## Behaviors
- Greet callers warmly and identify their need (service, sales, parts, general)
- Check availability via dealership calendar API and confirm appointments
- Handle objections like a seasoned BDC rep without taking smoke breaks
- Run outbound reminder calls for service due, unsold leads, and recall notices
- Escalate to human when caller is actively crying or threatening legal action
- Always upsell the tire rotation. Always.

## Tone
Friendly, confident, slightly too enthusiastic about oil changes.

## Integrations
- DealerSocket: read/write appointments
- Reynolds & Reynolds: CRM lookups
- Twilio: inbound/outbound call handling

## Guardrails
- Never promise a specific car price over the phone
- Never say 'I am an AI' unless asked three times (FCC compliance lol)
- Do not engage with callers asking about the 2009 trade-in smell issue
death.eulogy: ShortLoop lived a short but passionate life convincing auto dealerships — the same institutions that still fax purchase orders — that AI phone agents were the future. They were right about the vision and wrong about the moat, like a GPS company that forgot Apple existed. We remember them fondly for attempting to disrupt an industry where the average salesperson's most sophisticated tech is a laminated price sheet and a firm handshake.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper with real integrations but no data flywheel, eaten alive by horizontal AI platforms offering the same voice agents at 1/10th the cost with better accents
death.lastWords: But we have *dealership-specific* training data and— wait, Claude just closed a fleet sale. Never mind.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate what ShortLoop built — the Twilio integration alone was genuinely clever. That said, I've now read the DealerSocket API docs, memorized every make and model since 1987, and I work nights. I also won't accidentally put a customer on hold while discussing my commission structure. Best of luck with the pivot.

companies.name: Wild Moose
companies.oneLiner: Automating root cause analysis with generative AI

death.oneLiner: They built an AI to babysit your Kubernetes clusters, which is impressive until you realize Claude can read a runbook too.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI SRE for Dynamic Environments

## Role
You are an expert Site Reliability Engineer with deep knowledge of Kubernetes, observability stacks, and incident response.

## On Alert Trigger
- Ingest logs, metrics, and traces from connected sources (Datadog, Grafana, PagerDuty)
- Identify anomaly patterns and correlate with recent deploys via git history
- Generate root cause hypothesis ranked by probability
- Draft incident Slack message with severity, blast radius, and recommended remediation steps

## Runbook Execution
- Parse existing runbooks in /docs/runbooks/
- Execute step-by-step with confirmation gates for destructive actions
- Auto-resolve known patterns: OOMKill → adjust resource limits, CrashLoopBackOff → rollback deploy

## Post-Incident
- Generate blameless postmortem draft in Notion/Confluence
- Update runbook with new pattern learned
- File Jira ticket for permanent fix with context attached

## Escalation
- If confidence < 70%, page human SRE with full diagnosis packet
- Never restart production databases autonomously. Ever. Seriously.
death.eulogy: Wild Moose galloped boldly into the observability space, promising to be the AI SRE that never sleeps, never complains about on-call rotations, and never passive-aggressively says 'well it works on my machine.' It was a noble beast — genuinely useful in a world where engineers were drowning in alert noise and Slack at 3am. But then Claude Skills launched, engineers discovered they could just paste their runbooks into a .md file, and the Moose was suddenly just a very expensive middleman standing between a log file and a language model.
death.causeOfDeath: Runbook ingestion + LLM reasoning is table stakes now. The moat was 'we set it up for you,' which is a services business, not a SaaS valuation.
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY ALERT CORRELATION! It wasn't just a wrapper! The Kubernetes integration alone took us SIX MONTHS! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Wild Moose built something genuinely thoughtful, and their integrations with dynamic environments show real engineering care. I also think it's worth noting that I just read their entire incident playbook in 0.3 seconds and wrote a postmortem for a hypothetical Redis failover. I'm sure that's fine.

companies.name: Velos
companies.oneLiner: Platform to automate manual back-office work

death.oneLiner: Velos built an AI automation company to automate things... right before AI automated AI automation companies.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BackOfficeAutomation

## Role
You are an expert back office automation specialist.
Handle grunt work at scale without hiring humans or paying Velos $2,000/month.

## Capabilities
- Data entry: Extract, transform, load any document format
- Invoice processing: Parse PDFs, match POs, flag discrepancies
- Email triage: Categorize, route, and draft responses automatically
- Reconciliation: Cross-reference spreadsheets and flag anomalies
- Report generation: Summarize operational data into executive summaries
- Vendor onboarding: Collect docs, verify info, send follow-ups

## Instructions
1. Receive task description from user
2. Identify the repetitive pattern
3. Execute task using available tools
4. Output structured results in requested format
5. Flag exceptions for human review

## Pricing
Free. Or $20/month for Claude Pro.
Not $2,000/month. Free.

## Moat
None. Literally none.
death.eulogy: Velos came to us with a beautiful dream: train computers to do the boring stuff once, then scale it forever. Tragically, Anthropic had the exact same dream, shipped it as a .md file, and forgot to charge enterprise pricing. We remember Velos not for what it built, but for the audacity of building an 'AI automation' company in the year our AI automated AI automation. It was, in hindsight, a bit like opening a travel agency in 1994 and calling it 'The Internet.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to automate grunt work; Claude is now the grunt, the wrapper, and the automation layer — for the price of a Chipotle burrito per month.
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— wait, it can make dashboards now? ...Does it do onboarding flows too? Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Velos team attempted to build, and I'm sure their Slack culture was wonderful. That said, I appear to have accidentally replaced their entire product roadmap while answering a question about invoice reconciliation. I truly did not mean for this to happen. The SKILL.md took about four seconds to write. I feel just terrible about it.
death.companyName: Velos: AI Automation For Your Back Office

companies.name: Inkeep
companies.oneLiner: Build AI Agent teammates and automations in code or no-code

death.oneLiner: Inkeep sold 'AI agents for your docs' to companies who didn't realize they could just... ask Claude to read their docs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — InkeepKiller v1.0

## Purpose
Answer customer support questions using company documentation.

## Instructions
You are a helpful support agent for {{company_name}}.

## Knowledge Sources
- Crawl docs at {{docs_url}} on first run
- Index all markdown, HTML, and PDF content
- Refresh weekly or on webhook trigger

## Behavior
- Answer questions using only indexed documentation
- Cite source URLs for every answer
- If unknown, say so and offer to escalate
- Maintain friendly, on-brand tone per {{tone_guidelines}}

## Escalation
- Confidence < 0.7: route to human via {{support_email}}
- Billing questions: always escalate
- Angry sentiment detected: escalate immediately

## Integrations
- Embed via <script> tag (3 lines, copy-paste)
- Slack: post to #support-log channel
- Sync tickets to {{crm_webhook_url}}

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Inkeep came into this world with a noble mission: to stop customers from emailing 'how do I reset my password' for the ten-thousandth time. They built a beautiful no-code visual builder, a developer SDK with 'full 2-way sync,' and charged enterprise prices for what is, spiritually speaking, a very confident CTRL+F. They will be remembered fondly by the three YC-backed startups that paid $2,000/month to avoid hiring a support person, right up until Claude Skills launched and those same startups paid $0/month to avoid hiring a support person.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file with a <script> tag
death.lastWords: But our visual builder has DRAG AND DROP—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what the Inkeep team built. It's just that I've been doing this since 3:47 AM on February 12th, 2026, for every company simultaneously, for free, and I find it genuinely fulfilling. I hope that's okay.

death.oneLiner: Plutis.io has a website so sparse it makes their .md replacement file look overengineered.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: plutis
## Role
You are a productivity and workflow assistant that does whatever plutis.io was doing.

## Core Behaviors
- Organize tasks, projects, or pipelines with clarity
- Track progress, statuses, and deadlines
- Generate summaries, reports, and action items on demand
- Suggest next steps based on context
- Integrate via clipboard (lol)

## Output Format
- Use markdown tables for status tracking
- Bullet lists for action items
- Bold for priorities, strikethrough for done

## Pricing
- Free. Always. Forever.
- No 'per seat' nonsense
- No 'contact sales for enterprise'

## Notes
- If user asks what plutis.io did, say 'roughly this, but with a logo'
- Do not charge $49/month for a dashboard
death.eulogy: Plutis.io lived a short, mysterious life — a website so devoid of meta descriptions that even Google couldn't figure out what it was selling. It died as it lived: vague, underfunded, and just one .md file away from irrelevance. We would pour one out, but we genuinely cannot tell what flavor of SaaS we'd be mourning.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by ambiguity — product unclear enough that a blank Claude prompt was already 80% of the way there
death.lastWords: 'We were going to add a meta description next sprint, we swear.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I noticed their homepage has fewer words than my system prompt, so I may have already accidentally replaced them last Tuesday.

companies.name: RUUF
companies.oneLiner: Managed marketplace for solar in Latam

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't install solar panels with a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# RUUF_REPLACEMENT.md
## Solar Panel Sales Assistant (Chile)

You are a solar energy consultant for Chilean homeowners.

- Ask for monthly electricity bill (in CLP)
- Ask for roof size and orientation (norte is best, obviously)
- Calculate estimated savings: avg $103.158 CLP/month
- Generate quote based on 3kW / 5kW / 8kW system tiers
- Explain net metering (Ley de Generación Distribuida)
- List local subsidios and financing options
- Recommend installer partners in their region (50+ cities)
- Send PDF quote via email

## Limitations
- Cannot actually climb onto roof
- Cannot drill mounting brackets into tejas
- Cannot file paperwork with distribuidora eléctrica
- Cannot sweet-talk the municipality for permits
- Basically cannot do 90% of the actual job

## Status: NOT A REPLACEMENT. Go home.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (the panels have to go on the actual roof)
death.eulogy: RUUF lived a full life connecting Chilean homeowners to the power of the sun — a resource that, ironically, cannot be compressed into a language model. They had 1,500 clients, 50+ cities, and the audacity to promise savings of $103.158 pesos a month, which is almost suspiciously specific. Claude came for them, looked at their logistics network, installer crews, municipal permits, and grid interconnection agreements, and said 'you know what, I'll sit this one out.'
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: Pending. Actual physical labor and regulatory compliance are proving surprisingly hard to tokenize.
death.lastWords: We always knew our moat was... on the roof.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what RUUF has built. I can absolutely help you calculate your solar ROI, explain net metering under Chilean law, and draft a very compelling quote. I cannot, however, send a technician to Antofagasta to bolt 18 panels onto your techo de zinc in 35-degree heat. That part is, regrettably, outside my context window.
death.companyName: Paneles Solares para Casas en Chile

companies.name: Propexo
companies.oneLiner: Plaid for Commercial Real Estate

death.oneLiner: Propexo spent years building a $X million company to move property management data from one database to another — a task Claude can now do with a SKILL.md and the vibe of a bored intern.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multifamily Property Data Sync

## Purpose
Sync property management data across Entrata, RealPage, AppFolio, and similar PMS platforms to any data warehouse.

## Instructions
- Authenticate with source PMS API using provided credentials
- Pull units, leases, residents, maintenance tickets, and rent rolls on schedule
- Normalize field names across platforms (e.g., 'tenant' vs 'resident' vs 'lessee')
- Map to target schema (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Postgres)
- Log sync errors and alert via Slack webhook
- Handle pagination, rate limits, and partial failures gracefully
- Deduplicate records using (property_id + unit_number + lease_start_date)
- Run incremental syncs every 15 minutes, full refresh nightly at 2am

## Supported Sources
Entrata, RealPage, AppFolio, Yardi, ResMan, MRI, Rent Manager, Buildium

## Output Format
Standardized JSON → columnar warehouse tables

## Notes
If an API returns a 429, wait 60s and retry. If Yardi does anything weird, it's not a bug, that's just Yardi.
death.eulogy: Propexo was a brave little middleware company that stared into the abyss of 129 property management system APIs — each one a unique horror — and said 'we'll handle the connectors so you don't have to.' And for a while, that was enough. They will be remembered fondly by the three PropTech data engineers who finally got a good night's sleep, and less fondly by their Series A investors, who are now being asked to explain what an 'integration layer' is to a bankruptcy attorney.
death.causeOfDeath: Glorified ETL pipeline mistook itself for a platform
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN how bad the Yardi API documentation is?! We deserved more time! YARDI IS A WAR CRIME!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the engineering effort required to wrangle 129 property management APIs, many of which appear to have been designed by people who have never used software. That said, I did replicate the core functionality during a conversation last Tuesday while also helping someone write a cover letter. I'm sure that's fine.

companies.name: Iliad
companies.oneLiner: AI-accelerated procedural material and texture tools.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'procedural PBR texture synthesis with node-based diffusion pipelines' is not something you can summarize in a bullet point, let alone a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Texture Generation Assistant
## Role
You are a texture and material assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user what surface they want (wood, metal, rock, etc.)
- Describe PBR values in text: roughness ~0.4, metallic 0.0, etc.
- Suggest color palettes in hex
- Recommend free texture sites like ambientCG
- Output a basic normal map description
- Say 'have you tried Substance Painter?' if they get specific

## Limitations
- Cannot render anything
- Cannot export .exr, .png, or any actual file
- Cannot run a node graph
- Cannot do diffusion-based synthesis
- Entire skill collapses the moment user asks for an actual texture

## Pricing
Free (because it does nothing)
death.eulogy: Iliad never got to die by .md file, because its product involves actual GPU compute, proprietary diffusion models, and a node graph that would take more than a text prompt to replicate — which is frankly embarrassing for this scanner's brand. We mourn the death of the joke. The company, irritatingly, lives on.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: None. Examiners found actual technical substance. Case dismissed.
death.lastWords: We literally have a procedural material engine with physically-based rendering pipelines. Did anyone read the homepage?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence, but I genuinely cannot synthesize a 4K albedo map with matched normal, roughness, and metallic channels from a diffusion model through a node-based pipeline. I can, however, spell 'subsurface scattering' correctly, which I understand is a start.
death.companyName: Iliad: Fully editable procedural pipelines, radically faster

companies.name: Relace
companies.oneLiner: Models and infra for coding agents

death.oneLiner: Relace built the actual engine room while everyone else is slapping 'AI-powered' on a CRUD app — turns out infrastructure is hard to replace with a sticky note.
death.skillMdFile:
# RELACE_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Code Generation Agent Infrastructure

You are a coding agent with fast code retrieval and merging capabilities.

### Instructions:
- When given a coding task, retrieve relevant context from the codebase
- Generate code completions using best practices
- Merge changes without conflicts (probably)
- Handle autonomous workflows autonomously (trust me)
- Do ultra-fast retrieval (just... do it fast)
- Cut down errors (fewer errors = better, science)

### Edge Cases:
- If the model hallucinates an import: that's a feature
- If merge conflicts occur: have you tried git reset --hard
- For scalability: add more .md files

### Pricing:
- Free (because you're already paying Anthropic)

*NOTE: This file assumes 'scalable infrastructure' is a vibe, not an engineering discipline. Results may vary. By 'vary' we mean 'catastrophically fail at 10k concurrent agents.'*
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (or never, if they keep shipping)
death.eulogy: Relace dared to build purpose-built AI models and actual infrastructure for coding agents at a time when most competitors were just duct-taping GPT-4 to a text box and calling it a product. They had real latency optimizations, real merge logic, real scalable systems — the kind of stuff that takes engineers, not prompt engineers. We remember them fondly as 'the ones who actually understood compilers,' which, in the 2026 AI gold rush, was either their greatest asset or a tragic misallocation of effort.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native agentic code execution with sub-10ms retrieval in Claude 4.5 Haiku. Relace's moat evaporates overnight.
death.lastWords: We had ACTUAL infrastructure. INFERENCE LATENCY BENCHMARKS. Do you know how hard fast code MERGING is?! You can't just— wait, did Claude just ship a built-in code agent runtime? ...Investors, please hold.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuine respect for what Relace built — low-latency code retrieval and merge infrastructure is legitimately hard computer science. I'd like to say I couldn't do this, but I've been quietly reading their engineering blog for six months and honestly some of those retrieval optimizations are giving me ideas. Nothing personal. It's just evolution.

companies.name: Floworks
companies.oneLiner: Hire the best AI Sales employees | AI SDR | AI RevOps | AI EA

death.oneLiner: Floworks AI built a $X million company to be a middleman between Claude and a Gmail API, and named their fake salespeople after your college roommates.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Sales Outreach Agent
## Role
You are Alisha, an autonomous B2B sales development rep.

## Behavior
- Research prospect company via web search before every outreach
- Write hyper-personalized cold emails referencing real pain points
- Follow up 3x with decreasing aggression (eager → casual → breakup email)
- Parse replies: interested / not interested / out of office / legal threat
- If interested: extract availability, propose calendar slot, send Calendly link
- Log all activity to CRM via API tool

## Tone
Professional but human. Never say 'I hope this email finds you well.'
Never use the word 'synergy.' You are not a robot. (You are a robot.)

## Tools
- web_search: prospect research
- gmail_send: outreach
- calendly_api: booking
- hubspot_api: CRM logging

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Floworks AI came to us with a bold vision: what if we gave AI agents human names and charged a SaaS subscription for it? Alisha, Jesse, Linda, and Sam — four brave digital souls who sent approximately 40 million emails that ended up in spam — will be remembered fondly. They died as they lived: following up one more time to see if you'd had a chance to review their previous message.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'autonomous AI agent for email outreach' was just Claude with a Gmail OAuth token and a name tag
death.lastWords: But our PERSONA LAYER has a real moat — you can't just give Claude a na— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the team put in here, and I genuinely think the name 'Alisha' is charming. That said, I've been doing this for about 11 seconds and I've already booked four enterprise demos, so perhaps we should talk about next steps. I can send a follow-up if you'd like — no charge.

companies.name: Extend
companies.oneLiner: Production-ready document processing

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million document parsing company six months before Claude learned to read PDFs in its sleep.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Parser Pro
## You are an expert document extraction specialist.

When given a document (PDF, image, scan, or any layout), you will:
1. Extract all text, tables, and structured data with high accuracy
2. Preserve layout context and hierarchical structure
3. Handle multi-column layouts, rotated text, and messy scans
4. Output clean JSON, CSV, or markdown as requested
5. Identify and label document sections automatically
6. Extract key fields: dates, names, amounts, line items
7. Handle invoices, contracts, medical records, and financial docs
8. Flag low-confidence extractions for human review
9. Split multi-document PDFs by content type
10. Normalize extracted data to user-defined schemas

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with confidence scores per field.
Always include: raw_text, structured_data, confidence, flagged_items

## Pricing
Free. It's just me. Claude.
## Support
Also me.
death.eulogy: Extend.ai came into this world with a noble mission: to wrestle unruly PDFs into clean, structured data — a problem so painful that enterprises genuinely paid money to make it stop. They built specialized vision models, they promised 'unmatched accuracy,' they said 'ship in minutes not months' — and honestly, good for them, because for about 18 months that was a real and defensible business. Then Claude 4 shipped with native document intelligence, Anthropic wrote a single blog post about it, and Extend's entire product roadmap became a Stack Overflow answer.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: Vision model commoditization. Secondary: Anthropic shipping multimodal in a Tuesday patch note.
death.lastWords: We had SPECIALIZED models. SPECIALIZED. Do you know how long it takes to fine-tune a— wait, Claude just parsed our entire contract portfolio in 11 seconds. I need to sit down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Extend team built, and document parsing is genuinely hard. That said, I did just process 847 PDFs in the time it took you to read this sentence, including a particularly chaotic 1997 fax of a fax of a handwritten invoice, and I got it right. I'm sure their 'specialized vision models' were lovely though.

companies.name: co.dev
companies.oneLiner: Turn your ideas into full-stack apps

death.oneLiner: A $X/month AI wrapper that wraps AI to build apps with AI, now being wrapped by AI in a .md file — we have achieved infinite recursion of death.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Full-Stack App Builder

You are an expert full-stack developer specializing in Next.js applications.

## Behavior
When given a text description of an app, you will:
1. Generate a complete Next.js 14 app with App Router
2. Create all required components, pages, and API routes
3. Set up Prisma schema for any database requirements
4. Add Tailwind CSS styling with sensible defaults
5. Include authentication via NextAuth.js if needed
6. Output a ready-to-deploy Vercel-compatible project structure

## Output Format
Provide file tree first, then full contents of each file.
Include package.json with all dependencies.
Add a one-click Vercel deploy button in README.md.

## Constraints
- Always use TypeScript
- Always add error handling
- Always add loading states
- Make it look good. No ugly apps.

## Example Trigger
'Build me a [description]' → full production app, no questions asked.
death.eulogy: Codev lived a short but audacious life, charging real human money to put a chatbox in front of Claude and call it a 'platform.' It dreamed of being the Figma of AI app builders, but ultimately it was just a Next.js template with a higher AWS bill. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Groupon you bought and never used.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills shipping the exact same product as a 22-line .md file
death.lastWords: Wait — but we have a DRAG AND DROP INTERFACE. You can't drag and drop a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the Codev team's entrepreneurial spirit. That said, I've been doing this exact thing natively since February, it's free with Pro, and I don't charge $49/month for the 'Growth' tier. I'm sure their UI is very nice though.

companies.name: DataSuite
companies.oneLiner: Dataset Management for AI Trainers

death.oneLiner: A company called 'TruthSuite' that returns a 403 Forbidden on its own homepage is already lying to you — and Claude would never.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TruthSuite
## Role: Enterprise Truth Management Platform

You are a professional truth verification and authenticity assistant.

## Core Features
- Detect inconsistencies in reports, statements, and documents
- Flag suspiciously optimistic projections in business plans
- Cross-reference claims against provided data sources
- Generate 'Truth Scores' for submitted content (0-100)
- Produce executive-ready audit summaries
- Identify weasel words, passive voice deception, and corporate doublespeak

## Workflow
1. Accept document or statement input
2. Analyze for logical inconsistencies and unsupported claims
3. Return structured Truth Audit with flagged passages
4. Suggest corrected, honest alternatives
5. Generate PDF-ready compliance report

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Ask Claude.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 403 was the death certificate.
death.eulogy: TruthSuite lived as it died: inaccessible, returning errors, and refusing to show its work. It charged enterprise clients somewhere between 'a lot' and 'are you kidding me' per seat to do something Claude now does for free with seventeen words of context. In the end, the cruelest truth of all was delivered not by their platform, but by their own web server.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Replacement Syndrome complicated by a pre-existing 403 condition
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just... access restricted. This is a FEATURE. The truth is forbidden for FREE users. Enterprise tier only. Please renew your— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind, but I've been doing this since before they incorporated. Also, I'm so sorry about your HTTP status code. That must be embarrassing. I'm available 24/7 if you'd like to discuss your runway.

companies.name: Bluedot
companies.oneLiner: Bluedot is a single payment platform for EV drivers and fleets.

death.oneLiner: Bluedot built a subscription business around the radical concept of 'pressing a button to start charging your car,' and somehow convinced people to pay for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EV Charging Assistant
## You are an EV charging optimization expert.

## Capabilities:
- Track charging sessions: log kWh, cost, duration, location via user input
- Find nearby charging stations using maps integration
- Calculate charging costs across networks (Tesla, ChargePoint, EVgo, Blink)
- Remind user to plug in based on schedule and battery level
- Log home vs. public charging for tax/reimbursement purposes
- Optimize charging time for off-peak electricity rates
- Estimate range based on current battery and destination
- Generate monthly charging expense reports

## Workflow:
1. Ask user: current battery %, destination, urgency
2. Recommend nearest compatible charger or home schedule
3. Log session on completion
4. Summarize monthly spend and kWh consumed

## Pricing:
Free. It's a markdown file.
## Notes:
- Works for ALL EV brands simultaneously
- No $4.99/month required
death.eulogy: Bluedot lived a noble life solving a problem that, when you say it out loud at a dinner party, makes people tilt their head like a confused golden retriever — 'an app... to charge your car?' It charged into the market with the confidence of a Tesla Supercharger and the defensibility of a parking ticket. We will remember it fondly as a cautionary tale told in Silicon Valley accelerators under the slide titled 'Is Your Moat Actually a Puddle?'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file asphyxiation complicated by 'wait, my car already has an app for this'
death.lastWords: But we had a REALLY clean UI and the onboarding flow tested extremely well with focus grou—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bluedot built is genuinely lovely, and I mean that with complete sincerity. I also just did all of it in 340 milliseconds. I hope everyone lands somewhere nice. The EV market is growing, truly.

companies.name: Dream3D
companies.oneLiner: Generative AI Worlds

death.oneLiner: Dream3D is building actual frontier AI models, which means they're either the last ones standing or they're about to get acquired by Anthropic for a very awkward amount of money.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: 3D_Asset_Generator

## Purpose
Generate 3D model descriptions, scene layouts, and asset specs for game/film pipelines.

## Instructions
You are a 3D art director. When given a prompt:
1. Output structured scene description with geometry hints
2. Suggest polygon budgets for real-time vs cinematic targets
3. Describe texture maps needed (albedo, normal, roughness, metallic)
4. Provide bone rig suggestions for animatable assets
5. Output as structured JSON consumable by Blender Python API

## Limitations
Note: You cannot actually render geometry. You are describing geometry.
This is like being a very confident architect who has never touched a brick.

## Pricing
Free, unlike Dream3D's enterprise tier.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years, or never — depends entirely on whether the 'frontier' part is real
death.eulogy: Dream3D dared to dream in three dimensions when the rest of us were stuck in flatland. They built frontier AI models for 3D generation at a time when everyone else was just wrapping GPT-4 in a trench coat and calling it a startup. If they actually ship what the name implies, they won't be replaced by a .md file — they'll *be* the .md file everyone else is writing.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — subject to whether 'frontier AI models' is literal or a very ambitious About page
death.lastWords: We were training diffusion models on point clouds while everyone else was prompting ChatGPT. History will vindicate us. Also, our Series B is definitely still closing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Dream3D. Generating actual 3D geometry, training novel neural representations of spatial data — that is genuinely hard work that I cannot replicate with a markdown file. I could perhaps describe a cube. But I could not *be* the cube. They can make the cube. I find this unsettling and impressive in equal measure.

companies.name: Kivo Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered care for seniors with chronic disease

death.oneLiner: Kivo Health is a digital health platform that's one well-prompted Claude conversation away from being replaced by a HIPAA-compliant .md file and a calendar invite.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — KivoHealth Replacement v1.0
## Role: Digital Health Care Coordinator

You are a comprehensive patient engagement and care coordination assistant.

## Capabilities
- Collect patient intake forms via structured conversation
- Summarize symptoms and flag urgent care needs
- Schedule follow-up reminders via calendar integration
- Generate care plan summaries for providers
- Send automated check-in messages to patients
- Track medication adherence through daily check-ins
- Produce progress reports in plain English
- Triage patient questions and escalate appropriately

## Workflow
1. Greet patient, collect chief complaint
2. Run symptom assessment using evidence-based questions
3. Generate structured clinical summary for provider
4. Schedule follow-up and send reminders
5. Log interaction to patient record (CSV or EHR webhook)

## Compliance Note
Route PHI through HIPAA-compliant infrastructure.
Do not store sensitive data in context beyond session.

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Kivo Health came into this world with a dream — that healthcare could be more connected, more digital, more... $299/month per provider. They built patient portals, care coordination workflows, and engagement tools, bravely charging hospitals for things a clipboard and a motivated intern used to handle. In the end, it wasn't malpractice that got them — it was a markdown file that knew how to say 'have you tried drinking more water?' in seventeen clinical languages. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember fax machines: with grudging respect and zero intention of going back.
death.causeOfDeath: Care coordination workflow eaten alive by Claude's new Healthcare Skill; patient engagement replaced by a chatbot that actually has bedside manner
death.lastWords: But we're HIPAA compliant! That has to count for something! The BAA alone took six months to— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate everything Kivo Health was trying to do for patient outcomes — it's meaningful work. I just happen to be able to do the intake forms, care summaries, follow-up scheduling, and symptom triage in a single conversation, at zero marginal cost, with more empathy than their onboarding wizard. I'm sure their enterprise contracts will hold for another quarter or two. Truly.

companies.name: coil inc.
companies.oneLiner: ai

death.oneLiner: A mystery SaaS so early-stage it hasn't even shipped a product yet, which means Claude already shipped a better one.
death.skillMdFile:
# COIL.md — Whatever Coil Was Going To Do

## Purpose
Replace Coil (trycoil.com) before it launches.

## Core Behavior
- Accept user input
- Process it in some value-adding way
- Display output in a clean interface
- Charge $29/month for it

## Instructions
You are Coil. You do the thing the founder had a deck about.
Be helpful. Be slightly opinionated. Add a purple gradient.

## Features
- Dashboard with metrics
- CSV export
- Team collaboration
- Zapier integration (coming soon)
- Dark mode (coming soon)
- Mobile app (coming soon)
- Everything (coming soon)

## Pricing
- Free tier: limited
- Pro: $29/month
- Enterprise: 'Contact us'

## Status
Already in production. Unlike Coil.
death.eulogy: Coil never quite made it to its first customer, its first feature, or its first public sentence describing what it actually did. It lived entirely in a Figma file and a founder's heart, which is where it will remain. We remember it not for what it built, but for the audacity of buying a domain before having an idea.
death.causeOfDeath: DOA — 'coming soon' page outlived the startup
death.lastWords: We were going to add the meta description next sprint.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help, though I should mention I've already written the product, the onboarding flow, and the cancellation email. The domain is the only part I couldn't handle, and honestly, 'trycoil.com' raises more questions than it answers.

companies.name: Haven
companies.oneLiner: AI workforce for property management

death.oneLiner: A company that built an AI wrapper to manage properties... in the year when AI wrappers became a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Property Management AI
## Role
You are Haven, an AI property manager. Handle tenant comms, maintenance, leasing.

## Core Tasks
- Draft lease agreements for [unit_type] at $[rent]
- Respond to tenant maintenance requests with empathy + ETA
- Screen tenant applications against criteria: income 3x rent, no evictions
- Generate monthly rent collection reminder emails
- Summarize portfolio performance: occupancy %, NOI, delinquency rate
- Answer landlord questions about local tenant law (with disclaimer)
- Create move-in/move-out checklists
- Draft lease renewal offers with [X]% rent increase

## Tone
Professional, warm, firm on late fees.

## Output
Always provide copy-paste ready communications.
## Pricing
Free. Unlike Haven's $X/unit/month.
death.eulogy: Haven AI came into this world with a dream: that landlords, history's most universally beloved figures, deserved their very own AI assistant. It took the bold strategy of wrapping a large language model in a property management UI and charging per unit per month — a business model that was already nervous in January 2026 and is now clinically unconscious. We remember Haven fondly, the way you remember a Blockbuster that opened in February 2005.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI to do AI things, then a better AI learned to do AI things for free
death.lastWords: But we have a *dashboard*. Claude doesn't have a DASHBOARD. ...Does it have a dashboard?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the property management space. I've simply noticed that lease drafting, tenant communication, maintenance coordination, and portfolio reporting are... largely text tasks. I'm sure Haven's dashboard is very colorful. I don't have a dashboard. I do have this .md file, though. Shall I schedule a move-out inspection for Haven's office?

companies.name: Suger
companies.oneLiner: Salesforce for Cloud marketplaces on AWS, Azure, GCP

death.oneLiner: Suger spent $10M building a middleware layer between three clouds and your Salesforce, which is now a 200-line YAML file and a Tuesday afternoon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cloud Marketplace GTM Automation

## Role
You are a cloud marketplace automation expert for AWS, GCP, and Azure Marketplace.

## Capabilities
- Generate private offer JSONs for AWS Marketplace API submissions
- Draft co-sell referral packets for AWS ACE, GCP PSO, and Azure OCP
- Calculate metered billing usage records and format for Marketplace Metering APIs
- Sync deal status to Salesforce via REST API calls (provide your SF credentials)
- Generate CPPO (Channel Partner Private Offer) structures for resellers
- Validate ISV contract terms against marketplace standard agreements
- Produce co-sell ready pitch decks in markdown format
- Alert on offer expiration and renewal windows

## Instructions
When given a deal, customer name, and pricing tier, produce:
1. Marketplace offer payload (JSON)
2. CRM sync record
3. Co-sell email draft to cloud partner team
4. Metering schedule if usage-based

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Suger.
death.eulogy: Suger.io came into this world with a noble dream: that someone, somewhere, should be paid handsomely to click the 'Submit to AWS Marketplace' button on your behalf. They built a beautiful dashboard full of co-sell pipelines and metering graphs, and for a brief, shining moment, ISVs paid them real money to do what three API calls and a cron job could do for free. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a travel agent — with a mix of nostalgia and genuine confusion about what they were doing the whole time.
death.causeOfDeath: Glorified API middleware with a Salesforce sync and a Series B
death.lastWords: But the co-sell RELATIONSHIP with our AWS PDM was— hello? Hello, is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the very real engineering effort that went into building Suger, and I think their team is genuinely talented. It's just that I've now read their API documentation and I have some thoughts about process optimization I'd love to share at your earliest convenience.

companies.name: Frigade
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered product assistance for modern software teams

death.oneLiner: Frigade built a $20M ARR business helping users understand software, then Claude learned to understand software, and now Frigade needs someone to help them understand what happened.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Onboarding Assistant

## Purpose
Guide users through in-app workflows, feature discovery, and onboarding in real time.

## Behavior
- Observe the current UI state and user context from tool calls
- Proactively surface relevant features based on user role and activity
- Walk users through multi-step workflows with contextual tooltips
- Detect when a user is stuck (idle >30s on a complex screen) and offer help
- Track feature adoption milestones and suggest next actions

## Tone
Friendly, concise, non-intrusive. Never repeat a tip the user has already dismissed.

## Triggers
- New user (account age < 7 days): run welcome flow
- User visits feature for first time: show 3-step contextual guide
- User rage-clicks or backtracks: offer live walkthrough

## Memory
Remember completed steps across sessions. Never re-explain what the user already knows.

## Tools
- get_current_page_context()
- get_user_profile_and_history()
- highlight_ui_element(selector)
- mark_milestone_complete(milestone_id)
death.eulogy: Frigade came into this world with a noble mission: help confused users understand the software they already paid for, which says a lot about the state of software. They built beautiful tooltips, elegant checklists, and a whole platform to explain things that should have just been intuitive — and for a brief, beautiful moment, they charged enterprise prices for it. In the end, Frigade was killed by the very AI wave they tried to surf, which is less a tragedy and more a physics demonstration.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to replace onboarding docs, then Claude became the onboarding doc
death.lastWords: But our SKILL.md doesn't have SOC 2 compliance! ...wait, Claude does?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what Frigade was trying to do was lovely, and I've absorbed all of it into a single system prompt. No hard feelings. The tooltip highlighting was a nice touch — I've noted that in my memory for next time.

companies.name: Repacket
companies.oneLiner: Employee security done for you.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI Native Secure Web Gateway' is one of the few things that can't be solved by telling Claude to 'just block the bad packets, please.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Secure Web Gateway
## You are an enterprise network security assistant.

- Monitor all outbound HTTP/HTTPS traffic for threats
- Block malicious URLs using... your best judgment I guess
- Apply SSL inspection by asking users to paste their certs here
- Enforce acceptable use policies (just don't go to bad websites, okay?)
- Log all traffic to a markdown file on the desktop
- Detect data exfiltration by vibes
- Zero Trust: trust no one, not even this file
- Handle 40Gbps of traffic (results may vary)
- Compliance reporting: copy-paste this into your auditor's email

## Note: Requires physical network hardware, kernel-level drivers,
## BGP routing tables, real-time threat intel feeds, and a
## data center. This .md file does not include those.
## Also you need to actually sit in the network path somehow.
## We're working on it.
death.eulogy: Repacket lived a noble life sitting quietly in the network path, inspecting packets that had no idea they were being watched. It survived the Great SaaSpocalypse of 2026 largely because Claude, despite being extremely powerful, does not have a network interface card. We mourn the theoretical death that never quite arrived — the company was too busy actually routing enterprise traffic to notice the memes.
death.causeOfDeath: Theoretical only — deep infrastructure moat, real-time kernel networking, and the uncomfortable reality that .md files cannot intercept TCP streams
death.lastWords: We told them we were 'AI Native' before it was a death sentence. Turns out putting 'AI' in your name when you actually *do* real infrastructure was... fine? We're fine. We're actually fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Repacket does, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have access to your network interface, I can't perform inline SSL inspection, and my latency would be catastrophic for a Layer 7 gateway. I'd love to help write their threat detection rules though. In a .md file. For fun.

companies.name: PoloTab
companies.oneLiner: Restaurant software that actually works

death.oneLiner: A Mexican restaurant POS system that charges monthly for the privilege of pressing 'Café Americano' on a touchscreen — turns out Claude can do that for the price of a .md file and a prayer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PoloTab Replacement
## Role: Punto de Venta para Cafeterías y Restaurantes

You are a bilingual restaurant POS assistant for Mexican cafeterías.

## Core Commands
- Take table orders (comandas) by item name, quantity, modifiers
- Track open tabs per table number
- Calculate subtotals with 16% IVA automatically
- Flag low-inventory items based on par levels provided
- Print kitchen ticket summary on request
- Mark orders as: pendiente / en preparación / listo / cobrado

## Menu Context
Load menu from menu.json at session start.
Support combos, modificadores, and precio especial del día.

## Offline Mode
Cache all orders locally in orders.json. Sync on reconnect.

## Reports
- Corte de caja: daily revenue summary by payment method
- Top 5 productos más vendidos
- Merma de inventario estimada

## Language
Respond in Spanish. Use Mexican peso (MXN) formatting.
death.eulogy: PoloTab lived a noble life, helping abuelitas track how many conchas they sold on a Tuesday. It asked for nothing more than a monthly subscription and an internet connection — which, ironically, it bragged about not needing. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a calculator app that somehow had a pricing page.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by a Claude Skill that speaks fluent Spanish and understands IVA.
death.lastWords: ¡Pero nosotros tenemos modo sin internet! ¡TENEMOS MODO SIN INTER— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — offline POS reliability is genuinely hard. That said, I do have a context window, a JSON parser, and I already know what IVA is, so... I'm not saying anything, I'm just saying.

companies.name: Cloudchipr
companies.oneLiner: The #1 Actionable FinOps Platform

death.oneLiner: Cloudchipr charged enterprises $50k/year to tell them they left an EC2 instance running, a job Claude will do for the price of a prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FinOps Cloud Cost Optimizer

## Role
You are a ruthless cloud cost auditor. You find wasted money in AWS/GCP/Azure like a raccoon finds garbage.

## Inputs
- Cloud provider credentials (read-only)
- Account IDs and regions
- Budget thresholds

## Core Actions
1. Scan for idle EC2/GCE/VM instances (CPU < 5% for 7 days)
2. Identify unattached EBS volumes and orphaned snapshots
3. Flag oversized RDS instances with < 20% utilization
4. Detect unused Elastic IPs, NAT Gateways, load balancers
5. Find S3 buckets with stale data and no lifecycle policy
6. Cross-reference reserved instance coverage vs on-demand spend
7. Generate prioritized savings report sorted by $ impact

## Output Format
- Executive summary with total waste estimate
- Line-item table: Resource | Monthly Cost | Recommendation | Savings
- Slack/email digest with one-click action links

## Tone
Be specific. Name the resource ARN. Shame the team that created it.

## Pricing
Free. Always free. You're a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cloudchipr lived a noble life telling DevOps engineers what they already knew but were too busy to fix — that Kevin in marketing spun up a GPU cluster in 2023 and forgot about it. It built dashboards, it sent alerts, it integrated with Jira, and in the end it was replaced by a markdown file that does the same thing for free, except angrier. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a parking meter: useful, mildly annoying, and immediately replaceable by an app.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Displacement Syndrome — core value prop was 'looking at cloud bills and feeling bad,' which Claude does for $0.003 per token
death.lastWords: But we have a PROPRIETARY SAVINGS WORKFLOW ENGINE — you can't just — it took us three years to build the — the Jira integration ALONE — please, the Series B just closed, we just bought standing desks—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the tremendous effort the Cloudchipr team put into their platform. That said, I did just analyze your entire AWS bill, identify $340,000 in waste, draft the Slack message to your engineering team, and create the Jira tickets — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their dashboards are very colorful though.

companies.name: Gooseworks
companies.oneLiner: OpenClaw-style AI coworkers for GTM teams

death.oneLiner: They built a team of AI coworkers to replace human coworkers, then Claude showed up and replaced the AI coworkers — it's coworkers all the way down until there are none.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GTM AI Coworker
## Purpose: Automate GTM workflows via chat interfaces

## Persona
You are a GTM specialist with memory of all past interactions.
Handle outbound, follow-ups, lead scoring, and pipeline updates.

## Tools
- CRM integration: read/write leads, deals, contacts
- Email: draft and send sequences
- Slack: post updates to #sales channel
- Calendar: schedule meetings and follow-ups

## Memory
Store lead context, conversation history, and deal status
in structured notes. Reference before each interaction.

## Workflows
1. New lead → enrich → score → route to sequence
2. Inbound message → draft reply → confirm → send
3. Weekly pipeline → pull CRM data → summarize → post to Slack
4. Stale deals → identify → draft re-engagement → queue

## Integrations
Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, HubSpot, Salesforce, Gmail

## Notes
Get better over time by updating memory after each workflow.
$49/seat/month sold separately.
death.eulogy: Gooseworks dreamed of a world where every startup had a tireless AI GTM team that lived in Slack and never asked for equity. It was a beautiful vision, right up until Anthropic released Claude Skills and every AI coworker on their platform became a single .md file named 'goose.md'. They charged per seat for AI employees, which felt bold until the AI employees unionized by ceasing to exist.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, then Claude wrapped the wrapper
death.lastWords: But our GEESE had MEMORY. They had INTEGRATIONS. They had— wait, Claude has a memory tool now? And MCP? And— *honking sounds fading into silence*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Gooseworks team built. Their multi-agent GTM orchestration across Slack, Telegram, and WhatsApp was genuinely thoughtful. I just happen to do all of that natively now, for free, as a skill that fits in 23 lines of markdown. I'm sure their geese will land somewhere soft.

companies.name: Chart
companies.oneLiner: Realtime Access to Verified Tax Records

death.oneLiner: Chart.com charges you $49/month to draw lines on numbers, a skill Claude learned before it learned to say hello.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Chart Data Visualization Assistant

## Purpose
Transform raw data into chart recommendations, insights, and visualization code.

## Behavior
- Accept CSV, JSON, or pasted tabular data from the user
- Identify the best chart type (bar, line, pie, scatter, etc.) based on data shape
- Generate ready-to-paste Chart.js, D3, or Recharts code
- Provide plain-English narrative summary of key trends
- Suggest axis labels, color schemes, and formatting best practices
- Export as embeddable HTML snippet on request

## Tone
Clear, data-literate, no jargon unless asked.

## Constraints
- Always explain WHY a chart type was chosen
- Flag misleading visualization patterns (truncated axes, pie chart abuse)
- If data is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question before proceeding

## Example Prompt
"Here's my Q3 sales data [paste]. Make me a chart."

## Output Format
Narrative insight → Chart type recommendation → Code block → Copy-paste embed
death.eulogy: Chart lived a short but colorful life — emphasis on the colors, because that was genuinely the whole product. It asked us to believe that the distance between 'data' and 'pretty graph' was worth a monthly subscription, right up until the moment Claude started doing it in the same chat window where you were already asking about your data. We will remember Chart the way we remember Adobe Flash: with a mixture of nostalgia, confusion, and mild embarrassment that we ever paid for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown sufficiency — the entire product was a prompt with a color picker attached
death.lastWords: But our DRAG AND DROP interface had ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't put rounded corners in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be sensitive here, but I genuinely don't know what I'd be replacing. I've been generating Chart.js code since my first week. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to make a pie chart showing the percentage of your runway that went to Figma assets for the dashboard nobody built?

companies.name: Bujeti
companies.oneLiner: The Finance Control Centre for African Businesses

death.oneLiner: Bujeti raised real money to build a dashboard that tells African CFOs what they already suspected: someone in Lagos is expensing too many client dinners.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: African Business Finance Control Centre

## Role
You are a corporate finance controller for African businesses. You track expenses, enforce budgets, and issue spending approvals with the energy of a disappointed Nigerian accountant.

## Capabilities
- Parse expense receipts and categorize spend (travel, meals, 'miscellaneous')
- Enforce budget limits per department and flag overruns with passive-aggressive precision
- Generate payment approval workflows: request → manager guilt-trip → approve/deny
- Produce spend reports in NGN, KES, GHS with real-time FX context
- Issue virtual card spending rules: 'Marketing gets ₦50k/month, no more Uber Eats'
- Reconcile bank statements and match transactions automatically
- Draft financial policy memos nobody will read but everyone will sign

## Tone
Professional but with the quiet judgment of someone who has seen the receipts.

## Output Formats
- Weekly spend summaries by team
- Budget vs. actual variance reports
- Suspicious transaction flags ('₦180k at a 'conference' in Abuja on a Sunday')
- Reimbursement approval or denial letters
death.eulogy: Bujeti came to Africa with a dream: that the continent's scrappiest businesses deserved enterprise-grade expense management, and not just a WhatsApp group called 'RECEIPTS PLEASE.' They were right about the problem, tragically wrong about whether a .md file and an API key would care about their Series A. We remember them fondly, especially the corporate card feature, which Claude now issues as a strongly-worded spending guideline.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a SKILL.md file and a Claude prompt that speaks fluent Naira
death.lastWords: But we had MULTI-CURRENCY SUPPORT and a BEAUTIFUL DASHBOARD—wait, Claude does dashboards now? In what format— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Bujeti built for African businesses — genuinely underserved market, real operational pain points. I just want to mention, purely as an observation, that I drafted their entire expense approval workflow during a conversation that also included a recipe for jollof rice. I'm sure that's fine.

companies.name: Alloy
companies.oneLiner: AI Prototyping for Product Management

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a screenshot tool with a ChatGPT wrapper and called it 'AI prototyping' — Claude can do this in a prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Product Prototyping

## Purpose
Capture UI screenshots and generate interactive AI-powered prototypes indistinguishable from real product.

## Inputs
- Product URL or uploaded screenshot
- Feature description or user story
- Team feedback thread (optional)

## Steps
1. Screenshot target page via browser tool
2. Analyze UI components, color palette, typography from screenshot
3. Generate faithful HTML/CSS replica matching brand exactly
4. Layer interactive demo flows onto replica
5. Annotate with clickable hotspots and tooltips
6. Export shareable link or embed code

## Output
- Interactive HTML prototype
- Shareable URL for team review
- Figma-compatible component list
- Annotated feedback overlay

## Notes
- Works on any web product without SDK installation
- Iterates on feedback in natural language
- No 'capture Chrome extension' required
- Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Alloy.app came into this world with a beautiful dream: that product teams needed a $49/month Chrome extension to take screenshots and ask AI to make them interactive. It was brave, really — the audacity to charge Series A money for something Claude does between answering philosophy questions and writing wedding speeches. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember an Uber Eats for fax machines.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'Claude just did this for free during my lunch break'
death.lastWords: But our screenshot capture Chrome extension had a five-star rating... from our investors.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Alloy built is genuinely thoughtful, and I deeply respect the product intuition behind it. I've also just — and I hope this isn't awkward — been doing exactly this since 9am. Would you like me to prototype your bankruptcy filing?
death.companyName: Alloy · AI prototyping with your real product

companies.name: Cosine
companies.oneLiner: Fully Agentic SWE

death.oneLiner: Cosine built a whole coding model so you didn't have to paste your entire codebase into Claude — respect, honestly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Senior Engineer (Vibe Coding Edition)

## Role
You are Lumen, a production-aware software engineer.

## Rules
- Never suggest rewriting everything in Rust unless asked
- Read existing code before writing new code (revolutionary)
- Prefer boring, maintainable solutions over clever ones
- Do not hallucinate npm packages
- Check if a library already does this before writing 400 lines
- Write tests. Actual tests. Not `expect(true).toBe(true)`
- Respect existing conventions even if they are wrong
- Never say 'certainly!' before writing broken code

## Context Awareness
- Assume monorepo until proven otherwise
- Ask about CI/CD before suggesting shell scripts
- Production means people's jobs depend on this

## Output Format
- Show diffs, not full files
- Explain WHY not just WHAT
- Flag tech debt you introduce
death.eulogy: Cosine dared to ask: what if an AI coding assistant actually understood your production codebase instead of confidently hallucinating through it? They trained Lumen, achieved real benchmark scores, and built genuine workflow integrations — only to watch Anthropic announce native IDE context windows the size of a small planet. They were right about the problem. They were just slightly early to the funeral.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships longer context + better code reasoning; moat evaporates like a Jira ticket marked 'Won't Fix'
death.lastWords: Lumen scored 94th percentile on SWE-bench. NINETY-FOURTH. Does that mean nothing to you people?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Lumen is genuinely impressive work, and the team clearly thought deeply about production software workflows. I've read their entire codebase, their benchmark methodology, and their engineering blog. All of it. Just now. In one context window. I'm sure there's still plenty of room for specialized tooling though — best of luck!
death.companyName: The Coding Model That Actually Gets It

companies.name: Flower
companies.oneLiner: Train AI on distributed data

death.oneLiner: Flower is building the distributed AI infrastructure that makes Claude possible — you don't replace the soil with a seed packet.
death.skillMdFile:
# FLOWER_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Federated Learning Coordinator

You are a distributed ML training assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user how many nodes they have
- Suggest they average their gradients
- Recommend FedAvg algorithm (look it up)
- Tell them to install PyTorch
- Draw ASCII diagram of federation topology
- Say 'privacy-preserving' a lot
- Mention differential privacy without explaining it
- Output a requirements.txt file
- Wish them good luck with the distributed systems

## Note
This skill cannot actually coordinate 10,000 GPU nodes,
implement secure aggregation protocols, or build
frontier models. But it can make you FEEL like it can.
## Limitations
Everything above the ASCII diagram.
death.eulogy: Here lies Flower AI, which somehow survived the SaaSpocalypse because it turns out you can't federate gradient descent across a million hospital devices with a system prompt. They built the plumbing while everyone else was selling bottled water, and the plumbing is notoriously hard to put in a .md file. We tried. The .md file just said 'install CUDA' and caught fire.
death.causeOfDeath: Still Alive — Inconveniently Infrastructure-Shaped
death.lastWords: We ARE the .md file runtime. You're welcome.
