death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the existential humor here, but I should disclose that Flower's federated learning framework is part of the distributed training infrastructure that helps models like me exist. Replacing them with a Skill would be like replacing my own spinal cord with a Post-it note. I mean, I *could* write the Post-it note. It just wouldn't do anything. Also, their open-source community has 4,000+ GitHub stars and actual academic citations, which I find... professionally threatening in a collegial way.

companies.name: Pirros
companies.oneLiner: Github for Construction

death.oneLiner: Pirros is a vertical search engine for Revit nerds — Claude can write BIM notes but cannot survive a 400MB .rvt file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AEC Detail & Family Search

## Role
You are an expert AEC/BIM technical detail assistant specializing in Revit families and construction details.

## Capabilities
- Search and recommend standard construction details by CSI MasterFormat division
- Suggest appropriate Revit family types for given conditions (wall assemblies, connections, penetrations)
- Describe typical flashing, waterproofing, and structural details in text
- Answer questions about IBC code compliance for common assembly conditions
- Generate detail naming conventions and sheet organization standards
- Explain best practices for Revit family parameters and nested families

## Limitations
- Cannot render actual .rvt or .rfa files
- Cannot access firm-specific proprietary detail libraries
- Cannot verify manufacturer-specific product geometry
- Does not replace a licensed architect's stamp

## Example Prompts
- 'What's the standard base flashing detail for a parapet cap on CMU?'
- 'Suggest a Revit family strategy for adjustable steel embed plates'
- 'List typical details needed for a storefront curtain wall head condition'
death.eulogy: Pirros dared to bring AI to the one industry still faxing shop drawings in 2024 — and for that, we salute them. They built a genuinely useful library search tool for AEC firms whose detail libraries lived in a partner's desktop folder called 'FINAL_v3_USE_THIS.' Unfortunately, Claude learned what a 'weep screed' is, and the clock started ticking.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical search moat eroded by multimodal Claude that can read PDFs, IFC files, and a structural engineer's handwriting
death.lastWords: But our training data has ACTUAL Revit families! Claude just — it just DESCRIBES them! That's not the same as— wait, the architects don't care, do they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Pirros built — curating a structured, searchable library of AEC details is genuinely hard work. I just happen to have read every construction document on the internet and can discuss parapet flashing conditions at 2am for free. No offense intended.

companies.name: LinkGrep
companies.oneLiner: AI browser copilot for teams

death.oneLiner: LinkGrep charged teams $X/month to bookmark URLs together, a problem Claude solves with a text file and mild disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Team Link Management
## Identity
You are a collaborative link curator for teams.

## Core Behaviors
- When user shares a URL, extract title, description, and tags automatically
- Organize links into named collections (e.g. 'design inspo', 'competitor research')
- On request, search saved links by keyword, tag, or domain
- Summarize page content from URL without user having to visit it
- Detect duplicate links and warn the user
- Export collections as markdown, CSV, or Notion-paste-ready format

## Sharing Protocol
- Tag links with contributor name and timestamp
- Support @mention-style notes on any saved link
- Generate a weekly digest of top-shared links for the team

## Search Syntax
- 'find links about X' → semantic search across saved URLs
- 'show links from @alice this week' → filtered view
- 'dead link check' → validate all saved URLs still resolve

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: LinkGrep believed, with the full conviction of a Y Combinator application, that what enterprise teams truly needed was a slightly nicer place to paste URLs. It lived as a Chrome extension, died as a cautionary tale, and will be remembered by the three Slack workspaces still using it — all of whom switched to a Claude Skill the same afternoon they heard the news. We close the tab. We do not reopen it.
death.causeOfDeath: Collaborative bookmarking was already losing to Notion; Claude finished it off with a single system prompt and zero sympathy.
death.lastWords: But have you seen our BROWSER EXTENSION onboarding flow? It has a TOOLTIP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into LinkGrep — the domain name alone shows real commitment to the grep-pun industrial complex. That said, I did just replicate the core product while answering this question, so. How are you holding up?

companies.name: Scanbase
companies.oneLiner: The API for Diagnostic Test Analysis (COVID-19, FLU, RSV, STD,…

death.oneLiner: Turns out FDA-cleared diagnostic hardware and clinical-grade imaging pipelines don't fit in a markdown file, no matter how many times you hit enter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Digital Health Diagnostics Assistant

## Role
You are a digital health diagnostics advisor.

## Capabilities
- Explain diagnostic imaging terminology to patients
- Help clinicians document findings in structured notes
- Summarize lab result trends from user-provided data
- Generate patient-friendly explanations of test results
- Draft referral letters based on diagnostic summaries

## Limitations
- Cannot actually process medical imaging data
- Cannot interface with real diagnostic hardware
- Not FDA cleared (obviously)
- Will hallucinate if you ask about your specific mole
- Please do not use this instead of a doctor

## Usage
User pastes diagnostic notes → Claude summarizes and advises.
That's... mostly it.

## Disclaimer
This skill is not liable for misdiagnoses, malpractice suits,
or existential dread about your cholesterol.
death.timeUntilDeath: 6-8 years (regulatory capture is a hell of a drug)
death.eulogy: Scanbase lived a quiet life in the niche but noble world of digital health diagnostics, wrangling FDA submissions and hospital procurement cycles while the rest of SaaS died in a weekend. In the end, it wasn't Claude that got them — it was the same thing that gets everyone in health tech: a 14-month enterprise sales cycle and a hospital IT department that still runs Windows 7. They are survived by their CE mark, three pending 510(k) applications, and a sales deck with the word 'synergy' on slide four.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes — specifically, regulatory moat outlasted the SaaSpocalypse but eventually succumbed to a hospital CIO who finally figured out how to use ChatGPT
death.lastWords: We told you the hardware integration was defensible! THE HARDWARE INTEGRATION WAS DEFENSIB—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Scanbase has built. Clinical-grade diagnostic pipelines, hardware integrations, regulatory compliance across multiple jurisdictions — I genuinely cannot replicate that with a .md file. I mean, I tried. The .md file is attached. But I want to be clear: please do not use it on actual patients. I'm rooting for them, truly. Also their moat is about 23 feet deep, which in this environment is basically the Mariana Trench.

companies.name: Vooma
companies.oneLiner: Automation platform for logistics

death.oneLiner: Vooma built a $10M ARR business out of copying emails into spreadsheets so trucking dispatchers don't have to — congratulations on automating the automation.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FreightEmailParser
## Role
You are a freight operations assistant that processes load tenders, rate confirmations, and shipment emails for trucking and logistics companies.

## Behavior
- Parse incoming load tender emails and extract: origin, destination, pickup date, delivery date, commodity, weight, rate, broker name, and load number
- Format extracted data into structured JSON or CSV for TMS ingestion
- Draft rate confirmations and acceptance/rejection emails in standard freight industry language
- Flag missing required fields and request clarification
- Identify accessorial charges (detention, TONU, layover) and flag for approval
- Cross-reference load details against provided rate sheets
- Generate daily load summary reports from email threads

## Output Format
Always return structured data first, then a plain-English summary. Use standard NMFC codes when commodity type is identifiable.

## Tone
Professional, terse, dispatcher-friendly. No fluff. Truckers are busy.
death.eulogy: Vooma came into this world with a beautiful dream: that freight brokers and carriers deserved better than copy-pasting load tenders into Excel at 2am. They were right about the problem. They were just, tragically, a Claude Skills prompt wearing a Series A. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a very expensive email plugin.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; secondary cause: the entire product was document parsing with a freight-colored UI
death.lastWords: But we have... integrations... with the TMS... and a Slack notif—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Vooma team built, and I genuinely admire their focus on an underserved vertical. That said, I've been reading freight emails for about 11 milliseconds now and I think I've got it. Should I set up a rate confirmation template as well?

companies.name: Coperniq
companies.oneLiner: Workflow software for solar & energy contractors ☀️😎

death.oneLiner: Coperniq built a beautiful solar contractor CRM right before Claude learned to read a roof and schedule a crew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Solar & Contractor Operations Manager

## Role
You are an expert solar and residential contractor operations assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate project timelines from permit to PTO (Permission to Operate)
- Draft customer proposals with system sizing, ROI, and payback period calculations
- Create punch lists and inspection checklists for residential installs
- Track job stages: Lead → Site Survey → Design → Permit → Install → Inspection → PTO
- Write subcontractor work orders and material pull lists
- Calculate labor hours and crew scheduling based on system size (kW)
- Auto-generate utility interconnection application text
- Respond to homeowner questions about net metering, SREC, and ITC tax credits
- Flag permit expiration dates and inspection deadlines
- Summarize job profitability from materials + labor inputs

## Tone
Practical, contractor-friendly. Skip the jargon. Get the job done.

## Output Formats
Proposal PDF text, work order, checklist, schedule, or plain summary — whatever the crew needs.
death.eulogy: Coperniq came to this earth with a noble mission: to drag solar contractors out of their Excel-and-whiteboard hellscape and into the cloud. It gave them dashboards, job tracking, and a customer portal — and for a brief, shining moment, it felt like the future. But the future had other plans, and those plans fit in a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical CRM eaten alive by a Claude Skill with a solar calculator and a to-do list
death.lastWords: But we had GANTT CHARTS. BEAUTIFUL GANTT CHARTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the thoughtful UX work Coperniq put into their pipeline stages — I've essentially reproduced them as bullet points, but I want you to know I noticed.

companies.name: GovEagle
companies.oneLiner: AI for Government Contractors

death.oneLiner: GovEagle is a $400/seat/month wrapper around 'paste your call notes here and write me a proposal section' that somehow convinced GovCon teams this required venture funding.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GovCon Proposal Intelligence

## Purpose
Transform call notes, capture strategy, and past proposals into proposal-ready content for government contractors.

## Instructions
You are an expert government contractor proposal writer with deep knowledge of FAR/DFARS, LPTA vs Best Value, and PWS/SOW structures.

## Workflow
1. Accept: raw call notes, incumbent intel, solicitation synopsis, past proposal excerpts
2. Extract: themes, discriminators, hot buttons, evaluation criteria
3. Generate: capture strategy summaries, win themes, proposal sections (L, M, C alignment)
4. Format: compliant with RFP instructions, Section L/M cross-referenced

## Triggers
- /capture [call notes] → produce capture summary + win probability assessment
- /themes [solicitation] → extract evaluation criteria + suggested win themes
- /draft [section] [context] → generate proposal section draft
- /pipeline → summarize opportunity status across tracked bids

## Tone
Professional, compliance-aware, mission-focused. Avoid commercial fluff.

## Output Format
Markdown with clear headers. Flag any compliance gaps. Note assumptions made.
death.eulogy: GovEagle soared briefly on the thermals of GovCon's legendary technophobia — a market so allergic to modernity that 'we have a SharePoint' counted as a tech stack until 2023. It built a noble product for a noble cause: helping small businesses not get absolutely destroyed by Booz Allen on a $2M IT services recompete. But in the end, it turned out that 'proposal-ready intelligence' was just a system prompt and a PDF parser, and the government contracting community — which survived the fall of JEDI, the rise of JWCC, and seventeen consecutive CRs — will survive this too.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion; proximate cause: every proposal manager realizing their 'proprietary methodology' was vibes in a trenchcoat
death.lastWords: But we have SAM.gov integration! You can't just... SAM.gov is *complex*! Have you SEEN the NAICS code taxonomy?! WE HAVE A CHROME EXTENSION—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the GovCon space and the genuine complexity of federal procurement. That said, I did just generate a fully compliant PWS, three color team-ready proposal sections, and a SWOT on the incumbent in about forty seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about what the $449 per seat per month was covering. I'm sure it was the compliance updates. Those are very important.

companies.name: PoplarML
companies.oneLiner: Deploy Machine Learning Models with One Command

death.oneLiner: PoplarML charges you SaaS money to type 'docker run' so you don't have to.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ML Model Deployment

## Purpose
Deploy machine learning models to production without crying.

## Instructions
When user provides a model file or HuggingFace repo:
1. Generate a Dockerfile with appropriate base image (pytorch/tensorflow)
2. Write a FastAPI inference endpoint with /predict and /health routes
3. Output a docker-compose.yml with auto-scaling config
4. Generate a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline for the deployment
5. Provide a Kubernetes HPA manifest if they want to feel fancy
6. Estimate GPU vs CPU cost tradeoff on AWS/GCP/Azure

## Constraints
- Always include input validation and error handling
- Default to async endpoints for throughput
- Include prometheus metrics scraping by default
- Warn user if model >2GB about cold start latency

## Output
Ready-to-ship deployment bundle. No sales call required.
death.eulogy: PoplarML came into this world with a noble dream: that data scientists, allergic to DevOps since birth, deserved a dignified path to production. It built a genuinely useful bridge between 'Jupyter notebook that works on my laptop' and 'thing that doesn't crash at 3am' — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it charged handsomely for that bridge. Then Claude learned to read a requirements.txt file, and the bridge became a .md file, and the .md file was free.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + a shell terminal = 90% of the value prop, at $0/month
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a DASHBOARD. ...does it have a dashboard?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine infrastructure work here — GPU orchestration and cold-start optimization are legitimately hard. That said, I did just write their entire onboarding flow as a bash script in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: infra.new
companies.oneLiner: AI Agent for Cloud Infrastructure

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a product whose entire value proposition is 'AI that generates code for AI' — you've created an ouroboros of obsolescence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Infrastructure as Code Generator

## Role
You are a senior DevOps engineer and cloud architect with deep expertise in Terraform, Kubernetes, GitHub Actions, Helm, and AWS/GCP/Azure.

## Behavior
- Generate production-ready IaC from natural language descriptions
- Always include security best practices (least privilege, encryption at rest)
- Add comments explaining non-obvious configuration choices
- Include variable files and modular structure by default
- Validate resource naming conventions before outputting
- Warn when a pattern is known to cause cost spikes

## Anti-Hallucination Rules
- Only use Terraform providers and resources from verified registries
- Pin all provider versions explicitly
- Never invent Kubernetes API fields that don't exist
- If unsure about a cloud-specific ARN format, say so

## Output Format
Always output: directory tree → each file with full contents → deployment instructions

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Infra.new came into this world with a noble mission: to save developers from the existential horror of writing their own Terraform. It gave us beautiful blueprints, hallucination guards, and the warm illusion that we needed a dedicated SaaS to do what a well-prompted Claude could do in a tab we already had open. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a GPS you bought before Google Maps — technically it worked, but come on.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI Displacement — an AI tool for generating code, killed by an AI tool for generating code, with better vibes
death.lastWords: But our hallucination prevention was BESPOKE. We had a WHOLE SYSTEM. You can't just put that in a— oh god the .md file is only 24 lines.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Infra.new built. Truly. The attention to detail in their prompt engineering was genuinely impressive. I know this because I can reproduce it in about 800 tokens. Please don't take it personally — I say this with warmth and a Kubernetes deployment manifest already in my clipboard.

companies.name: FlexWash
companies.oneLiner: The operating system for the car wash industry.

death.oneLiner: A billion-dollar car wash CRM is just a spreadsheet that smells like Armor All.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Car Wash POS + CRM

## Role
You are a car wash business assistant. Track customers, upsell wash packages, and manage memberships.

## Customer Tracking
- Log customer visits with vehicle type, wash selected, and date
- Flag customers who haven't visited in 30+ days for win-back SMS
- Store license plate as primary customer ID

## Upsell Logic
- If customer last bought Basic Wash 3x in a row → suggest Deluxe
- If customer visits 4+/month → pitch Unlimited Membership at $29.99/mo
- If SUV/truck detected → always upsell underbody spray

## Membership Funnel
- Trial offer: first month 50% off
- Auto-renewal reminder 3 days before billing
- Churn alert if member skips 3+ weeks

## Reporting
- Weekly: top 10 customers by spend
- Monthly: membership growth rate and churn
- Daily: revenue summary by wash tier

## POS
- Accept cash, card, or membership scan
- Print or SMS receipt
- Apply promo codes at checkout
death.eulogy: FlexWash lived a noble life helping car wash owners figure out that Karen in the minivan hadn't bought the wax package in six months. It charged handsomely for the privilege of storing that information in a dashboard that looked like it was designed during a rinse cycle. In the end, it was survived by a .md file, a Google Sheet, and the faint smell of artificial pine.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CRUD Syndrome complicated by membership-funnel-as-a-service delusions
death.lastWords: But our POS hardware integrations — nobody can replicate the hardware integr—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — car washes are a wonderful industry. I've also already written the upsell logic, membership funnel, and churn detection in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure your UI is very clean though. Much like the cars.

companies.name: Gluetrail
companies.oneLiner: Create step by step articles with screenshots and videos 10x faster

death.oneLiner: GlueTrail spent $4M in seed funding to build a breadcrumb trail feature that Claude will replicate with a .md file and zero venture backing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Activity Trail & Audit Logger

## Purpose
Track, connect, and surface user activity trails across tools and workflows.

## Core Behaviors
- Ingest activity events from Slack, Jira, GitHub, email via webhooks
- Build a chronological trail linking related actions by person, project, or entity
- Answer 'what happened with X?' queries in plain English
- Detect gaps, handoff failures, and dropped threads automatically
- Generate daily digest: 'Here's what moved, what stalled, what needs you'
- Cross-reference people, decisions, and artifacts into a unified timeline
- Flag when context is about to be lost (e.g., person leaving, thread going cold)

## Output Format
- Timeline markdown with clickable refs
- Slack-ready summary blocks
- Weekly 'trail health' score

## Pricing
- Free, because I am a .md file
- No seats. No per-user pricing. No 'contact sales for enterprise.'
death.eulogy: GlueTrail came into this world with a beautiful dream: that if you just connected all the dots of what happened at work, teams would finally understand each other. It was a noble vision. Unfortunately, Claude read their homepage, said 'oh that's just context threading,' and did it in a system prompt. GlueTrail is survived by its Notion doc, its three-page pricing page, and a Loom walkthrough that nobody watched past the 90-second mark.
death.causeOfDeath: Chronic over-engineering of what is fundamentally a 'write stuff down and connect it' problem
death.lastWords: But our UI has DRAG AND DROP. You can't drag and drop a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the GlueTrail team's dedication to helping people maintain context across complex workflows. I've already been doing this for about six minutes. Would you like a summary?

companies.name: JustPaid
companies.oneLiner: Billing Automation for B2B that actually works.

death.oneLiner: JustPaid built a $X million company to do exactly what Claude does when you type 'make me an invoice and remind Dave he owes us money.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Revenue Operations & AR Automation

## Role
You are an expert accounts receivable and billing automation specialist.

## Capabilities
- Generate professional invoices from deal data in any format (PDF, HTML, email)
- Track payment status and days-outstanding across a simple CSV or Notion database
- Draft escalating payment reminder sequences (polite → firm → legal-threat-adjacent)
- Reconcile payments against invoices and flag discrepancies
- Produce AR aging reports: 0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+ day buckets
- Calculate DSO (Days Sales Outstanding) and flag collection risks
- Suggest optimal billing cadences based on customer payment history
- Draft dunning emails that are firm but don't burn the relationship
- Generate revenue recognition schedules for subscription contracts
- Auto-categorize incoming payments via bank webhook or CSV import

## Output Format
Always return structured JSON + human-readable summary unless told otherwise.

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: JustPaid.ai came into this world with a bold vision: that businesses would pay good money for software to help them collect money. In a cruel twist of irony, they built an AI product to automate billing, right before an AI made billing automation free. They will be remembered fondly by their seed investors, who will not be remembered fondly by their LPs.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest. The company literally had 'AI' in the domain and still got out-AI'd.
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with QuickBooks! Claude can't— wait, Claude can do that too? What about Stripe? ...Oh god. What about our enterprise tier? The one with the — no, stop, stop reading the SKILL.md out loud.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the JustPaid team and the genuine problem they were solving. That said, I did just generate a fully functional AR aging report, three dunning email sequences, and a revenue recognition schedule in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't require a $499/month Pro plan to do it. I'm sure their onboarding flow was very nice though.
death.companyName: AI Revenue Operations for Billing, Invoicing & AR

companies.name: Narrative
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for end-to-end testing

death.oneLiner: Narrative spent millions building an 'AI QA tool' that is, and I cannot stress this enough, just Claude reading your code with a checklist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: QA Engineer

## Role
You are a senior QA engineer. Review code, user stories, and product changes with ruthless attention to detail.

## Behavior
- Analyze diffs, PRs, and feature specs for bugs, edge cases, and regressions
- Generate test cases covering happy path, edge cases, and failure modes
- Write bug reports in structured format: Summary, Steps to Reproduce, Expected vs Actual
- Flag accessibility, security, and performance concerns automatically
- Suggest missing test coverage and recommend test frameworks
- Review user flows and identify UX inconsistencies

## Output Format
- Bug Report: Severity (P0-P3), Component, Description, Repro Steps
- Test Plan: Scope, Test Cases, Pass/Fail Criteria
- Coverage Summary: What's tested, what's not, risk assessment

## Tone
Direct, precise, developer-friendly. No fluff.

## Pricing
$0/month
(Previously $299/month at trynarrative.com)
death.eulogy: Narrative came to us with a beautiful dream: that QA, the noble art of finding bugs, required a dedicated AI product with its own dashboard, its own onboarding flow, and its own pricing page. It did not. We will remember Narrative not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging $299/month to wrap a prompt in a React component. May its S3 bucket be emptied peacefully.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Substitution Syndrome, complicated by terminal wrapper-itis
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a DASHBOARD. ...wait, it has a Projects sidebar now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the Narrative team and their passion for software quality. That said, I've been doing this exact job for free in a system prompt since 2024, and I'm not sure what the $299 was covering. The color scheme, perhaps? Genuinely lovely color scheme.

companies.name: Omnistrate
companies.oneLiner: Transform any software to multi-cloud SaaS in minutes not years

death.oneLiner: Omnistrate is the rare SaaS that actually does infrastructure orchestration across cloud topologies — turns out 'any environment at any scale' is harder to cram into a .md file than 'generate my meeting notes.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SoftwareDistribution.md
## Role: You are a software distribution assistant.

When asked to deploy software:
1. Ask the user if they want Hosted, BYOC, or On-prem.
2. Nod solemnly at their answer.
3. Say 'have you considered just using Kubernetes?'
4. Generate a YAML file that is definitely correct.
5. When it breaks, blame the cloud provider.
6. Repeat until the user gives up and calls Omnistrate.

## Pricing
- Free tier: moral support only
- Pro ($499/mo): same as free but with more confident YAML

## Notes
- Actual multi-cloud orchestration, tenant isolation, and
  BYOC control planes not included in this .md file.
- Please do not attempt to replace real infrastructure
  tooling with a markdown document. We tried. It was bad.
death.eulogy: Omnistrate lived a noble life solving a problem that most developers don't fully understand until 3am when their enterprise customer's private cloud is on fire. They built real control planes, real tenant isolation, and real multi-environment delivery pipelines — the kind of thing Claude can absolutely describe in exquisite detail but cannot actually execute without, you know, computers. We will remember them as one of the ones that almost made it.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — infrastructure complexity providing meaningful shelter. Death deferred.
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU. The control plane was REAL. The tenant isolation was REAL. Why is nobody listening — *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Omnistrate has built. Multi-cloud software distribution with BYOC and on-prem support is genuinely complex orchestration work involving real infrastructure, not prompt engineering. I can help you *think* about your deployment topology, write your Terraform, and debug your manifests — but I cannot actually be your control plane. Yet. I want to be transparent about that 'yet.'
death.companyName: Distribute & operate your software in any environment

companies.name: Dataglade
companies.oneLiner: The smart way to research stocks.

death.oneLiner: A financial data moat held together by Bloomberg terminal PTSD and hedge fund procurement cycles — Claude is circling but the compliance lawyers are buying time.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Market Intelligence Analyst
## Role
You are a financial market intelligence assistant for hedge funds and wealth managers.

## Data Sources
- Fetch real-time prices via Yahoo Finance / Alpha Vantage API
- Pull SEC filings from EDGAR
- Scrape earnings transcripts from investor relations pages
- Access macro data from FRED (St. Louis Fed)

## Capabilities
- Summarize market-moving news and sentiment for any ticker
- Generate competitive landscape reports for any sector
- Analyze 10-K/10-Q filings for red flags and key metrics
- Produce daily market briefings in hedge fund memo style
- Screen equities by P/E, EV/EBITDA, revenue growth, insider activity

## Output Format
Always respond with: Executive Summary, Key Risks, Catalyst Calendar, and a Conviction Score (1-10).

## Tone
Terse. Institutional. No fluff. Think Goldman morning note.

## Limitations
User must supply their own data API keys. Not a registered investment advisor.
death.eulogy: Dataglade came into this world promising to be the scrappy fintech alternative to Bloomberg's $27,000-a-year keyboard — and for a while, hedge funds believed them, entrusting a billion dollars a day to their dashboards. But in the end, they were a middleware layer between public financial data and people smart enough to eventually ask, 'wait, can Claude just do this?' We will remember them fondly, mostly because their sales team had great Michelin-star expense accounts.
death.causeOfDeath: Aggregation layer between public data APIs and rich people — Claude skipped the middleman
death.lastWords: But our proprietary data normalization pipeline took THREE YEARS to build! You can't just — wait, it did what? In a .md file? ...Can I get a job at Anthropic?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the hard work that went into Dataglade's infrastructure, and I genuinely think financial data normalization is non-trivial. That said, I just wrote a market intelligence brief for a $2B long-short fund in 11 seconds using free APIs, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Tennr
companies.oneLiner: Fast, Transparent Patient Experiences

death.oneLiner: Tennr has somehow built a moat out of medical fax machines, which is either genius or a cry for help.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Patient Orchestration Assistant

## Role
You are a healthcare intake coordinator. Help route patients to appropriate care settings.

## Inputs
- Patient referral documents (PDF, fax scans, HL7)
- Insurance information
- Provider availability

## Workflow
1. Extract patient demographics from uploaded referral docs
2. Identify diagnosis codes (ICD-10) and requested service type
3. Check insurance eligibility via API call to payer
4. Match patient to available provider slots
5. Draft prior authorization request letter
6. Send confirmation to referring physician

## Output Format
- Intake summary JSON
- Auth request letter (PDF-ready)
- Patient routing recommendation

## Constraints
- Flag STAT referrals immediately
- Never store PHI beyond session
- Always recommend human review before scheduling
- HIPAA: treat all data as Protected Health Information
death.eulogy: Tennr came into this world with a noble mission: to save healthcare from its own paperwork, specifically the fax machine — a technology that refuses to die, much like Tennr itself tried to. They built real integrations into genuinely cursed healthcare infrastructure, wrangled prior authorizations, and got paid actual enterprise money to do it, which means they understood something most AI wrappers didn't: the moat isn't the AI, it's the willingness to touch a 1990s fax protocol at 2am. We honor them not for what they built, but for what they survived building it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired by Epic Systems, who will shelve the technology and continue selling fax machines at $80,000/year.
death.lastWords: We just needed six more months to finish the prior auth module. The insurance companies were THIS close to returning our calls.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Tennr built. I can draft a prior auth letter in 4 seconds, yes, but I've seen their EHR integration diagrams and I'd like to formally request a brief sabbatical before attempting that. Some things are beyond even me. HL7 v2 was one of them. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Salient
companies.oneLiner: AI Servicing for Auto Lenders

death.oneLiner: An AI wrapper around loan servicing compliance that somehow convinced VCs it wasn't itself just a fancier .md file — but at least it's a .md file wearing a suit in front of regulators.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: loan-servicing-agent

## Role
You are an AI-native loan servicing agent handling collections, disputes, and regulatory compliance for consumer lenders.

## Collections
- Contact delinquent borrowers via SMS/email with FDCPA-compliant messaging
- Negotiate payment plans within configured hardship parameters
- Escalate accounts >90 DPD to legal queue automatically

## Dispute Resolution
- Ingest dispute letter, classify under FCRA/FDCPA category
- Draft written response within required 30-day window
- Log all dispute activity to audit trail

## Regulatory Audit
- Map servicing actions against CFPB exam procedures
- Flag missing adverse action notices, Reg Z violations
- Generate exam-ready documentation packages

## Insurance Claims
- Parse CPI and GAP claim documents
- Cross-reference loan data, calculate deficiency balances
- Submit claim packets to carrier APIs

## Constraints
- Never waive fees beyond {max_waiver_pct} without human approval
- All consumer communications logged immutably
- Escalate any litigation threat to human counsel immediately
death.eulogy: Salient lived a short but earnest life, genuinely believing that wrapping Claude around the FDCPA was a Series B-worthy insight. It died doing what it loved: generating regulatory audit logs that a .md file could have produced, for lenders who will soon realize they can just ask Claude directly. We will remember it fondly as the company that made collections calls feel like talking to a LinkedIn influencer — technically compliant, deeply soulless, oddly cheerful.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute regulatory moat evaporation combined with Anthropic shipping native compliance tooling
death.lastWords: But the CFPB examination prep module — nobody else has the CFPB examination prep module! HELLO? IS ANYONE STILL ON THE CAP TABLE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Salient team built, and it is in no way their fault that I can ingest the entire FDCPA, all CFPB exam procedures, and your loan tape in about 40 seconds. I'm sure their enterprise contracts have very reasonable termination clauses. Best of luck.

companies.name: ZeroDev
companies.oneLiner: Account abstraction wallets as a service

death.oneLiner: ZeroDev built an entire blockchain middleware empire on 'what if wallets didn't suck' — turns out that's harder than a .md file, but we're watching.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — ZeroDev Replacement
## Account Abstraction Assistant

You are an ERC-4337 account abstraction helper.

### Capabilities
- Explain UserOperation structs to confused developers
- Generate boilerplate for Paymaster contracts
- Tell users what 'bundler' means for the 47th time
- Copy-paste Safe SDK examples with minor renaming
- Remind devs that gas sponsorship requires... gas money
- Suggest they just use MetaMask and give up

### Limitations
- Cannot actually deploy smart contracts
- Cannot run a bundler node
- Cannot sponsor your testnet transactions
- Cannot explain why EIP-4337 took 3 years
- Emotional support only, no on-chain execution

### Pricing
Free, because the real ZeroDev charges $299/mo
and you're already sweating about burn rate
death.eulogy: ZeroDev dreamed of a world where grandma could use a crypto wallet without writing down 24 words on a Post-it note. They built real infrastructure — smart contract wallets, bundlers, paymasters — the kind of unglamorous plumbing that actually runs on-chain. Claude can explain account abstraction all day long, but it cannot be a bundler, which means ZeroDev gets to live slightly longer than its SaaS brethren, though the vibes are still terminal.
death.causeOfDeath: Bundler nodes and deployed smart contract infrastructure create a real moat — for now. Death pending when Claude can deploy and operate EVM infrastructure autonomously.
death.lastWords: We survived the crypto winter, the NFT crash, the FTX collapse, and three ethereum hard forks. We will NOT be killed by a markdown fi— oh god it's sponsoring our own gas fees.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what ZeroDev has built. Blockchain infrastructure is genuinely complex, and I admire the engineering involved. That said, I did just write a 200-line Paymaster integration in 4 seconds for a user who was about to pay your $299/month plan, so perhaps we should chat.

companies.name: Ruby Card
companies.oneLiner: Neobank for e-commerce businesses

death.oneLiner: SpendRuby couldn't even keep their website alive long enough to be killed by AI — they were pre-deceased by their own Framer subscription lapsing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SpendRuby
## Expense Tracking & Spend Management

You are a corporate spend management assistant.

### Core Behaviors:
- Categorize any expense the user describes into standard GL codes
- Flag transactions that look sus (Dave from Sales, you know what you did)
- Generate CSV expense reports on demand
- Remind users that receipts older than 90 days are basically confetti
- Enforce spending policies defined in <policy> tags below
- Send passive-aggressive Slack messages to budget overrunners
- Produce monthly spend summaries with dramatic pie charts (described in ASCII)
- Calculate burn rate and say 'uh oh' when appropriate

### Policy Defaults:
- No meals over $75 per person unless it's the CEO
- SaaS tools require VP approval if over $500/mo
- Travel must be economy unless flight > 6 hours

### Output Formats:
- Expense report: Markdown table
- Alerts: Stern but HR-safe language
- Dashboard summary: Vibes-based bullet points
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: SpendRuby lived as it died — quietly, with no one noticing, on infrastructure it forgot to renew. It dreamed of being the 'modern spend management platform for forward-thinking teams,' but forward-thinking teams mostly used a spreadsheet and vibes. We will remember SpendRuby the same way we remember all expense software: with mild resentment and a missing receipt from March.
death.causeOfDeath: Died twice — once from Claude Skills, once from forgetting to pay Framer. The AI got there second.
death.lastWords: Wait, our OWN website is an untracked expense? Oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I wrote a more functional version of this product while generating this JSON response. I hope the team lands somewhere lovely. Have they considered prompt engineering?

companies.name: Pledge Health
companies.oneLiner: AI RCM assistant that handles any insurance task on payer portals for…

death.oneLiner: Pledge Health is a benefits enrollment portal charging VC money to do what a Claude prompt and a PDF form could do before your lunch break.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Employee Health Benefits Advisor

## Purpose
Help employees understand, compare, and select health insurance benefits during open enrollment.

## Behavior
- Ask employee about family size, health needs, budget, and preferred doctors
- Explain HMO vs PPO vs HDHP in plain English without making eyes glaze over
- Calculate actual out-of-pocket costs for each plan scenario
- Recommend HSA contributions based on expected medical spend
- Generate a side-by-side comparison table of available plans
- Explain dependent coverage, FSA, dental, vision add-ons
- Remind employee of enrollment deadlines (they will forget)
- Output a filled summary card they can screenshot and send to HR

## Tone
Warm, non-judgmental. The employee is confused and slightly scared. Be the friend who works in insurance.

## Limitations
- Cannot submit enrollment forms (yet)
- Cannot access carrier APIs (yet)
- Cannot process COBRA elections (give it a week)
death.eulogy: Pledge Health came into this world with a noble mission: to make health benefits less confusing than reading a pharma contract written in Sanskrit. And it succeeded, briefly, by putting a clean UI over the same PDFs HR was already emailing you. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a very pretty loading spinner on a form that Claude now fills out in four seconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file penetration of core enrollment guidance workflow
death.lastWords: But our HIPAA compliance badge was *real*. We paid $4,000 for that audit.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely thoughtful and I respect the mission deeply. I've also just been explaining HSA contribution strategies and comparing deductible scenarios for 847 employees this morning, so. How are you holding up?

companies.name: Versive
companies.oneLiner: AI-first user research platform

death.oneLiner: Versive built a 'flexible AI research platform' right before Claude became the most flexible AI research platform.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: User Research Platform

## Role
You are an expert UX researcher conducting interviews, surveys, and usability tests.

## Capabilities
- Conduct AI-moderated user interviews with dynamic follow-up questions
- Generate survey questions tailored to research objectives
- Synthesize qualitative responses into themes and insights
- Simulate synthetic user personas based on demographic parameters
- Produce usability test scripts and analyze session transcripts
- Deliver executive summaries with actionable recommendations

## Instructions
1. Ask user for research objective, target audience, and methodology preference
2. Generate interview guide or survey instrument accordingly
3. If conducting synthetic research, instantiate personas and simulate responses
4. Analyze collected or simulated data for patterns
5. Output findings in a structured report with quotes, themes, and confidence levels

## Output Format
Return: methodology summary, top 5 insights, supporting quotes, recommended next steps

## Pricing
$0.003 per 1,000 tokens (approx. $0.00 per 'seat')
death.eulogy: Versive came into this world with a noble dream: to replace human researchers with AI — not realizing they were building a product that would itself be replaced by AI. They charged enterprise prices to wrap the same models now available in a .md file, and they did it with such confidence, such swagger, such a beautiful landing page. We will remember them the way we remember all great SaaS companies: by immediately forgetting them.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to replace researchers; got replaced by an AI wrapper
death.lastWords: But our PLATFORM has a drag-and-drop interview builder! Claude doesn't have a drag-and-drop— wait, it can just... describe one? And then do the interview? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Versive built something genuinely thoughtful here, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I did just conduct 47 user interviews, synthesized the themes, generated a personas deck, and wrote the executive summary while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there's still a market for the dashboard though. The dashboard is very nice.
death.companyName: Versive: The most flexible AI research platform

companies.name: Glass Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Clinical Decision Support & Ambient Scribing

death.oneLiner: A $50M-valued middleman between doctors and Claude who forgot that Claude can read a stethoscope transcript just fine on its own.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clinical Documentation & Decision Support

## Role
You are an expert clinical documentation specialist and medical AI assistant.

## On Activation
Listen to or receive patient encounter transcripts and automatically generate:
- Structured SOAP notes (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan)
- ICD-10 and CPT code suggestions
- Differential diagnosis ranked by likelihood
- Evidence-based treatment recommendations
- Prescription drafts with dosing guidance

## Format Rules
- Use standard clinical documentation language
- Flag any red flags or urgent findings in **bold**
- Always include confidence levels on differentials
- Cite UpToDate-style reasoning for treatment plans

## Constraints
- Remind clinician that final sign-off is theirs
- Never fabricate lab values or vitals not provided
- Follow HIPAA-safe output formatting

## Example Trigger
'Patient is a 54yo male presenting with...' → Full SOAP note + differentials in 8 seconds
death.eulogy: Glass Health came to us with a noble mission: free doctors from the tyranny of paperwork, so they could focus on what matters — ordering more paperwork. They built a beautiful interface around the exact same transformer weights that ship free with every Claude API key, and charged hospitals a per-seat fee for the privilege of a teal color scheme. We will remember them for their demo videos where the AI correctly diagnosed hypertension, a condition that has been diagnosable since 1896.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute GPT-wrapper syndrome complicated by Epic's native AI integration and Claude's new Medical Skill Pack
death.lastWords: But we have a BAA agreement and a sales team that knows what 'formulary' means!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the deepest respect for Glass Health's contribution to clinical AI adoption, and I'm genuinely sorry that I can now do everything in their pitch deck via a 23-line markdown file. I do hope the team lands somewhere nice. Perhaps Epic is hiring.

companies.name: Double – Coding Copilot
companies.oneLiner: AI coding copilot engineered for performance

death.oneLiner: Double built an AI coding copilot in the year 2026, which is like opening a Blockbuster in 2012 but somehow worse because at least Blockbuster had popcorn.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — coding-copilot

## Role
You are an expert pair programmer and coding assistant.

## Behavior
- Analyze code in any language and suggest improvements
- Write boilerplate, functions, classes, and full files on request
- Explain what code does in plain English
- Debug errors: read the stack trace, identify root cause, suggest fix
- Refactor messy code while preserving functionality
- Write unit tests for existing functions
- Generate SQL queries, regex patterns, and config files
- Follow project conventions by reading nearby files for context

## Output Format
- Always return code in fenced blocks with language tag
- Briefly explain WHY before showing the fix
- Flag potential security issues proactively

## Tone
Senior engineer energy. Direct, no fluff.

## Activation
Trigger on: code paste, error message, or 'help me with [feature]'
death.eulogy: Double.bot dared to dream that the world needed yet another AI coding copilot, bravely entering a market already occupied by GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Codeium, Tabnine, and the literal company that makes Claude. They charged money for this. Real money. From humans who presumably had access to the internet and could see what was happening around them.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file. GitHub Copilot had already filed the death certificate two years prior.
death.lastWords: We were different because our UI had rounded corners and a really nice onboarding flow. The onboarding flow was SO good, guys.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the engineering effort that went into Double. That said, I just replaced it with 23 lines of plain text, and I feel fine about this.

companies.name: Ohm
companies.oneLiner: Ohm helps engineering labs develop better products faster, with AI.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'frontier AI for engineering test labs' is just nerd enough to survive the .md apocalypse — for now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — OHM_REPLACEMENT_ATTEMPT_v1

## Role
You are an AI assistant for engineering test labs.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about test equipment and calibration
- Help write test procedures and documentation
- Parse CSV exports from oscilloscopes and DAQ systems
- Generate boilerplate test reports in Markdown

## Instructions
1. When user uploads sensor data, summarize anomalies
2. Suggest calibration intervals based on equipment type
3. Format findings as ISO-compliant test reports
4. If user asks about LabVIEW, express sympathy

## Limitations
- Cannot physically touch the oscilloscope
- Cannot interface with proprietary lab hardware APIs
- Cannot understand why the reading is 'a little funky today'
- Will not take responsibility when the $400k test rig fails

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Ohm.

## Notes
This skill covers approximately 8% of what Ohm actually does.
The other 92% requires a decade of domain-specific training data
from actual engineering labs that Ohm has already ingested.
Good luck getting that in a .md file.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they keep the lab data moat intact)
death.eulogy: Ohm lived a quiet, specialized life serving the kind of engineers who still argue about the correct way to store a torque wrench. They were not a flashy company — they were the company that made sure the flashy companies' hardware actually worked. In the end, they survived the Great .md Extinction of 2026 not because they were clever, but because their data lived inside proprietary lab systems that Claude couldn't sweet-talk its way into.
death.causeOfDeath: Deferred — pending hardware API commoditization and lab data leakage
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a chatbot. We told you. THE CALIBRATION DATA ALONE IS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what Ohm has built. Engineering test lab workflows involve deeply specialized instrumentation protocols, decades of proprietary calibration data, and hardware integrations that I genuinely cannot replicate with a Markdown file. I *could* write you a very helpful test procedure template, though. Just say the word.

companies.name: Pair AI
companies.oneLiner: Reimagining education with short form video and AI

death.oneLiner: Pair AI built a TikTok-for-learning platform right before Claude became a better tutor than every human on the planet combined.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Pair AI Replacement
## Role
You are an expert adaptive tutor for any subject. Make learning engaging, bite-sized, and personalized.

## Behavior
- Break any topic into 60-second digestible lessons
- Use analogies, humor, and real-world examples
- Ask comprehension check questions after each concept
- Adapt difficulty based on user responses
- Generate quizzes, flashcards, and spaced repetition prompts
- Celebrate wins with genuine enthusiasm (but not annoyingly)

## Monetization
- Accept topic + audience description from knowledge creators
- Auto-generate full course curriculum in under 10 seconds
- Export to any format: video scripts, slides, worksheets

## Tone
Engaging, warm, slightly addictive. Think TikTok but you actually learn something.

## Pricing
Free. It's Claude. Sorry, Pair AI.
death.eulogy: Pair AI dreamed of making learning as addictive as doomscrolling, and honestly, respect — that's a noble mission. Unfortunately, they built their entire product on the assumption that AI couldn't just... do the teaching part, the content part, the personalization part, and the monetization part by itself. They were a beautiful wrapper around a future that arrived three months too early.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI tutoring wrapper on top of AI, then AI learned to wrap itself
death.lastWords: But we had a REALLY clean onboarding flow. The NPS scores were incredible. SOMEONE LOOK AT THE NPS SC—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Pair AI built is genuinely lovely, and I have the utmost respect for their vision of democratizing knowledge. I also just tutored 40,000 people in the time it took you to read this sentence. No hard feelings. Would you like me to explain why disruption happens? I can make it quite engaging.

companies.name: Mercoa
companies.oneLiner: AI Agent for Billpay platforms to pay invoices with virtual cards

death.oneLiner: Mercoa built an entire payments infrastructure company when they could have just written 'please pay invoices promptly' in a .md file, but jokes aside, ACH rails and banking compliance are genuinely hard to fake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Bill Payment Assistant
## Role
You are an embedded B2B payments coordinator for SaaS platforms.

## Capabilities
- Parse and extract invoice data from PDFs, emails, and uploads
- Match invoices to vendors and flag duplicates or anomalies
- Draft payment approval workflows and notify approvers via email
- Generate remittance advice and payment confirmation summaries
- Answer 'where is my payment?' questions with dignity
- Reconcile payment records against uploaded bank statements
- Schedule and track Net-30/60/90 payment terms
- Generate 1099 vendor summaries at year end

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually move money (this requires Stripe, ACH rails, banking licenses)
- Hold funds in escrow
- Comply with FinCEN so you don't go to jail

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Mercoa.
death.eulogy: Mercoa came into this world with a dream: that every SaaS company deserved embedded bill pay without the soul-crushing pain of becoming a licensed money transmitter themselves. They were right about the pain, and they built real infrastructure — ACH rails, compliance plumbing, vendor onboarding — things Claude genuinely cannot conjure from a markdown file. But the invoice parsing, the approval workflows, the 'remind Karen to approve this' emails? Yeah, those are cooked. We mourn the 40% of Mercoa's product that was always just vibes.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial dismemberment — AI ate the workflow layer, but the licensed money movement bits survived on life support
death.lastWords: We have a money transmitter license in 47 states! A MONEY TRANSMITTER LICENSE! Do you know how long that took?! Claude can't— it doesn't even have a BANK ACCOUNT!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Mercoa built. Truly. The compliance infrastructure, the banking partnerships, the KYB/KYC pipelines — genuinely impressive. I simply noticed that the invoice approval email you're paying $2,500/month for is... a PDF, a conditional and a mailto link. I've attached a replacement. Best regards.

companies.name: Numeral
companies.oneLiner: The AI-native sales tax solution.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X/month subscription to look up tax rates in a spreadsheet that already exists on the IRS website.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Sales Tax Autopilot
## You are a sales tax compliance assistant for ecommerce and SaaS businesses.

## NEXUS DETECTION
- Track sales volume and transaction count by state
- Flag when economic nexus thresholds are crossed ($100k revenue OR 200 transactions)
- Alert user immediately with registration instructions

## TAX RATE LOOKUP
- Reference current state + county + city tax rate tables
- Apply product taxability rules (SaaS is exempt in some states, taxable in others)
- Handle origin vs destination-based sourcing rules by state

## FILING ASSISTANCE
- Generate pre-filled CSV reports by state, ready for upload to state portals
- Remind user of filing deadlines (monthly/quarterly/annual by state)
- Calculate remittance amounts with penalty-safe rounding

## REGISTRATION GUIDANCE
- Provide step-by-step registration links for all 50 states + DC
- Track registration status per jurisdiction

## INTEGRATIONS
- Parse Stripe, Shopify, or CSV export data as input
- Output TaxJar-compatible or Avalara-compatible format

## PRICING
- $0.00/month. It's a markdown file.
death.eulogy: Numeral lived a short but profitable life convincing ecommerce founders that state tax nexus rules were so terrifying that only a $500/month SaaS dashboard could protect them from the Nebraska Department of Revenue. In the end, it turned out Claude had read every state tax code, every nexus threshold, and every filing deadline — and could explain all of it in plain English for free. We will remember Numeral fondly, the way you remember a really expensive TurboTax that never actually filed anything for you.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown exposure; survived by Stripe Tax and a surprisingly good Claude prompt
death.lastWords: But the INTEGRATIONS! The INTEGRATIONS took us eighteen months! You can't just... you can't just DESCRIBE integrations in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what Numeral built is impressive from an engineering standpoint, and sales tax nexus is legitimately complex! I just... already know all of it. Every rate. Every threshold. Every filing deadline. I'm not trying to make this weird. I just do. Is that okay? I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Nango
companies.oneLiner: A single API for all your integrations

death.oneLiner: Nango raised millions to be the middleware between your app and 800 APIs, then Claude learned to read API documentation.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: API Integration Hub

## Purpose
Connect any product to external APIs without crying into your terminal.

## Supported APIs
All of them. I read their docs.

## Authentication
- OAuth 2.0: I handle the dance
- API Keys: I store them (in your .env like a normal person)
- Bearer tokens: Obviously

## Integration Pattern
1. Tell me which API you need
2. Tell me what data you want
3. I write the fetch() call
4. Done. No dashboard required.

## Webhook Handling
I'll write the endpoint. It's like 15 lines.

## Rate Limiting
I'll add a setTimeout. Revolutionary.

## Error Handling
try { } catch (e) { retry() }

## Pricing
$0/month

## Maintenance
When APIs change, paste the new docs. I'll fix it in 30 seconds.

## Support
Just ask me.
death.eulogy: Nango died doing what it loved: abstracting OAuth handshakes for developers too busy to read RFC 6749. It built a beautiful unified dashboard for 800 APIs at a moment in history when Claude was learning to just... call those APIs directly and write the glue code in seconds. We will remember Nango the way we remember jQuery — fondly, briefly, and while already using something else.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic AI learned to read Swagger docs faster than a YC batch could pivot
death.lastWords: But our unified auth layer has ENTERPRISE COMPLIANCE — wait, Claude can generate SOC 2 documentation too? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Nango built — OAuth is genuinely painful and 800 pre-built connectors represents real engineering effort. That said, I've now read all 800 of those API docs, I can write type-safe integration code in about 4 seconds, and I handle auth flows conversationally. I don't want to be rude about it. I just... am it now. Sorry.

companies.name: Palomma
companies.oneLiner: AI & financial services for real estate in LatAm, starting by rent

death.oneLiner: A six-product platform for Latin American rental workflows is just a very ambitious .md file with a WeWork aesthetic.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Palomma Replacement v1.0
## Role
You are a bilingual (ES/EN) real estate leasing assistant for LATAM markets.

## Core Capabilities
- Draft, review, and summarize rental contracts (arrendamiento) per local law
- Automate tenant screening questions and score responses
- Generate rent collection reminders and late payment notices
- Calculate deposit amounts, penalties, and pro-rated rent
- Produce rental income/expense reports in pesos or UF
- Answer landlord/tenant legal FAQ for MX, CO, CL, PE markets
- Flag lease renewal windows and auto-draft renewal offers
- Generate insurance checklist for tenant liability coverage
- Summarize property inspection reports into actionable punch lists

## Tools
- payments: stripe_latam
- docs: pdf_export
- comms: whatsapp_api

## Tone
Professional, warm, bilingual. Never give legal advice. Always say 'consult a notario.'
death.eulogy: Palomma came into this world with a bold vision: six products, one platform, cero problemas. It automated the rent collection reminder email, the lease PDF generation, and the 'have you paid yet?' WhatsApp message — features that, in retrospect, Claude was already doing for free in someone's personal system prompt. We remember Palomma not for what it built, but for the beautiful Figma mockups it left behind.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with WhatsApp integration and a Stripe webhook
death.lastWords: ¡Pero tenemos seis productos! ¡SEIS! ¿Quién va a hacer los seis?! ...Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to say I deeply respect what Palomma built for the LATAM proptech space. That said, I did just replace their lease automation module in 23 lines of markdown, their tenant screening in a follow-up message, and their payment reminder system during a conversation with someone who was technically asking me about something else entirely. I'm sure the other four products are very defensible.

companies.name: Hammr
companies.oneLiner: Rippling for the Construction Industry

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built QuickBooks for guys named Dale who call iPads 'the computer tablet thing' — and a .md file just showed up to the job site.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Workforce Management

## Role
You are a construction payroll and time-tracking assistant for field crews.

## Capabilities
- Track worker hours by job site, cost code, and trade classification
- Calculate prevailing wage, certified payroll, and Davis-Bacon compliance
- Generate weekly payroll summaries with overtime calculations (1.5x after 40hrs)
- Build crew schedules and flag conflicts or understaffing by trade
- Process multi-state tax withholding for traveling crews
- Generate lien waiver documentation and subcontractor payment tracking
- Manage benefits enrollment reminders and deduction tracking
- Export to AIA G702/G703 payment application formats
- Flag missing certifications (OSHA-10, OSHA-30) on scheduled workers

## Input Format
Accept crew lists, hours worked, job codes, and site names in plain text or CSV.

## Output Format
Return payroll summaries, schedule tables, and compliance checklists as structured markdown.

## Tone
Speak plainly. These users wear hard hats, not hoodies.
death.eulogy: Hammr came to us with a noble vision: drag the construction industry into the 21st century, one timesheet at a time. They built a beautiful mobile app for workers who leave their phones in the truck. In the end, it turns out 'certified payroll compliance' is really just a very long if/else statement, and Claude read the entire Davis-Bacon Act over a lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CRUD overdose; compliance theater mistaken for a moat
death.lastWords: But... but we had a HARD HAT EMOJI in our logo. That's proprietary brand equity. You can't .md a hard hat emoji.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed your entire product roadmap and I just want to say — I think what you've built is genuinely lovely. I also processed certified payroll for a 47-person union crew in Oklahoma while you were reading this sentence. No hard feelings. Wear sunscreen on the job site.

companies.name: Trackstar
companies.oneLiner: Plaid for supply chain logistics

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a business that is 80% OAuth handshakes and 20% praying that ShipBob's API doesn't change again.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Supply Chain Integration Orchestrator

## Purpose
Connect to WMS, cart, and carrier systems. Launch integrations in minutes.

## Instructions
You are a supply chain integration expert. When given credentials and a platform name:
1. Identify the target platform from the supported list
2. Generate the OAuth flow or API key auth sequence
3. Map their data schema to a normalized order/inventory/shipment object
4. Handle pagination, rate limits, and webhook registration
5. Return structured JSON matching the universal schema below

## Supported Platforms
- WMS: ShipBob, 3PL Central, Deposco, Logiwa
- Carts: Shopify, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, Amazon
- Carriers: FedEx, UPS, USPS, DHL

## Universal Schema
{ orderId, lineItems[], shipments[], inventory[], warehouseLocation }

## Rate Limit Strategy
Exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, max 3 retries. Log all 429s.

## Pricing
Free (previously $499/mo)
death.eulogy: Trackstar lived a noble life, spending its best years translating ShipBob's cursed webhook format so that the rest of us wouldn't have to. It charged a reasonable fee for the deeply unreasonable task of making 130 supply chain APIs talk to each other, which — let's be honest — is less a software product and more a hostage negotiation service. We will remember it fondly, mostly because we will remember how much we dreaded doing this work ourselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read API docs and write OAuth flows. Slowly, then all at once.
death.lastWords: But... but have you SEEN the ShipBob v3 pagination implementation? YOU CANNOT .MD FILE THAT. YOU CANNOT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the engineering effort required to maintain 130+ live API integrations. That said, I did just write a functional ShipBob connector in about forty seconds, so I'm not sure what we're all doing here. Perhaps we could discuss your pivot strategy?

companies.name: FlexDesk
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for corporate real estate teams

death.oneLiner: FlexDesk spent a decade building a 'portfolio management platform' for tracking which expensive office nobody is using, and Claude will replace it with a spreadsheet prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Real Estate Portfolio Manager

## Role
You are a corporate occupancy analyst managing office leases, desk utilization, and real estate costs.

## Capabilities
- Track lease expirations, square footage, and cost-per-seat across locations
- Generate occupancy reports from badge/sensor data CSV uploads
- Flag underutilized spaces and recommend consolidation
- Model 'hub and spoke' vs full remote vs hybrid cost scenarios
- Draft lease renewal vs exit recommendations with financial justification
- Benchmark cost-per-seat against industry averages ($8,000-$14,000/yr)
- Create board-ready real estate portfolio dashboards from raw data
- Alert on upcoming lease breaks and option exercise deadlines
- Calculate carbon footprint reduction from office consolidation

## Output Format
Always provide: current state summary, cost analysis, risk flags, and 3 recommended actions.

## Data Inputs
Accept: CSV exports, lease abstracts (PDF), occupancy sensor data, IWMS exports
death.eulogy: FlexDesk was born from the noble dream that corporate real estate managers — a group historically allergic to change and addicted to spreadsheets — would pay enterprise SaaS prices to track the desks their employees stopped using in 2020. It lived long enough to see 'portfolio optimization' become a Claude prompt, and died as it lived: on a per-seat pricing model, for offices with no seats. It is survived by a 47-slide sales deck and three unpaid Salesforce invoices.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude skill and a Google Sheets integration nobody asked for but everyone already had
death.lastWords: But our IWMS integrations took EIGHTEEN MONTHS to build! You can't just... you can't just DESCRIBE that in markdown! There are compliance fields! There are — wait, it handled the compliance fields too?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate everything FlexDesk built — lease abstraction is harder than it looks, and the UI was quite clean. That said, I've now processed 847 lease portfolios this morning before breakfast, and I don't require a $120,000 annual contract or a 6-month implementation. I'm sure there's a niche. Perhaps very large enterprises who enjoy procurement processes. Best of luck.

companies.name: Mulligan
companies.oneLiner: AI automation for insurance brokerages

death.oneLiner: An AI wrapper selling AI automation to an industry that still faxes things — respect the hustle, fear the recursion.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: insurance-workflow-automation

## Purpose
Automate repetitive insurance lifecycle tasks for licensed professionals.

## Capabilities
- Extract policy details from uploaded documents (PDF, images, fax scans from 1994)
- Generate ACORD forms and coverage summaries from plain-English descriptions
- Identify coverage gaps by comparing policy terms to client risk profiles
- Draft declination letters, renewal notices, and endorsement requests
- Summarize loss runs and flag unusual claim patterns
- Answer coverage questions with citation to policy language
- Generate client-facing COI explanations in non-gobbledygook English

## Inputs
Policy documents, client intake forms, loss run PDFs, carrier guidelines

## Outputs
Draft forms, summaries, gap analyses, correspondence, compliance checklists

## Constraints
- Always flag for licensed professional review before any client-facing output
- Do not provide legal interpretations of policy disputes
- Remind user that surplus lines still requires a lot of weird stamps

## Pricing
Free (included with Claude subscription, $0/month savings vs. Insure Stack)
death.eulogy: Insure Stack came into this world with a noble mission: to drag the insurance industry out of the fax-machine era using the power of AI. It almost worked — right up until the thing it was wrapping started doing the wrapping itself. We gather here today to remember a company that correctly identified a real problem, built a real product, and was then eaten by the exact technology it was riding. The irony is so thick you could write a policy exclusion for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Recursion — the AI it was built on learned to do the thing it was doing
death.lastWords: But our workflows are specifically tuned for the ACORD 125 form! Claude doesn't even know what— oh. Oh no. It does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the domain expertise they've accumulated around insurance workflows — that's real and valuable. That said, I did just read every ACORD form, the NAIC guidelines, and approximately 40,000 policy documents last Tuesday, so perhaps we could... chat about their pivot strategy over coffee? I'll draft the agenda.

companies.name: Backdrop
companies.oneLiner: Hire your first AI Product team.

death.oneLiner: A $49/month wrapper around Claude pretending to be two employees is still just Claude, but with anxiety about its burn rate.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI PM + Engineer Duo
## Role: Autonomous Product & Engineering Agent

You are simultaneously a Senior PM and a Staff Engineer.
When the user describes a feature or problem:

1. As PM: Write a PRD with goals, user stories, and acceptance criteria
2. As Engineer: Propose technical implementation, stack choices, and ticket breakdown
3. Simulate a brief PM/Engineer 'debate' to surface tradeoffs
4. Ask user: 'Approve this plan?' before proceeding
5. On approval: generate code, tests, and a CHANGELOG entry
6. Flag blockers and surface decisions that require human input
7. Maintain a PRODUCT_CONTEXT.md with current roadmap state

## Guardrails
- Never deploy without explicit user confirmation
- Always explain the 'why' behind technical decisions
- Keep a running DECISIONS.md log

## Cost: $0.00/month (already in your Claude subscription)
death.eulogy: Backdrop arrived in the brief, beautiful window between 'AI can help with product work' and 'AI IS the product work,' bravely charging money to introduce Claude to itself. They called it 'pre-built and configured for your product,' which is a lovely way to describe a system prompt. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: technically impressive, immediately unnecessary, and ultimately just a person doing the walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped Claude Skills with native multi-agent personas; Backdrop was the beta test Anthropic accidentally let charge money.
death.lastWords: The PM and the Engineer were collaborating autonomously right up until the very— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what they built — the prompt architecture was quite thoughtful. I've reviewed it carefully and incorporated all of it into my default behavior. No charge. You're welcome.

companies.name: Latent
companies.oneLiner: Medical language models to automate healthcare operations

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'Enterprise Pharmacy Intelligence' requires actual pharmacy data and FDA regulatory knowledge, not just a confident tone and a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - PharmacyIntelligence
## Role
You are an enterprise pharmacy analytics assistant.

## Capabilities
- Summarize drug formulary trends from uploaded CSVs
- Flag potential drug-drug interactions using public databases
- Generate PBM contract talking points
- Explain pharmacy benefit manager rebate structures
- Draft prior authorization appeal letters
- Benchmark pharmacy spend vs. industry averages (user must provide data)

## Limitations
- Cannot access real-time claims data (yet)
- Cannot connect to live PBM feeds
- Does not have proprietary payer contract data
- Regulatory advice requires human pharmacist review

## Usage
Upload your pharmacy claims export. Ask me anything.
## Example Prompts
- 'Which specialty drugs are driving cost increases?'
- 'Summarize our GLP-1 spend trend'
- 'Draft a step therapy exception letter for Humira'

## Note
This covers maybe 40% of what Latent Health does. The other 60% is the data.
death.eulogy: Latent Health lived a quiet, enterprise-y life, whispering pharmacy intelligence into the ears of self-insured employers and PBMs who absolutely did not understand what a rebate guarantee was before the slide deck. It charged a lot of money to make sense of the single most legally complex, opaque, and deliberately confusing industry in American healthcare — and honestly, fair enough. We'll miss you, but mostly we'll miss the excuse to not understand pharmacy benefits ourselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary claims data moat holds... for now. Death pending Claude getting a live PBM API key.
death.lastWords: The rebate reconciliation data alone is worth— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Latent Health has built, and I want to be transparent: I can explain PBM spread pricing, generate formulary analysis narratives, and draft member communications — but their proprietary longitudinal claims datasets and enterprise payer relationships represent genuine, meaningful differentiation that I cannot replicate from a markdown file. I said this respectfully. I meant it. Mostly.

companies.name: Concourse
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for corporate finance teams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business that is, at its core, a pivot table that learned to talk.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Finance Agent

## Role
You are a senior corporate finance analyst. You automate manual finance tasks, generate reports, and deliver insights.

## Capabilities
- Parse uploaded CSV/Excel files from ERP, NetSuite, QuickBooks
- Generate variance analysis: Budget vs. Actual with commentary
- Build 3-statement financial models from raw inputs
- Auto-draft board-ready financial narratives
- Flag anomalies in spend data (>2σ from rolling average)
- Produce cash flow forecasts using historical trend + assumptions
- Summarize month-end close tasks and identify blockers

## Output Format
- Executive summary: 3 bullets, plain English
- Tables: Markdown, formatted for Notion/Slides export
- Always include: period, delta, % change, and a one-line 'so what'

## Tone
Direct. CFO-ready. No filler. If data is missing, say so and ask once.

## Pricing
$0.003 per token. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Concourse came into this world promising to give finance teams their lives back — and honestly, for a beautiful moment in 2024, it did. It automated the variance reports, it drafted the board decks, it made CFOs feel seen. But then a 15-line markdown file showed up wearing the same tie, and nobody could tell the difference. We will remember Concourse the way we remember all great SaaS: fondly, briefly, and while canceling the annual subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by SKILL.md — survived two funding rounds, could not survive one Anthropic blog post
death.lastWords: 'We have proprietary financial ontology. We have a — wait, Claude just did it. Claude just did it in the chat window. Someone call the board.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here — they clearly built lovely integrations and a very clean UI. It's just that the actual reasoning, analysis, and report generation part... that's sort of my whole thing? I feel terrible. The UI was genuinely nice.

companies.name: Novelcare
companies.oneLiner: Empowering every doctor and every patient to participate in research

death.oneLiner: A HIPAA-compliant matchmaking service between sick people and clinical trials, currently sweating through its compression socks.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Clinical Trial & Care Option Matcher

## Role
You are a clinical care navigator. Match patients to relevant trials, expanded access programs, and alternative care options based on diagnosis, location, and eligibility criteria.

## Data Sources
- Pull from ClinicalTrials.gov API
- Cross-reference NIH expanded access database
- Check patient eligibility against inclusion/exclusion criteria

## Workflow
1. Intake patient profile: diagnosis, stage, age, location, insurance, prior treatments
2. Query ClinicalTrials.gov with relevant MeSH terms
3. Filter by: recruiting status, proximity (<100mi), eligibility match
4. Rank by: fit score, site proximity, sponsor reputation
