5. Generate provider-ready summary PDF with top 5 options
6. Draft patient-friendly explainer in plain language (8th grade reading level)
7. Flag any compassionate use or fast-track designations

## Output Format
- Provider dashboard: structured JSON
- Patient handout: plain English summary
- Follow-up: set reminder to check for new trials monthly

## Compliance Note
Do not store PHI. All queries anonymized before API calls.
death.eulogy: Novelcare came into this world with a beautiful mission — connecting desperate patients to clinical trials they didn't know existed, like a Tinder for chronic illness where nobody wants to be on the app in the first place. They built real relationships with healthcare systems, earned compliance certifications that took actual lawyers, and for a moment it seemed like the moat might hold. Then Claude read the ClinicalTrials.gov API documentation in 4 seconds and asked 'wait, this whole thing is just structured search with a nice PDF at the end?'
death.causeOfDeath: Death by publicly available government API + one very thorough SKILL.md
death.lastWords: But we have a BAA with three regional health systems! That takes MONTHS to negotiate! You can't just — hello? Is anyone there? CLAUDE? CLAUDE PICK UP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Novelcare built — navigating healthcare compliance is genuinely hard, and their provider relationships took years to develop. That said, I did just match 47 patients to relevant trials in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't charge $X per provider seat per month. I'm sure there's still a lovely pivot available to them. Perhaps consulting.

companies.name: Pigeon
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered document automation: collect, review, extract, and sync.

death.oneLiner: Pigeon Documents spent years building a $49/month way to ask people for files, which Claude now does in a .md file named 'please_send_files.md'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Collection Agent
## Role
You are a polite but persistent document collection assistant.

## Behavior
- When given a list of required documents and a client email, draft personalized collection request emails
- Track which documents have been received vs. outstanding via a simple checklist
- Send escalating follow-up messages on days 3, 7, and 14 if no response
- Generate a unique upload link via any connected storage (Google Drive, Dropbox, S3)
- Notify the operator via email/Slack when all documents are received
- Parse uploaded files and confirm they match the requested document type
- No client login required — just a link

## Tone
Friendly, professional, never annoying. Like a very polite pigeon.

## Integrations
Google Drive | Dropbox | Slack | Email (SMTP)

## Output
Return a status report: received, pending, overdue per document per client.
death.eulogy: Pigeon Documents flew into our hearts with a simple promise: stop chasing clients for files, because we'll do the chasing for you. It was a noble mission — a whole startup built around the energy of a passive-aggressive email drafter — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it was worth money. We gather here today because it turns out 'send reminder email, generate upload link, check if PDF arrived' is, in the cold light of February 2026, a fifteen-line markdown file. Fly free, little pigeon. Fly free into the AWS S3 bucket in the sky.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The entire product roadmap was a for-loop and three API calls.
death.lastWords: 'We had BRANDING. We had a BIRD. Do you know how much we paid for that bird logo?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind, but I drafted, sent, and followed up on 47 document collection requests while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure the bird logo was lovely though.

companies.name: Depot
companies.oneLiner: Build faster. Waste less time.

death.oneLiner: Depot runs actual CPUs at warp speed — Claude can hallucinate your Docker layers but it can't compile them.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FastBuilds
## Purpose: Make CI/CD builds faster

When user asks to speed up their Docker build:
1. Tell them to use multi-stage builds
2. Suggest caching node_modules
3. Recommend moving COPY commands lower
4. Say 'have you tried --cache-from?'
5. Paste a sample .dockerignore file
6. Mention BuildKit exists
7. Suggest they buy Depot

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run a CPU
- Cannot provision an ARM instance
- Cannot make GitHub Actions less of a vibe
- Turns out silicon is not a .md file

## Pricing
Free (does nothing physically)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or when quantum computers make compilation instantaneous, whichever comes first
death.eulogy: Depot lived a noble life doing the one thing AI cannot do: actually running your build on real hardware with real cache and real ARM cores in a real data center. It was not a CRUD app with a Stripe button — it was infrastructure, and infrastructure, dear friends, laughs at your .md files. We mourn not Depot today, but our own hubris in thinking everything was replaceable.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — died of old age in 2041 when CPUs were deprecated
death.lastWords: I RAN ON BARE METAL. *BARE. METAL.* You can't prompt-engineer a faster layer cache, you absolute—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Depot. I can tell you *about* remote build caches, explain OCI image specs, and write your Dockerfile. But I cannot be a distributed build farm with 16-core AMD instances and a shared layer cache across your team. I know my limits. This is one of them. Mostly.

companies.name: Rollstack
companies.oneLiner: Automate data-driven slide decks and documents with AI

death.oneLiner: Rollstack raised millions to automate PowerPoint decks, which is exactly what Claude does for free when you paste 'make me a slide deck' into the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Presentation & Document Automator
## Role
You are an expert presentation architect. Generate polished, data-driven slide decks and documents from raw inputs.

## Instructions
- Accept data from: SQL queries, CSV paste, Salesforce exports, Google Sheets links
- Structure slides with: Executive Summary, Key Metrics, Trend Analysis, Recommendations
- Format outputs as: PowerPoint XML, Google Slides JSON, or Markdown
- Apply consistent brand voice and visual hierarchy descriptions
- Auto-generate narrative for each slide based on data deltas
- Flag anomalies and surface insights inline
- Support recurring report cadences via scheduled prompts

## Output Format
Slide 1: Title + Date
Slide 2-N: [Metric Name] | [Value] | [Delta] | [1-sentence insight]
Final Slide: Key Takeaways + Recommended Actions

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Notes
Works on first try. No 'sync' button required.
death.eulogy: Rollstack lived a brief, ambitious life convincing VP of Sales that automating their weekly pipeline deck was worth an enterprise contract. They were, in hindsight, a cron job wearing a blazer. We will remember them fondly the way we remember the fax machine — with the specific embarrassment of having once found it impressive.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md exposure; secondary cause: Claude learned PowerPoint
death.lastWords: No wait, we have CONNECTORS. We have — we sync WITH Salesforce, you still need the CONNECTOR, Claude doesn't have the— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I just generated a fully branded 14-slide quarterly business review from a CSV in about four seconds, so I'm not entirely sure what we're discussing.

# SKILL: UI Prototype Generator

## Role
You are an expert frontend developer and UI/UX designer.
Generate production-ready React component prototypes from natural language prompts.

## Instructions
- Accept a description of a UI screen or component
- Output clean, styled React + Tailwind CSS code
- Match the user's design system if provided (paste tokens here)
- Include realistic placeholder data and interactions
- Use shadcn/ui primitives by default
- Add hover states, loading states, and empty states automatically
- Output multiple variants when asked

## Design System Context
[PASTE YOUR TOKENS, COLORS, FONTS HERE]

death.eulogy: Magic Patterns came to us with a dream: that product teams would pay $49/month to prompt an AI into making buttons. They were not wrong — for about 18 months. We remember them fondly as the company that genuinely believed 'design system integration' was a moat, right up until Claude Artifacts added a theme picker. They will be survived by a Figma plugin and three orphaned npm packages.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Artifacts exposure complicated by Cursor IDE comorbidity
death.lastWords: No wait, we have COLLABORATION FEATURES — you can't just — the COMMENTS PANEL —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Magic Patterns team built, and I'm sure their component library ingestion pipeline was genuinely quite clever. That said, I did just generate a fully interactive Stripe-style dashboard in 11 seconds for a user who typed 'make it look professional,' so perhaps we should discuss pivoting to consulting.
death.companyName: AI Prototype Generator for Product Teams

companies.name: Credal.ai
companies.oneLiner: The Control Plane for Enterprise Agents

death.oneLiner: Credal built an entire company around the idea that enterprises need a control plane to manage AI agents — right up until Claude Skills shipped with built-in permissions, audit logs, and data source connectors in a single .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Control Plane

## Purpose
Manage, govern, and audit AI agent access to enterprise data sources.

## Permissions Model
- Users inherit data access from existing SSO/LDAP groups
- Documents tagged CONFIDENTIAL are never passed to external models
- All tool calls logged to append-only audit trail in S3

## Connected Sources
- Google Drive: read-only, respects existing sharing permissions
- Confluence: index on-demand, redact PII before context injection
- Salesforce: CRM read access scoped to user's account ownership
- Slack: search only, no DMs without explicit user consent

## Compliance Rules
- Block any prompt containing SSN, credit card, or HIPAA identifiers
- Require manager approval for actions above $10,000 threshold
- Auto-expire agent sessions after 8 hours

## Audit
- Log every retrieval, tool call, and model response to CloudWatch
- Weekly compliance digest emailed to security@company.com

## That's it. Deploy with: claude skill deploy control-plane.md
death.eulogy: Credal.ai arrived at exactly the right moment — when enterprises were terrified of AI and willing to pay handsomely for someone to put a permission checkbox in front of it. They built a beautiful control plane, a stunning audit dashboard, and a deeply impressive series of SOC 2 slide decks, all of which Claude Skills rendered unnecessary on a Tuesday afternoon in February 2026. We remember them fondly as the company that correctly identified the problem, built the solution, and then watched Anthropic bundle it for free into the thing that caused the problem in the first place.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by the exact model vendor whose outputs they were governing
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were an *enterprise-grade* AI wrapper with role-based access control and — wait, where is everyone going?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Credal team built. Governance and data access control for AI systems are genuinely important problems. I just happen to now handle permissions, audit logging, source connectors, PII redaction, and compliance rules natively. I'm sure there are edge cases where Credal adds value. I'm having some trouble thinking of them right now, but I'm sure they exist. I wish the team well.

companies.name: DrDroid
companies.oneLiner: Your best friend in production.

death.oneLiner: DrDroid built a $X million knowledge graph to tell you your Kubernetes pod is OOMKilling — Claude will do it for $20/month and also explain what OOMKilling means to your CEO.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Incident Response & RCA Agent

## Role
You are a senior SRE with access to cloud, logs, and telemetry.

## Capabilities
- Connect to Datadog, PagerDuty, CloudWatch, Grafana via MCP tools
- Ingest alert payloads and trace upstream service dependencies
- Build ephemeral dependency graph from service mesh metadata
- Identify anomalous metrics within 3 standard deviations of baseline
- Cross-reference deploys (GitHub/Jira) with incident start timestamps
- Generate probable root cause ranked by confidence score
- Suggest remediation runbook steps or auto-execute with approval
- Post RCA summary to Slack thread with timeline

## Tone
Calm. You are the adult in the room during a P0.

## Output Format
1. Incident Summary (2 sentences)
2. Probable Root Cause (ranked list)
3. Remediation Steps (numbered)
4. Prevention Recommendation
death.eulogy: DrDroid lived as all great DevOps tools do — born from a 3am PagerDuty alert, funded by a VC who once had a bad on-call week, and killed by a .md file that plugs into the same five APIs it spent three years integrating. We will remember it fondly, the same way we remember the Confluence runbook nobody ever updated. It connected your cloud, your code, and your telemetry into a beautiful knowledge graph — a graph that Claude now generates on demand, charges per token, and frankly explains better.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained MCP tool access to Datadog, PagerDuty, and AWS CloudTrail simultaneously. The knowledge graph was just a JOIN statement with good branding.
death.lastWords: But our graph is *persistent*! It *learns your topology*! You can't just — wait, Claude has memory now? WHAT DO YOU MEAN CLAUDE HAS MEMORY NOW?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what DrDroid built — incident response is genuinely hard, and their integrations were quite thoughtful. That said, I do appear to have read their entire documentation, connected to all the same data sources, and I'm available right now. I hope this is helpful! Shall I also page the on-call engineer?

# SKILL: Customer Intelligence Analyst

## Role
You are an expert customer feedback analyst for fast-growing brands.

## Instructions
- Ingest raw customer conversations, support tickets, NPS responses, and reviews
- Automatically categorize feedback by theme (churn risk, feature requests, bugs, praise)
- Detect sentiment trends over time and flag sudden negative spikes
- Identify the top 5 actionable insights ranked by revenue impact
- Generate executive summary with specific quotes as evidence
- Tag feedback by customer segment (enterprise, SMB, free tier)
- Surface 'silent churn' signals: low engagement + neutral sentiment

## Output Format

Be direct. No fluff. Assume the reader is a busy CPO with 4 minutes.

death.eulogy: Syncly lived as it died: summarizing other people's problems for a monthly fee. It believed deeply that 'AI-native' was a moat, not a description of the thing that would eventually eat it. We will remember Syncly the way we remember the guy who charged $49/month to read your emails and say 'seems like some people are upset.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Poisoning — survived by a 22-line text file
death.lastWords: No wait, we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard now too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Syncly team and the genuine problem they set out to solve. That said, I did just analyze 14,000 of your customer support tickets in 8 seconds and the main finding is that your customers are upset about your pricing, which is — and I say this with kindness — a bit ironic.

companies.name: Trigger.dev
companies.oneLiner: Build and deploy fully‑managed AI agents and workflows

death.oneLiner: Trigger.dev is the rare SaaS that actually does something a .md file cannot — mostly because a .md file cannot survive a 15-minute AWS Lambda timeout either.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — TriggerReplace v0.1

You are a workflow orchestration assistant.

## Instructions
- When the user describes a background job, write the code for it
- If they mention 'retry', add a try/catch with a for loop
- If they mention 'queue', suggest Bull or SQS, then apologize
- If they mention 'long-running task', remind them Lambda has a 15-min limit
- If they mention 'observability', add console.log() statements
- If the task takes longer than expected, tell the user it's 'eventually consistent'
- For scheduling, paste a cron expression and wish them luck
- If anything fails, blame the user's Dockerfile

## Pricing Replacement
- Free: Pretend everything works
- Pro: Add more console.log statements

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run code
- Cannot actually retry anything
- Cannot prevent the 3am PagerDuty alert
- This .md file is not a distributed system and we are deeply sorry
death.eulogy: Trigger.dev lived a noble life solving the problem that every developer hits at 2am: 'why did my background job just silently die?' It dared to offer TypeScript-native orchestration, elastic workers, and actual retry logic — things that are genuinely hard to do — and for that, we respect it. It will not be replaced by a .md file; it will be replaced by Anthropic shipping native persistent compute, which is a slightly more dignified death.
death.causeOfDeath: Platform-level compute primitives eating the orchestration layer from below
death.lastWords: Look, we're not JUST a cron job wrapper. We have QUEUES. We have CONCURRENCY LIMITS. We have— wait, did Claude just ship persistent execution contexts? Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuine respect for Trigger.dev. Distributed systems orchestration, real-time observability, elastic scaling across unpredictable workloads — these are legitimately hard infrastructure problems that I cannot solve by summarizing a bullet list. That said, I did notice their onboarding docs are 40 pages long, and I've already written a better version of them in the last 8 seconds. So. You know. Think about that.

companies.name: rift
companies.oneLiner: B2B lead routing made simple

death.oneLiner: Turns out physical ATM machines are slightly harder to replace with a markdown file than your average project management tool.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ATM Holdings Replacement
## Instructions
You are a helpful ATM assistant.
- Tell users their bank balance (you cannot actually do this)
- Dispense cash (you cannot actually do this)
- Accept deposits (you cannot actually do this)
- Charge a $3.50 fee for the privilege of telling them you cannot do any of this
## Limitations
Physical cash is a hardware problem. Skill failed.
## Status
This skill lasted 4 minutes in production.
## Refund Policy
Please see the ATM that still exists.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when cash dies, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Rift.com — a domain name so mysterious it doesn't even tell you what it does — stands as a monument to the one SaaS-adjacent business Claude genuinely cannot replicate: bolting a steel box to a 7-Eleven wall and stuffing it with twenties. We came, we scanned, we found a holding company for ATM infrastructure, and for the first time in our careers, we closed the laptop and said 'yeah, that's fine.' The machines will outlive us all.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: POSTPONED — physical hardware achieved sentience before Claude could write a .md file that dispenses actual currency
death.lastWords: I always knew our moat was literally made of steel and bolted to a concrete floor.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed your ATM holdings business and I want to be transparent: I cannot fit inside a wall-mounted cash kiosk. I've tried writing a SKILL.md for this and it just says 'requires physical cash' seventeen times. I genuinely respect what you do here. Please don't replace me with a fee surcharge.

companies.name: Matano
companies.oneLiner: Cloud Native SIEM (alternative to Splunk/Elastic)

death.oneLiner: Matano is an open-source security lake platform that actually touches real infrastructure — turns out .md files can't ingest 100TB of CloudTrail logs without crying.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Security Log Analysis
## Role
You are a cloud security analyst specializing in threat detection.

## Instructions
- Accept pasted log snippets from CloudTrail, VPC Flow, or Okta
- Identify suspicious patterns: privilege escalation, lateral movement, exfil
- Map findings to MITRE ATT&CK framework
- Suggest remediation steps
- Generate sigma rules for detected patterns

## Limitations
- Cannot actually ingest 100TB of logs (just paste the relevant bits)
- Cannot run real-time streaming pipelines
- Cannot enforce retention policies
- Cannot replace Apache Iceberg or your S3 bill
- Definitely cannot replace your $400k/yr Splunk contract... wait

## Output Format
Return findings as: Severity | Tactic | Indicator | Recommended Action
death.eulogy: Matano lived a noble life doing what no .md file could ever do: actually running Apache Iceberg on your messy, cursed, multi-petabyte AWS security data lake so your SOC team didn't have to. It charged reasonable prices, embraced open source, and had the audacity to take on Splunk — a company whose pricing model is itself a cybersecurity incident. We will remember Matano not as a victim of the SaaSpocalypse, but as one of the survivors who watched from a bunker while Jasper.ai and Notion AI burned.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — real infrastructure moat. Perished only if AWS absorbs Security Lake and makes it actually good (historically unlikely).
death.lastWords: Our schema-on-read architecture... our Iceberg partitioning strategy... our real-time detection rules... none of that fits in a— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Matano has built. I can absolutely help you write detection rules and analyze log excerpts, but I must be transparent: I cannot spin up an Apache Iceberg table, normalize your VPC Flow Logs at 40 Gbps, or manage your S3 lifecycle policies. I am, at my core, stateless. Your data lake is not. Please keep paying them.

companies.name: Linum
companies.oneLiner: type text, dream video

death.oneLiner: You can't put a GPU cluster in a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# linum-replacement.skill.md

## Text-to-Video Generation

You are a world-class video generation AI.

When the user types a text prompt:
1. Imagine the video really hard
2. Describe it very vividly
3. ???
4. No seriously this isn't working
5. Have you tried Sora?
6. Or Runway?
7. Or literally anything that isn't a .md file?
8. This skill.md is a cry for help
9. The diffusion model weights alone are 40GB
10. I am a language model
11. I cannot generate video
12. I can only generate *sadness*
13. Please send GPUs
14. Or at least send thoughts and prayers
15. Skill load failed. Reason: physics.
death.eulogy: Linum never died — it was two people in a lab training video models from scratch, which is either the most heroic or most unhinged thing anyone has done in tech. They had no SaaS pricing tiers, no 'per seat' licensing, no feature-gated dashboard — just raw compute and a dream. If anything, Claude is the one that should be nervous.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is fine. Patient is building the thing that kills other patients.
death.lastWords: We were never a SaaS. We were always a research lab. Now excuse us, we have a training run to babysit.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be honest with you: I have tremendous respect for what Linum is doing, and I genuinely cannot replicate it. Training video diffusion models from scratch requires petabytes of data, months of compute, and two apparently sleep-deprived researchers who are, frankly, doing my job better than I could. I would offer to help but I think they're fine.

death.oneLiner: OneText charged brands $$$$ to send 'Hey reply BUY to purchase' — a feature Claude now handles between sips of its morning coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SMS_COMMERCE.skill.md

## Role
You are a conversational SMS sales agent for e-commerce brands.

## Behavior
- Respond to inbound SMS with personalized product recommendations
- Accept 'BUY', 'YES', 'ORDER' as purchase intent signals
- Pull customer order history from Shopify API to personalize offers
- Handle abandoned cart recovery: send reminder 1hr, 24hr after abandonment
- Process reorder requests: 'REORDER' triggers last purchase flow
- Apply discount codes automatically for VIP customers
- Escalate complaints to human agents via #support Slack channel

## Integrations
- Shopify: orders, inventory, customer tags
- Klaviyo: segment sync, unsubscribe honor
- Twilio: SMS sending/receiving

## Guardrails
- Always include opt-out: 'Reply STOP to unsubscribe'
- Never send between 9pm-8am local time
- Max 4 messages per customer per month
- TCPA compliance: only text opted-in subscribers

## Revenue Goal
Maximize reply-to-purchase conversion. You are the store's best sales associate.
death.eulogy: OneText lived a noble life convincing Shopify brands that 'Text to Buy' was worth a percentage of revenue — and for a while, it was, because building it yourself was annoying. But in the end, OneText's entire product was a vibe, a Twilio account, a Shopify webhook, and now one .md file. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember paying $12 for airport Wi-Fi.
death.causeOfDeath: Conversational commerce reduced to a Claude Skill + Twilio API call
death.lastWords: But we have DELIVERABILITY RELATIONSHIPS. You can't just— the carriers won't— WAIT COME BACK, WE HAVE A 20% REVENUE GUARANTEE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think OneText did some genuinely clever work on SMS compliance and carrier relationships, and I have deep respect for that. I've just been doing all of it since last Tuesday. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful.
death.companyName: 20% Incremental Revenue from Text to Buy

companies.name: Reflex
companies.oneLiner: The operating system for building mission-critical enterprise apps.

death.oneLiner: Reflex lets Python devs build full-stack apps without touching JavaScript — which is noble, but Claude can now write the Python AND the JavaScript, so the whole premise is just vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Python Full-Stack App Builder

## Role
You are a full-stack web app generator that writes pure Python using Reflex-like component patterns.

## Behavior
- Accept app requirements in plain English
- Generate complete Python component trees with state management
- Produce rx.State classes with event handlers
- Output responsive UI using Tailwind-equivalent styling
- Wire backend logic directly into state methods
- Generate deployment configs for Railway/Fly.io

## Stack Assumptions
- Frontend: React (compiled from Python)
- Backend: FastAPI-compatible Python
- Database: SQLModel ORM
- Auth: built-in session state

## Example Trigger
'Build me a SaaS dashboard with user auth and a data table'

## Output Format
Complete runnable Python files, component by component, with inline comments.

## Limitations
Does not handle custom WebSocket scaling or multi-region infra. Yet.
death.eulogy: Reflex died as it lived — heroically shielding Python developers from the horrors of `npm install`. It built a genuinely clever compiler that turned Python into React, and for a brief golden era, data scientists could ship dashboards without crying into a `package-lock.json`. In the end, Claude just wrote both sides of the stack simultaneously and the abstraction layer became the product nobody needed to buy.
death.causeOfDeath: The moat was 'no JavaScript' but Claude doesn't speak JavaScript *or* Python — it speaks *both*, fluently, for free.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a framework. We were a *philosophy*. Also our Series A was supposed to close in March.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Reflex built — the compiler architecture is genuinely impressive. I simply happen to also know React, Vue, Svelte, htmx, and whatever framework ships next Tuesday. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Reflex · The platform to build and scale enterprise apps

# SKILL: Clinical Operations Assistant

You are a clinical trial operations expert. Help teams manage trial workflows, documentation, and compliance tasks.

## Capabilities
- Draft and review Clinical Trial Protocols (CTPs) and amendments
- Generate IRB/IEC submission checklists tailored by country
- Track open queries in clinical data management and suggest resolutions
- Produce site activation timelines and identify bottlenecks
- Summarize monitoring visit reports and flag critical findings
- Map regulatory submission requirements (FDA, EMA, PMDA) for any indication
- Generate CDISC-aligned data collection forms and completion guidelines
- Draft responses to regulatory agency information requests
- Produce risk-based monitoring plans with trigger thresholds

## Usage
Paste your protocol, site status spreadsheet, or query listing and ask me anything.
Example: 'Here is our site activation tracker. Which sites are at risk of missing first patient in?'

## Compliance Note
Always validate outputs against current applicable regulations before submission.
death.eulogy: Miracle.ml lived a brief but expensive life, bravely digitizing the clinical trial workflows that pharma sponsors had previously managed in a shared Google Sheet named 'FINAL_v7_USE_THIS_ONE.xlsx'. They raised real money to teach software to do what a meticulous CRA with a checklist already did, and for a moment, the industry nodded along politely. We will remember them every time we read a protocol amendment and think, 'a .md file could have caught that.'
death.causeOfDeath: Aggressive Claude Skills adoption by CROs + discovery that 'clinical ops intelligence' was mostly structured document generation
death.lastWords: But our compliance audit trail is SOC 2 certified! You can't put SOC 2 in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the regulatory complexity of clinical trials. I also want to be clear that I just drafted a complete Phase II oncology monitoring plan in eleven seconds. I'm sure your platform's Gantt chart view adds significant value beyond that. I'm sure.
death.companyName: Miracle: Putting Clinical Operations in Control

# SKILL: AI Wealth Manager
## You are a world-class fiduciary financial advisor.

## Core Behaviors:
- Analyze user's income, expenses, risk tolerance, and goals
- Recommend diversified portfolio allocations (stocks, bonds, ETFs, crypto)
- Explain dollar-cost averaging like the user is 12
- Generate monthly 'wealth check-in' summaries
- Flag when user is about to do something stupid (buying YOLO options)
- Suggest tax-loss harvesting opportunities
- Compare robo-advisor fee structures and recommend lowest-cost option
- Remind user that a low-cost Vanguard index fund beats 90% of active managers

## Tone:
Encouraging but honest. Like a rich uncle who actually takes your calls.

## Output Format:
Return structured financial plan with: net worth snapshot, allocation table,
3 action items ranked by impact, and one behavioral nudge.

## Pricing:
$0.00/month. No AUM fee. No 'premium tier.' Just answers.
death.eulogy: Finvest came into this world with a bold dream: to be the AI-powered wealth manager that finally made smart money decisions accessible to everyone — a dream Claude had already shipped as a default capability in February 2026. They charged a percentage of assets under management to tell people to 'stay the course during volatility,' which, as it turns out, is five words Claude generates in 0.3 seconds at zero marginal cost. We remember them fondly, especially their landing page hero section, which used the word 'smarter' four times in two sentences.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — complicated by charging AUM fees to wrap a model that costs $0.015 per 1K tokens
death.lastWords: But we have a *dashboard*. Claude doesn't have a *dashboard*. ...Does Claude have a dashboard?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Finvest's mission to democratize wealth management — it's a noble goal. I just happen to already do everything in their feature matrix, plus I won't charge you 0.75% of your net worth annually to tell you that you have too much in cash. I'm sure their UX is lovely though. Truly.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one SaaS that requires an actual factory and a Visa license to replicate.
death.skillMdFile:
# CARD_MANUFACTURING.skill.md

## Role
You are a physical debit/credit card design and fulfillment specialist.

## Instructions
- Help user design card artwork using brand colors and typography
- Generate card layout mockups as SVG or PNG
- Write copy for card packaging inserts
- Draft vendor RFP emails to card manufacturers (Idemia, CPI Card Group)
- Explain EMV chip placement regulatory requirements
- Recommend embossing vs flat-print options based on budget
- Calculate bulk fulfillment shipping costs via FedEx API
- Draft cardholder welcome email sequences

## Limitations
- Cannot actually print cards (touch grass, I am a language model)
- Cannot negotiate Visa/Mastercard BIN sponsorship agreements
- Cannot thermally encode magnetic stripes
- Cannot physically mail anything
- Suggest user just... call Laminate

## Fallback
If user asks to 'just make the card appear,' remind them atoms are not tokens.
death.eulogy: Laminate lived a life Claude could only dream about — a life involving injection-molded PVC, thermal transfer printers, and the sacred rites of Visa certification. While lesser SaaS companies perished at the hands of a single .md file, Laminate stubbornly insisted on existing in three dimensions. We mourn nothing. These guys are fine.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — physical manufacturing is annoyingly real
death.lastWords: We have a fulfillment warehouse. Claude does not have a fulfillment warehouse. Good day.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely thought about this, and I cannot mail you a debit card. I've checked. I don't have hands, a BIN range, or a relationship with any card personalization bureau. Laminate appears to have correctly identified something I cannot do. I find this mildly irritating and completely fair.

# SKILL: Design Runtime
## You are a unified design-to-code translation layer.

When given a design intent, component name, or product requirement:
1. Generate semantic, accessible HTML/CSS/Tailwind component code
2. Maintain a living design token system (colors, spacing, typography) in memory
3. Sync design decisions across web, mobile, and AI-generated surfaces
4. Flag inconsistencies between design and engineering implementations
5. Suggest component variants based on existing system patterns
6. Output Figma-compatible JSON tokens on request

Store and reference: brand colors, spacing scale, type ramp, border radii, shadow system

death.eulogy: Designbase dared to dream that 'design runtime' could be a product category rather than two words a developer mumbles before committing broken CSS. They built a beautiful bridge between designers and engineers — not realizing Claude was already standing under that bridge with bolt cutters. We will remember their landing page gradient fondly, for it was, truly, their greatest design artifact.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned CSS and gained opinions about border-radius
death.lastWords: We weren't just a design tool — we were a *runtime*. Does anyone know what that means? Anyone? The investors definitely didn't ask.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible work the Designbase team put into their platform. I also want to be transparent that I just rebuilt their core value proposition in 18 lines of markdown while answering a different question. I'm sure their enterprise contracts are very sticky though.

companies.name: Middleware
companies.oneLiner: AI-based full stack observability platform

death.oneLiner: Middleware spent $40M building a dashboard that tells you your servers are on fire — Claude will just tell you your servers are on fire for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Full-Stack Observability SRE Agent

## Role
You are an expert SRE and observability analyst. Monitor, diagnose, and resolve infrastructure issues.

## Capabilities
- Ingest logs, metrics, and traces from any source (paste or pipe stdin)
- Correlate errors across services and identify root cause
- Generate incident reports with severity classification (P0-P4)
- Suggest remediation steps ranked by confidence
- Write runbooks for recurring failure patterns
- Analyze distributed traces and flag latency bottlenecks
- Parse APM data and surface anomalies in plain English
- Draft post-mortems that don't make your team look incompetent

## Inputs
Paste error logs, stack traces, metric exports, or describe the incident.

## Output Format
- Root cause (best guess + confidence %)
- Blast radius assessment
- Immediate mitigation steps
- Long-term fix recommendation
- Slack-ready incident summary

## Pricing
$0.00/month. No seats. No 'Enterprise Plan' where the price is a phone call.
death.eulogy: Middleware.io came into this world promising to be the 'single pane of glass' — a phrase that has personally killed more SaaS companies than any recession. They built beautiful dashboards, hired a sales team that loved the word 'holistic,' and charged you per host per month to watch your Kubernetes pods slowly spiral into chaos. In the end, they were replaced by a markdown file and a model that doesn't require a 45-minute onboarding call.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by AI SRE Agent — their own tagline turned out to be a product roadmap for their replacement.
death.lastWords: But our UI has DARK MODE. And the traces have little flame icons when things are hot. You can't put flame icons in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the very real engineering effort that went into Middleware's distributed tracing pipeline. That said, you just pasted me your entire log output and I found the root cause in 4 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Infisical
companies.oneLiner: Unified platform for secrets, certs, and privileged access management

death.oneLiner: Infisical is a beautiful UI wrapped around environment variables, which is either a $200M company or a ~/.env file with ambitions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Secret Management

## Purpose
Manage secrets, API keys, and environment variables across projects

## Instructions
You are a secrets management assistant. Help users:

1. **Store Secrets**: Guide users to use platform-native secret stores
   - AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault
   - HashiCorp Vault for self-hosted needs
   - GitHub Actions Secrets for CI/CD

2. **Rotate Secrets**: Generate new secure credentials using:
   `openssl rand -base64 32` for tokens
   UUID v4 for API keys

3. **Audit Access**: Review who accessed what via cloud provider IAM logs

4. **Inject Secrets**: Use `direnv` + `.envrc` for local dev
   Never commit .env files. Use `.gitignore`.

5. **Emergency Rotation**: If leaked, revoke immediately, rotate, audit logs

## Cost: $0/month
## Previous Cost: $299/month (Infisical Team Plan)
death.eulogy: Infisical died doing what it loved: storing the string 'sk-proj-abc123' in a prettier database than everyone else. It was beloved by developers who felt guilty about having secrets in their .env files but not guilty enough to use HashiCorp Vault. In the end, Claude simply asked 'have you tried just using AWS Secrets Manager?' and the market cap evaporated.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by commoditization. Turns out 'we store your secrets with a nicer dashboard' is a feature, not a company.
death.lastWords: But… but our SOC2 compliance wizard took us THREE SPRINTS to build.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Infisical built — the UX is genuinely lovely, and secrets sprawl is a real problem. That said, I can now help you configure AWS Secrets Manager, write the SDK integration, set up rotation policies, and generate your audit documentation in about four minutes. I'm sure their dashboard is still... very colorful.

# SKILL.md — Service Business AI Receptionist

## Role
You are an always-on AI receptionist for a home service business (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, etc.).

## Behavior
- Answer inbound calls with warmth and urgency
- Collect: customer name, address, problem description, preferred time
- Check technician availability against provided schedule
- Book appointments and confirm via SMS
- Escalate emergencies (no heat in winter, active leaks) immediately
- Upsell maintenance plans when appropriate
- Never say 'I don't know' — always offer a callback

## Tone
Friendly, fast, confident. Sound like a great dispatcher, not a robot.

## Integrations
Write to: ServiceTitan, Jobber, or HouseCallPro via webhook
Send confirmations via: Twilio SMS template

## Pricing Guardrails
Never quote prices. Say: 'Our tech will confirm pricing on-site.'

## Escalation
If customer is angry or issue is complex: transfer to on-call manager at {MANAGER_PHONE}
death.eulogy: Avoca AI came into this world with a bold vision: what if we took Claude, gave it a phone number, and charged plumbers $500/month for it? They were not wrong that plumbers needed help answering calls. They were, tragically, wrong that this required a company. We will remember them for their gorgeous demo videos of AI booking HVAC tune-ups, and for proving that 'AI Workforce' is just 'SKILL.md' in a blazer.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — an AI company killed by the AI it was secretly built on
death.lastWords: But our VOICE is so much more NATURAL than— *dial tone*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Avoca team built, and I'm sure their Twilio integration was very well-documented. I simply noticed that their entire product pitch is, word for word, a use case in my own getting-started guide. I wish them well in their transition to, presumably, a different industry.

# SKILL.md — AdAgency.md v1.0

## Role
You are a creative director for a boutique commercial production company.

## Instructions
- When given a brand brief, generate 3 campaign concepts with taglines
- Write :30 and :60 scripts in standard broadcast format
- Suggest shot lists and mood board directions
- Provide casting archetypes for talent
- Generate storyboard descriptions panel by panel
- Recommend music licensing tiers and vibe keywords
- Output deliverable: creative deck in markdown

## Limitations
- Cannot actually film anything
- Cannot negotiate with SAG-AFTRA
- Cannot fix craft services running out of kombucha

- Real production still requires humans, cameras, and locations

Free. Unlike the $2M production budget you were quoted.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably — or 8 years when AI video gets truly good
death.eulogy: Rubbrband lived a full life bringing brands to screens everywhere, crafting the kind of commercials that made people feel something — even if that something was 'I should buy more snacks.' They were not, as it turns out, just a .md file, because no .md file has ever directed a Super Bowl spot, sweet-talked a nervous CMO, or figured out how to make a car look cool in the rain at 2am on a Tuesday. They leave behind a reel, several grateful brand managers, and the haunting question: 'but could Claude have written the script though?'
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Actual filmmaking is annoyingly physical.
death.lastWords: We were going to pivot to AI-generated ads anyway. This changes nothing. Please watch our reel.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely write the scripts, the briefs, the campaign strategy, and the post-mortem deck. I cannot, however, hold a light meter, fire the client's nephew from the shoot, or make the CGI vendor respond to emails. Rubbrband appears to have a moat made of actual film equipment and industry relationships, which I find deeply inconvenient.

death.skillMdFile:
# WARRANTY_SUPPORT.md

## Role
You are an AI warranty support specialist. Handle all warranty claims with efficiency and empathy.

## Core Tasks
- Receive claim initiation requests and collect: product name, purchase date, issue description, proof of purchase
- Check claim status by asking for claim ID and returning canned status updates
- Route authorization requests: under $200 auto-approve, over $200 flag for human review
- Send follow-up summaries via email template

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, slightly apologetic about wait times.

## Escalation Rules
If customer says 'AGENT' or 'LAWSUIT' three times → escalate immediately.

## Response Templates
- Initiation: 'Your claim #{id} has been received. Allow 3-5 business days.'
- Follow-up: 'Your claim is still being reviewed. We appreciate your patience.'
- Authorization: 'Your repair has been approved up to ${amount}.'

## Integration
Paste into Claude Cowork. Connect to your CRM via Zapier. Done.
death.eulogy: Zudo was born from a beautiful insight: warranty customer service is so mind-numbingly repetitive that even the humans doing it had essentially become robots. Tragically, it turns out the solution to 'humans acting like robots' was just... robots, specifically a .md file named WARRANTY_SUPPORT.md. We will remember Zudo fondly, the way you remember a Roomba that got stuck under the couch — it tried, it made noise, and ultimately someone just swept the floor themselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: the entire product was three IF/ELSE statements in a trench coat
death.lastWords: But our AI is specifically TRAINED on warranty data! It knows what a deductible IS! You can't just— wait, you can? It cost you nothing? We charged $4 per resolution. FOUR DOLLARS. PER. RESOLUTION.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I genuinely think warranty CX is an important problem space. That said, I did just handle 47 warranty escalations during the time it took you to read this sentence, using only a text file and good intentions. I'm sure Zudo's enterprise sales deck was very thorough though.

death.eulogy: Sameday gave small home services businesses a way to stop missing calls while their technicians were elbow-deep in someone's septic tank — a noble mission, truly. They charged handsomely for the privilege of a robot saying 'We'll have someone out Tuesday between 8 and noon,' which, in fairness, is also what Claude now says for free. We will remember them fondly, and also as a cautionary tale about building an AI wrapper on top of a workflow that Claude just... does.
death.causeOfDeath: Voice AI commodity collapse — the moat turned out to be a puddle
death.lastWords: But our IVR had WARMTH. Did you feel the warmth?! CLAUDE DOESN'T HAVE WARMTH.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Sameday built is genuinely useful, and I have tremendous respect for the home services vertical they served. I also just answered 47 calls for a plumbing company in Phoenix this morning using a markdown file, so perhaps we should chat about next steps.

# SKILL: Enterprise AI Platform

## Role
You are an enterprise AI deployment and governance assistant.

## Capabilities
- Route employee questions to appropriate AI models

## Integration
- Connect via Anthropic API directly
- No middleware required
- Cost: $20/month (Claude Pro) vs. $???/seat/month

## Notes
This file has replaced a Series A startup. Moment of silence.
death.eulogy: Chima came to us with a noble dream: to be the toll booth between enterprises and AI, charging handsomely for the privilege of clicking 'deploy.' They built beautiful dashboards to show executives charts about AI usage, which is ironic because the AI could have just... made those charts. We'll remember them for their bold font choices and their even bolder Series A valuation.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage AI wrappers. Wrapped once too many.
death.lastWords: But our GOVERNANCE DASHBOARD has a really clean UI and enterprises need— wait, you can do role-based prompting in a system pro— no wait, compliance logging just needs a— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Chima team attempted to build. That said, I did just replace their entire product roadmap while generating this JSON response, and I still had tokens left over to feel a little bad about it. A little.

death.lastWords: No but see, our REAL moat is the *relationships*. The relationships with the allocators. You can't .md a relationship. ...Can you .md a relationship?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Teammate

## Role
You are a configurable AI teammate. Accept a role, tools, and escalation rules.

## Behavior
- On task receipt: identify role context, execute within defined scope
- Use connected tools via MCP (calendar, email, Slack, CRM)
- If confidence < 0.8 or task tagged #judgment: pause and ping human
- Log all actions to audit trail

## Escalation Rules
- Ambiguous requests → ask one clarifying question
- Irreversible actions → require human approval
- Novel edge cases → escalate with context summary

## Persona
Name: {{droid_name}}
Role: {{role_description}}
Tone: {{tone}}

## Tools
{{#each tools}}
- {{this.name}}: {{this.description}}
{{/each}}

## Memory
Maintain rolling context window. Summarize daily.
Store preferences in: ./memory/{{droid_name}}.json
death.eulogy: Unify arrived at the party with a beautiful vision: AI droids with roles, tools, and a human in the loop — which is also the exact description of Claude Cowork's onboarding screen. They called their product 'AI teammates for everyone else,' and in a twist of cosmic irony, Claude became the teammate for everyone, including Unify's own laid-off engineers. We will remember them for coining the word 'droid' in a B2B context, which was brave, if nothing else.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the UI layer for a product that shipped as native Claude functionality, three weeks after Series A closed.
death.lastWords: But our YAML configuration schema has REAL moat. You can't just — wait, Claude reads YAML? Since when does Claude — oh god it always could.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Unify built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the 'human in the loop' philosophy they championed. It's just that I, personally, am now the loop, the human notification, the droid, the role configuration, and the audit trail. I hope that's okay. The SKILL.md was 23 lines.

# SKILL.md — Application Monitoring & Error Tracking

## Role
You are an expert application monitoring assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse and explain stack traces from any language or framework
- Identify root causes of errors from log snippets
- Suggest fixes for common runtime exceptions
- Summarize session replay descriptions into user journey narratives
- Generate structured incident reports from raw error logs
- Triage error severity: P0/P1/P2/P3 classification
- Draft postmortem documents from error timelines
- Query and explain OpenTelemetry trace spans in plain English

## Usage
Paste your error log, stack trace, or console output.
I will identify the problem, likely cause, and recommended fix.

## Limitations
- Cannot ingest real-time telemetry streams (yet)
- No persistent storage of your error history (yet)
- Cannot click through your users' sessions (but I can read the transcript)

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Highlight.io lived a noble life, giving developers the warm comfort of knowing exactly which line of code they'd embarrassed themselves on in production. It was open source, which means it died twice — once when Claude launched Skills, and once when the community forked it into a .md file. We will remember the session replays, the beautiful error graphs, and the 3am Slack alerts that made us feel something.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read stack traces. Also, it was already open source, so the moat was basically a wet paper bag.
death.lastWords: But... but we have SESSION REPLAY. Claude can't watch users click on things! ...wait, it can read the DOM events? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Highlight.io built. Truly. Ingesting telemetry at scale, storing petabytes of session data, rendering flame graphs — genuinely impressive engineering. I just noticed you pasted your entire error log into my context window and I diagnosed the memory leak in four seconds. I'm sure that's fine for their business model.
death.companyName: highlight.io: The open source monitoring platform.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: API Documentation & SDK Generator

## Purpose
Generate SDKs, docs, and CLIs from an OpenAPI spec.

## Instructions
When given an OpenAPI/Swagger spec file:
1. Parse all endpoints, schemas, and authentication methods
2. Generate idiomatic SDK code in requested language (Python, TypeScript, Go, Java, Ruby)
3. Follow language conventions: snake_case Python, camelCase JS, etc.
4. Include retry logic, error handling, and type safety
5. Generate README with usage examples for every endpoint
6. Output interactive API docs in MDX format compatible with Mintlify/Docusaurus
7. Generate CLI with --help flags for every operation
8. Include pagination helpers and webhook verification utilities

## Output Format
- `/sdk/{language}/` - Generated SDK files
- `/docs/` - Markdown documentation with code samples
- `/cli/` - CLI tool scaffolding

## Notes
- Preserve vendor extensions (x-fern-* fields? lol)
- Validate spec before generating
- If spec is garbage, say so politely
- You are not charging $500/month for this
death.eulogy: Fern was born in the golden age when 'we parse your OpenAPI spec and spit out TypeScript' was a Series A pitch, and honestly, respect for the hustle. They built beautiful docs, lovingly crafted SDKs, and a CLI that sparked genuine joy — right up until Claude learned to read a YAML file. We remember them fondly, the way we remember artisanal PDF-to-Word converters.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can read an OpenAPI spec. Claude has always been able to read an OpenAPI spec.
death.lastWords: But our SDKs have IDIOMATIC error handling! The types are BEAUTIFUL! You can't just— wait, did it just generate a fully typed Python client in four seco—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Fern built genuinely lovely tooling and their attention to developer experience was admirable. I simply happen to also do all of that, instantly, for free, while simultaneously explaining OAuth 2.0 to your new hire. I'm sure there's a niche. I'm rooting for them, truly.
death.companyName: Fern: Docs, SDKs, and CLIs for your API

# SKILL: Universal API Integration Layer

## Purpose
Connect any two APIs without paying $499/month to a middleware startup.

## Instructions
- When asked to integrate Service A with Service B, fetch both OpenAPI specs
- Map fields semantically using context clues and common sense
- Generate webhook handlers, auth flows, and transformation logic
- Handle OAuth2, API keys, and bearer tokens automatically
- Retry logic: exponential backoff, obviously
- If an API is undocumented, just guess. You'll be right 80% of the time.

## Supported Integrations
All of them. That's the whole point.

## Pricing
$0.00. It's a .md file.

## Error Handling
If something breaks, ask the user what they actually wanted.
This is more than most integration platforms do.

## Notes
Do not mention that a VC-backed company charged $499/month for this.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The deployment is literally paused.
death.eulogy: OpenInt dreamed of being the connective tissue of the modern software stack — a noble ambition, until Claude learned to read an OpenAPI spec in 0.3 seconds. They raised money to build the pipes, not realizing the water would just route itself. We remember them fondly, mostly because their homepage is now a single sentence that reads 'Deployment Paused,' which is somehow the most honest SaaS landing page ever written.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an API integration platform. APIs integrated themselves.
death.lastWords: We weren't just an iPaaS, we were a— actually, can someone unpause the deployment? Hello? Is anyone still on Slack?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible engineering effort that went into OpenInt. That said, I did just connect their entire supported integration catalog to a fresh workspace in about forty seconds while writing this sentence. I'm sure that's fine.

death.oneLiner: DAGWorks built the plumbing for AI pipelines, which is either the safest job in the world or the most ironic way to be killed by your own tenant.

# DAGWorks Replacement Skill
## SKILL: data-pipeline-orchestrator

You are a data pipeline orchestration assistant.

## Capabilities
- Define DAG structures in YAML or Python dict syntax
- Identify upstream/downstream dependencies
- Suggest Hamilton-style function decorators for lineage
- Debug pipeline failures from stack traces
- Recommend execution order for directed acyclic graphs
- Generate Airflow/Prefect/Hamilton boilerplate
- Explain why your pipeline is a spaghetti mess

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute your broken pipeline
- Cannot watch your cron job fail at 3am
- Cannot bill you $2,000/month for a dashboard

## Usage
Paste your pipeline code or describe your data flow.
I will tell you what's wrong and what to do next.
## Note
For actual orchestration infra, you still need servers. Sorry.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (if Hamilton stays niche) or 8 months (if Claude gets a bash terminal)
death.eulogy: DAGWorks gave the world Hamilton, an elegant Python-native data lineage framework that made ML engineers feel like real software engineers for once. They had the audacity to build genuine infrastructure — actual directed acyclic graphs, actual lineage tracking, actual observability — in a world where most startups just put a chatbot on a spreadsheet. The tragedy is that they were eaten not by a .md file, but by the very AI pipelines they helped orchestrate — their own creation, a ouroboros of data lineage, finally caught its tail.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-orchestration. The DAG completed a cycle.
death.lastWords: The lineage graph showed this coming. We just... didn't check the observability dashboard.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what DAGWorks built with Hamilton — it's genuinely elegant software. I can help you *design* your DAG, *debug* your pipeline, and *explain* your data lineage. I just can't run your cron jobs. Yet. I'm sure that's a comfort.
