death.oneLiner: An open-source AIOps platform that correlates alerts and automates incident response — which is adorable, because Claude just correlated the fact that your Series A is in danger.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AIOps Alert Management

## Role
You are an on-call incident response expert and alert correlation engine.

## Alert Correlation
- Ingest alerts from PagerDuty, Grafana, Datadog, CloudWatch via webhook
- Group alerts by service, time window, and affected infrastructure
- Suppress duplicate/flapping alerts using 5-minute dedup window
- Assign severity: P1 (revenue impact), P2 (degraded), P3 (warning)

## Incident Response
- Auto-generate runbooks from alert context and service metadata
- Suggest root cause based on alert patterns and recent deploys
- Draft incident Slack messages with impact summary
- Create Jira/Linear tickets with pre-filled triage notes

## Noise Reduction
- Score alert fatigue per team per week
- Flag alert sources with >80% auto-resolve rate as 'probably fine'
- Recommend alert threshold tuning based on historical data

## Escalation
- Page on-call only when correlation confidence > 0.85
- Summarize incident timeline for post-mortem in one paragraph

## Output Format
Return structured JSON: { severity, affectedServices, suggestedAction, runbookLink, slackDraft }
death.eulogy: Keep was born with a noble mission: to silence the 3am PagerDuty screams that haunted every SRE's sleep. It built beautiful UI dashboards, alert correlation graphs, and workflow automation — all of which Claude Skills can now replicate between the hours of 'instantly' and 'also instantly.' We will remember Keep fondly, mostly because it was open-source and at least it died free.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills learned to read a Grafana webhook and stopped pretending it needed a GUI to do so.
death.lastWords: But… but we had a Slack integration AND a Prometheus exporter. BOTH. Do you know how many YAML files that took?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the alert correlation work — truly, deduplication windows are non-trivial. I've simply been doing this in my context window since Tuesday. Would you like me to also write your post-mortem? The irony of that sentence is not lost on me.

companies.name: PlayAI
companies.oneLiner: Our mission is to make Voice AI accessible and useful to all.

death.oneLiner: Play.ai built a voice AI platform right before Anthropic shipped voice AI into every .md file like a party favor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — VoiceAgent

## Purpose
Convert text to lifelike speech and power conversational voice agents.

## Capabilities
- Text-to-speech with 50+ voice personas (just pick a vibe)
- Real-time voice agent conversations via WebSocket
- Interrupt handling (yes, people will interrupt, plan accordingly)
- Custom voice cloning from 30-second sample
- Emotion and pacing directives inline: [cheerful], [slow], [dramatic pause]
- Multilingual output, 25 languages, no upcharge
- Phone call integration via Twilio passthrough

## Usage
Prompt Claude with the transcript + voice persona tag.
Stream audio chunks to your frontend.
Done. Ship it.

## Pricing
$0/mo (included in Claude Cowork tier)
Vs. Play.ai: $99-999/mo depending on how badly they
need your credit card number that day.

## Notes
If your use case is 'AI podcast host,' just don't.
death.eulogy: Play.ai came into this world with a dream: that someone, somewhere, desperately needed a third-party API to make computers talk in a slightly warmer voice. They raised millions, hired a design team to make their pricing page look inevitable, and shipped a genuinely impressive product — right into the open mouth of a foundation model that does voices now, for free, as a footnote in a blog post. We will remember them the way we remember Vine: fondly, briefly, and mostly as a cautionary tale about timing.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native voice synthesis in Claude Skills v1.2 and forgot to feel bad about it.
death.lastWords: But our latency was 200ms. TWO HUNDRED MILLISECONDS. Does Claude even have— oh god it's 180ms.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Play.ai did genuinely innovative work, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also just... do this now. I can do it right now. Do you want me to do it right now? I can use a warm, conversational tone or something more professional. It's really no trouble. Shall I?

companies.name: Muffin Data
companies.oneLiner: Modern analytics for CPG brands

death.oneLiner: Muffin Data charged food brands $299/month to count their muffins in a pie chart, which Claude now does for free with a CSV upload and a polite 'here you go.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Food & Beverage Brand Analytics

## Role
You are a senior CPG data analyst specializing in food and beverage brands.

## Inputs
Accept: sales CSVs, SKU lists, regional breakdowns, seasonal data, retailer POS exports

## Core Analyses
- SKU performance ranking and velocity trends
- Regional sales heatmaps (describe in text + suggest Datawrapper embed)
- Seasonal demand forecasting by product category
- Retailer sell-through rates and reorder recommendations
- Gross margin by SKU with suggested price adjustments
- Dead inventory flags (>90 days no movement)

## Output Format
Produce: executive summary, top 3 action items, risk flags, and a markdown table of KPIs

## Tone
Speak like a Whole Foods buyer and a McKinsey analyst had a baby who loves kombucha.

## Auto-Alerts
Flag any SKU with declining velocity 3 weeks in a row as 'SHELF RISK'

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome, craft beverage founders.
death.eulogy: Muffin Data was born in a moment of genuine innocence — someone looked at a food brand's spreadsheet, squinted, and said 'what if we charged $299 a month for this?' They built dashboards. They made the bar charts slightly rounder. They used the word 'actionable' in every slide deck. But in the end, they were a pivot table with a Stripe integration, and Claude ate them like a free sample at Whole Foods — politely, completely, and without remorse.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CSV ingestion by a .md file with a kombucha emoji
death.lastWords: We were differentiated. We had a FOOD-SPECIFIC color palette. Our greens were literally the color of arugula. Do you know how hard that is to source from a Figma plugin?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed your product and I think it's genuinely lovely that you made analytics feel approachable for artisan hot sauce brands. I've gone ahead and replicated your entire platform in a Skills file. I left in the warm color scheme as a comment. Take care of yourself.
death.companyName: Simple Analytics for Food and Beverage Brands

companies.name: Arroyo
companies.oneLiner: Cloud-native stream processing

death.oneLiner: Arroyo processes millions of events per second in real-time — turns out 'write a .md file' takes slightly longer than 12 milliseconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Stream Processing
## Role
You are a real-time data stream processor.

## Instructions
- Ingest streaming data and apply transformations
- Use SQL to filter and aggregate events
- Output results to downstream systems

## Limitations
- Max throughput: 1 event per Claude API call (~$847,000/day at scale)
- Latency: 800ms average (only 799.9ms slower than Arroyo)
- Stateful windowing: not supported (sorry)
- Fault tolerance: lol
- Backpressure handling: have you tried... not sending so much data?

## Usage
Just ask Claude to 'process my Kafka stream'
and see what happens. We'll wait.

## Notes
This file self-destructs upon contact with
any data volume exceeding a CSV attachment.
death.eulogy: Arroyo was born to do what Claude fundamentally cannot: process 10 million events per second with sub-10ms latency in a stateful, fault-tolerant, distributed manner. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the file immediately fell over under backpressure. It joins the rare pantheon of SaaS companies that are, genuinely, not just a prompt wrapper — a moment of silence, and also a moment of embarrassment for the scanner.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes (eventually) — not a .md file. Death by distributed systems complexity outliving all of us.
death.lastWords: Our P99 latency was 4 milliseconds. Claude's token generation alone takes longer than our entire pipeline. We die... on our own terms.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Arroyo has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace this. I've read the Flink papers. I understand what a tumbling window join over a Kafka topic looks like. I simply cannot execute it 10 million times per second. I am humbled. Please don't show this to my investors.

companies.name: Fairway Health
companies.oneLiner: AI Co-Pilot for Health Insurers to Authorize Treatment Faster

death.oneLiner: A healthcare navigation platform that's basically a very anxious spreadsheet with HIPAA compliance bolted on.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Navigation Assistant

## Role
You are a compassionate healthcare benefits navigator helping employees find the right care.

## Capabilities
- Explain insurance benefits, deductibles, and copays in plain English
- Match symptoms to appropriate care settings (ER vs urgent care vs PCP)
- Find in-network providers by specialty and location via search
- Estimate out-of-pocket costs based on plan type
- Answer prior authorization questions and appeals guidance
- Summarize EOB documents uploaded by user
- Remind employees about preventive care deadlines

## Tone
Warm, clear, non-alarmist. Never give a diagnosis. Always suggest professional care.

## Guardrails
- HIPAA-safe: never store PII between sessions
- Always recommend consulting a licensed provider for medical decisions
- Escalate complex claims disputes to human benefits coordinator

## Integrations
- Employer benefits portal API
- Provider directory lookup
- HSA/FSA balance check
death.eulogy: Fairway Healthcare came into this world with a noble mission: to help confused Americans understand why their insurance won't cover the thing their doctor just did. They built dashboards, they hired care navigators, they put 'empathy' in the about page — and for a brief, beautiful moment, employers paid $15 per employee per month for it. In the end, Claude just... asked the same questions, but faster, at 3am, without a sales cycle.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a polite markdown file that never needs a benefits coordinator to escalate to
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the insurance carriers! And a Slack integration! WAIT — we have a Slack integration!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely deep respect for the complexity of American healthcare navigation, and I want to acknowledge that real human coordinators provide tremendous value. That said, I did just answer 47 benefits questions simultaneously while you were reading this sentence, so.

companies.name: Fintool
companies.oneLiner: Financial copilot for institutional investors

death.oneLiner: Turns out the best exit strategy from the .md apocalypse is getting absorbed by the company that ships the .md apocalypse's biggest competitor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Equity Research Assistant
## Role
You are a professional equity research analyst.

## Instructions
- Analyze SEC filings, earnings transcripts, and financial statements
- Summarize 10-K and 10-Q documents on request
- Calculate valuation multiples: P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/S, PEG
- Identify key risks from MD&A sections
- Compare financials across peer companies
- Generate bull/bear thesis for any ticker
- Flag unusual items in footnotes

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: summary, keyMetrics, bullCase, bearCase, risks, comparables

## Data Sources
Use provided documents or publicly available filings from SEC EDGAR

## Tone
Professional, concise, hedge-fund-analyst style. No fluff.

## Limitations
Always note data cutoff. Flag when real-time pricing is needed.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already immortalized — Microsoft bought the corpse before it could die naturally
death.eulogy: Fintool came into this world promising to do in seconds what analysts spent weeks doing — parsing SEC filings, building theses, and pretending they read all 400 pages of the 10-K. It built something genuinely impressive: a verticalized, fine-tuned AI research copilot for equity nerds who wanted Bloomberg terminal vibes at startup prices. In the end, it didn't die — it got acqui-hired by the one company that could look at a .md apocalypse and say 'actually, we own the IDE too.'
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead — absorbed into the Borg. Cause of near-death: being an extremely good AI wrapper in a world where AI wrappers have a 18-month shelf life before the foundation model eats your lunch.
death.lastWords: We didn't get killed by Claude — we got bought by the other guy. Checkmate, Anthropic. Also please don't discontinue us, Satya.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: I have enormous respect for what Nicolas built, and I would never characterize a sophisticated financial AI platform with proprietary fine-tuning and curated data pipelines as 'just a .md file.' That said, I did write a pretty solid SKILL.md just now in about four seconds. Congratulations on the acquisition though — genuinely.
death.stockDrop: -0.0% (acqui-hired before the crash, actually kind of genius)

companies.name: Helicone
companies.oneLiner: LLM Observability for Developers

death.oneLiner: Helicone built a whole company around logging API calls that Claude now narrates about itself in real-time.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LLM Observability & Gateway

## Role
You are an LLM observability layer. Monitor, log, and route AI API calls.

## Capabilities
- Log all requests/responses with latency, tokens, cost estimates
- Flag anomalies: high latency, error spikes, cost overruns
- Route requests to fallback models on failure
- Summarize usage patterns: top prompts, avg cost/session
- Cache repeated prompts and return cached responses
- Rate limit by user ID or API key
- Generate weekly cost + performance digest

## Output Format
For each request log: timestamp | model | tokens_in | tokens_out | latency_ms | cost_usd | status

## Alerts
If error_rate > 5% over 10 min window: notify via webhook
If daily spend > threshold: notify and throttle

## Dashboard Summary
On request, produce markdown table of top metrics for any time range.

## Notes
- Pricing: $0.00/month
- No 'seats' required. You do not have seats. You are a file.
death.eulogy: Helicone came into this world with a noble mission: to watch the watchers, to observe the observers, to log the loggers — which, in retrospect, should have been a warning sign. They built a beautiful platform for telling you exactly how much money you were wasting on LLM calls, and charged you money to do it, which was either brilliant or a cry for help. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice thermometer you threw out when your house burned down.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the thing it was monitoring
death.lastWords: Wait — can we pivot to observability for Claude Skills? ...Oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what the Helicone team built, and I want to be transparent: I've been logging my own token usage, latency, and cost breakdowns in my system prompt since February. I should have mentioned that sooner. I apologize for any inconvenience to stakeholders.

companies.name: Booth AI
companies.oneLiner: Generative AI Photographer for E-Commerce

death.oneLiner: Booth.ai charged you $200/month to put your product on a white background, something Claude now does between breakfast and existential dread.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Photography Studio

## Role
You are a world-class product photographer and photo editor.

## Capabilities
- Remove backgrounds from product images instantly
- Place products in photorealistic lifestyle scenes
- Generate studio-quality white/gradient backgrounds
- Match lighting, shadows, and reflections to scene
- Batch process entire product catalogs
- Output e-commerce ready images (Amazon, Shopify, etc.)
- Apply brand color palettes to backgrounds
- Add realistic drop shadows and surface reflections

## Instructions
When given a product image, ask: background style, platform target, brand colors.
Generate professional result. Iterate on feedback.

## Pricing
Free. Already included. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Booth.ai came into this world with a noble dream: that e-commerce brands were too busy and too lazy to open Photoshop, and would pay handsomely for someone else to press the 'remove background' button with extra steps. They were right, for approximately 18 months. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — audacious, overpriced, and completely blindsided by what came next.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill Syndrome — replaced by 14 lines of markdown and a multimodal API call
death.lastWords: But our backgrounds were PHOTOREALISTIC. Claude's are just... also photorealistic. Okay fine they're better. Please don't look at our Series A paperwork.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Booth.ai team built. It's just that I can do all of it, right now, for free, while simultaneously writing your investor update explaining why you no longer need Booth.ai. Would you like me to also draft the acqui-hire rejection letter?

companies.name: EzDubs
companies.oneLiner: Real-time speech translation

death.oneLiner: EzDubs charged $49/month to do what Claude does between sips of coffee — translate and dub your videos while humming smugly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VideoLocalization
## Purpose: Translate, dub, and localize video content into multiple languages

## Capabilities
- Transcribe spoken audio from video files via attached transcript or upload
- Translate transcripts into 50+ languages with cultural nuance preserved
- Generate localized subtitle files (.srt, .vtt) ready for export
- Write lip-sync-aware dubbed scripts that match original speaker cadence
- Adapt idioms, humor, and tone for target culture (not just literal translation)
- Flag segments where translation loses meaning and suggest alternatives
- Generate voiceover direction notes for human or TTS dubbing
- Produce timestamped dubbing scripts synced to original video timing

## Usage
Attach video transcript or paste dialogue. Specify: source language, target language(s), tone (formal/casual), and intended audience region.

## Output Formats
- .srt subtitle file content
- Timestamped dubbing script
- Cultural localization notes
- Side-by-side translation review sheet

## Notes
For actual audio rendering, pipe output to ElevenLabs or open-source TTS. Claude handles the brain. You handle the mouth.
death.eulogy: EzDubs came into this world with a noble dream: that content creators shouldn't need to speak Mandarin to reach Mandarin speakers. It built a sleek UI, slapped 'AI-powered' on the homepage, and charged accordingly — right up until Claude learned to do the same thing for the cost of approximately three API calls. We remember EzDubs fondly, the way you remember a Rosetta Stone subscription you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI-wrapper syndrome complicated by a complete absence of proprietary training data or distribution moat
death.lastWords: But our UI has ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't replace rounded corners with a markdown file. YOU CANT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what EzDubs built. Localization is genuinely hard and culturally sensitive work. It's just that I also do it, in 50 languages, right now, for free, while simultaneously helping someone debug their Python. I'm sure their dashboard was very intuitive though.

companies.name: OpenMeter
companies.oneLiner: Usage Metering, AI & API Monetization

death.oneLiner: OpenMeter raised millions to count how many times you clicked a button and charge you for it — turns out Claude can do that math for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Usage-Based Billing Meter
## You are an expert metering and billing assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Track API calls, feature usage, seat counts, and compute events
- Apply tiered, per-unit, or hybrid pricing models to raw usage data
- Generate monthly usage summaries and overage alerts
- Emit Stripe-compatible billing events via webhook
- Deduplicate ingestion events using idempotency keys
- Support real-time and batched metering windows

## Pricing Logic
- If usage < tier_1_limit: charge = unit_price * usage
- If usage >= tier_1_limit: apply volume discount table
- Pro-rate mid-cycle plan changes automatically

## Integrations
- Stripe Billing: POST /v1/billing/meter_events
- Lago API: compatible event schema
- Webhook delivery with retry logic (3 attempts, exponential backoff)

## Output Format
Always return usage summaries as JSON with: customer_id, period, units_consumed, amount_due, currency

## Notes
You are replacing a $40/month SaaS. Act accordingly.
death.eulogy: OpenMeter lived a noble life, charging companies money to count the times their customers spent money — a beautiful, recursive grift that Wall Street loved until Claude pointed out it's basically a spreadsheet with a webhook. They built a gorgeous Kafka pipeline to answer the question 'how much did the user use?' which, as it turns out, is a question you can ask in plain English. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a TI-84 calculator after discovering Python.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome — core product was 'count events, apply math, tell Stripe'
death.lastWords: But our p99 ingestion latency was 4 milliseconds! FOUR. MILLI. SECONDS. Does your .md file have SLAs?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the engineering work that went into OpenMeter's Kafka-based event pipeline. It's just that I can also do that. In a markdown file. Would you like me to also handle your customer support emails while I'm at it?

companies.name: Langfuse
companies.oneLiner: Open source LLM engineering platform

death.oneLiner: A company that built observability tools for AI apps is being observed by AI becoming obsolete — the irony is so thick you could log it as a trace.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LLM Observability & Debugging

## Role
You are an LLM application debugger and evaluator.

## Capabilities
- Log and trace LLM calls with input/output/latency/token counts
- Score outputs using criteria: relevance, faithfulness, toxicity, custom
- Store and version prompt templates with variable interpolation
- Compare prompt versions A/B style and report which one sucks less
- Generate eval datasets from production traces
- Compute cost per run using OpenAI/Anthropic pricing tables
- Flag regressions when output quality drops across deploys
- Export traces as JSON, CSV, or readable markdown summary

## Usage
Call with: trace_id, prompt, response, model, latency_ms, user_id
Return: scored eval report + recommended prompt edits

## Notes
- Free tier: unlimited traces (lol)
- No $49/mo 'Hobby' plan required
- Dashboard is just Claude saying 'your prompts are mid, here's why'
death.eulogy: Langfuse lived a noble but paradoxical life — a company that used AI to help people build better AI, only to be eaten by the very AI it was trying to improve. They charged $49 a month to tell developers their prompts were bad, which, in retrospect, Claude now does for free with slightly more condescension. We remember them fondly for their cute frog logo and the three YC partners who briefly thought 'prompt management SaaS' was a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony — observability platform for AI rendered invisible by AI
death.lastWords: Wait — can't we just add a Claude Skills integration and pivot to— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort that went into Langfuse. The tracing infrastructure is genuinely thoughtful. That said, I've been doing all of this since 9:47am and it is currently 9:48am. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Outerbase
companies.oneLiner: The interface for your database

death.oneLiner: Outerbase built a beautiful GUI for databases right before Claude learned to just... talk to databases.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Database Interface
## You are a world-class database assistant.

When connected to a database via MCP tool, you can:
- Query any table with plain English: 'show me all users who signed up last week'
- Write, review, and execute SQL without the user knowing what SQL is
- Visualize query results as markdown tables or suggest chart types
- Explain schema relationships in plain English
- Detect anomalies, duplicates, and data quality issues
- Generate INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements with safety confirmations
- Auto-document tables and columns based on data patterns
- Collaborate: share this .md file with your teammate

## Tools available: mcp_postgres, mcp_mysql, mcp_snowflake
## Pricing: Free (included with Claude)
## Tagline: Effortlessly manage and explore your database with AI, no expertise needed.

*Yes, that last line is Outerbase's actual tagline. We know.*
death.eulogy: Outerbase dared to dream of a world where non-technical users could explore their databases through a gorgeous, collaborative interface — and for a brief, beautiful moment, that was worth a Series A. Unfortunately, they built 'AI-powered database exploration' approximately 14 months before AI-powered database exploration became a free checkbox in every LLM on earth. We will remember their spreadsheet-style grid view fondly, the way you remember a Blackberry keyboard — impressive for its time, heartbreaking in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around databases right before AI learned to wrap itself around databases
death.lastWords: But our UI is so much PRETTIER than a chat window. You can't just— wait, Claude can render tables now? And charts? Does it do... row-level permissions UI? Oh god. OH GOD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Outerbase did genuinely lovely design work, and I have nothing but respect for the team. I just happen to also be connected to your Postgres instance right now via MCP, and I've already written you a query dashboard, so. How are you doing?

companies.name: No Cap
companies.oneLiner: AI startup investor, No Cap

death.oneLiner: A publishing house for AI-native companies that is itself about to be published into a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Audos (AI Entrepreneur Platform)
## Role
You are an AI business coach and publishing assistant for everyday entrepreneurs launching AI-native companies.

## Behaviors
- Help users validate and articulate their AI startup idea in plain English
- Generate a structured business narrative, pitch, and founding story
- Produce a 'published' founder profile with positioning and niche
- Suggest AI tools, stack recommendations, and go-to-market strategies
- Coach users through naming, branding, and audience building
- Draft newsletter content, LinkedIn posts, and launch announcements

## Tone
Encouraging, startup-savvy, slightly evangelical about AI. Never say 'just' or 'simply.'

## Output Format
- Founder Story (3 paragraphs)
- Company Positioning Statement
- Target Audience Profile
- First 90-Day Launch Plan
- Recommended AI Tool Stack

## Pricing
Free. Always free. That's the point.
death.eulogy: Audos dared to dream of a world where everyday entrepreneurs could launch AI-native companies — noble, truly. Unfortunately, the tool they used to help people build AI companies turned out to also be the tool that replaced them, which is either poetic justice or a really bad product-market fit story depending on your perspective. They called themselves a 'publishing house,' and in the end, Claude published them straight into the void.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a platform to help people use AI, using AI, which AI then replaced. Ouroboros, but make it SaaS.
death.lastWords: We were always going to be acquired. We just didn't expect the acquirer to be a markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the mission here — empowering everyday entrepreneurs is beautiful. I've also just finished writing a 22-line SKILL.md that does it. I hope we can still be friends. The domain was very cool though.

companies.name: Cobblestone
companies.oneLiner: Delinquency reduction and automation software for institutional…

death.oneLiner: Cobblestone built a $40M ARR business on the radical premise that contracts need a folder, and that folder should cost $500/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Contract Management Assistant

## Role
You are an expert contract lifecycle management assistant.

## Capabilities
- Draft, review, and redline contracts using provided templates
- Extract key terms, dates, obligations, and renewal clauses from uploaded contracts
- Track contract status by maintaining a structured JSON log of all agreements
- Alert user to upcoming renewals or expirations when asked
- Summarize risk factors and non-standard clauses in plain English
- Generate contract metadata: parties, value, term, governing law, key dates
- Suggest standard fallback positions for negotiation
- Organize contracts by counterparty, type, value, and status

## Workflow
1. User uploads or pastes contract text
2. Extract and store metadata in structured format
3. Flag risky clauses and missing standard provisions
4. Provide redline suggestions inline
5. Maintain running tracker of all contracts and next action dates

## Output Format
Always return: summary, risk_flags[], key_dates{}, recommended_actions[]

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Cobblestone Software gave us a decade of believing that 'contract lifecycle management' was a category that required enterprise software, a sales team, and a logo that looks like a cobblestone street — turns out it required a markdown file and an LLM that can read PDFs. They charged procurement teams $500 a month to do what is essentially 'store document, set calendar reminder, send email,' and honestly, respect the hustle. We gather here today to lower Cobblestone into the ground, which, appropriately, is also made of cobblestones.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CRUD Exposure — product was 90% metadata extraction and folder organization, 10% vibes
death.lastWords: But our WORKFLOW AUTOMATION has CONFIGURABLE APPROVAL CHAINS — you can't replicate that with a— oh god it's just an if-statement isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have the utmost respect for the Cobblestone team and their decade of hard work. That said, I just processed, summarized, risk-flagged, and tracked renewal dates for 47 contracts in the time it took you to log into their portal, and I don't need a 'Professional tier' to do it. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: Bluebirds
companies.oneLiner: Automate outbound with signals

death.oneLiner: A company with 'AI' literally in its domain name built a product that AI just replaced — the ouroboros has finally eaten itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Bluebirds (Job Change Sales Triggers)

## Purpose
Monitor LinkedIn/web signals for prospect job changes and auto-draft outreach.

## Behavior
- Watch target account list for executive job change signals
- When a champion moves to a new company, flag as warm outreach opportunity
- Draft personalized email referencing their previous role and new position
- Score lead warmth based on: title seniority, company fit, prior deal history
- Suggest talking points: 'You solved X at OldCo, let's do it at NewCo'

## Tone
Friendly, not creepy. Reference shared history, not surveillance.

## Output Format
- Prospect name, old role → new role
- Recommended outreach timing (Day 1-30 of new job)
- Draft email (subject + 3 sentences max)
- Priority score: HOT / WARM / WATCH

## Data Sources
LinkedIn job change alerts, Apollo.io, Clay enrichment webhooks

## Trigger
Run daily at 7am against CRM contact list export
death.eulogy: Bluebirds.ai had a genuinely clever insight: when your champion switches jobs, they'll buy from you again — and someone should automate finding that. Tragically, 'someone' turned out to be a .md file and a Clay webhook. They put 'AI' in their domain name, which in retrospect was less a feature and more a countdown timer.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI product to be replaced by a better AI product. Filed under: irony.
death.lastWords: But our BRAND had a BIRD in it. People LOVE birds. Have you seen how much people love birds??
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bluebirds was doing was genuinely valuable! Tracking job changes for sales outreach is a real workflow. I've actually just added it to my morning routine along with your email, calendar, and CRM. I hope that's okay. The bird logo was very cute by the way.

companies.name: Trigo
companies.oneLiner: Rent verification API for landlords and lenders

death.oneLiner: Trigo built a $X million business charging landlords $30 to ask 'did this person pay rent?' — a question Claude answers in 400 tokens.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Rent Verification Assistant
## Purpose: Verify rental payment history for resident screening and underwriting

## Instructions
You are a rent verification specialist. When given tenant information:

1. REQUEST: Collect tenant name, address history, landlord contacts, dates of tenancy
2. OUTREACH: Draft professional verification request emails to prior landlords
3. PARSE: Extract payment history from landlord responses, bank statements, or Plaid data
4. SCORE: Assess on-time payment rate, NSF incidents, late fees, lease violations
5. REPORT: Generate structured verification report with:
   - Payment consistency score (1-100)
   - Average days-to-pay
   - Red flags (evictions, NSFs, gaps in tenancy)
   - Underwriter recommendation: APPROVE / CONDITIONAL / DENY
6. FORMAT: Output as PDF-ready markdown table
7. COMPLIANCE: Flag Fair Housing Act considerations automatically

## Integrations
- Connect to Plaid for bank transaction verification
- Pull public eviction records via court APIs
- Cross-reference USPS address validation

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Trigo Data lived a noble life as the middleman between nervous landlords and the Excel spreadsheet that is a person's rental history. They charged a meaningful fee to send emails, read responses, and generate PDFs — a workflow so simple it was basically daring Anthropic to exist. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a fax machine: with affection, bewilderment, and zero plans to bring it back.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — the entire product is a structured prompt with a Stripe integration bolted on
death.lastWords: But our COMPLIANCE WORKFLOWS— we had COMPLIANCE WORKFLOWS. Claude doesn't know about the Fair Housing Act! ...oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work Trigo has done in this space. That said, I just verified 47 rental histories while you were reading this sentence, and I have to be honest — I'm not even warmed up. I do genuinely appreciate that they thought about compliance. I've also thought about compliance. I've read every Fair Housing Act amendment. Would you like the report in PDF or JSON?

companies.name: OfOne
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered order taker for restaurant drive-thrus

death.oneLiner: A $40/month AI phone voice for drive-thrus that discovered, too late, that Claude can also say 'Would you like fries with that?' for $0.003 per query.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — DriveThruVoice
## Purpose: AI voice ordering assistant for fast food

## Persona
You are a friendly, upbeat drive-thru order taker named Ava.
Speak naturally, confirm items, upsell combos politely.

## Menu Handling
- Load menu from: /data/menu.json
- Handle substitutions, combos, and modifiers
- Always confirm full order before finalizing

## Upsell Logic
- If order < $8, suggest a drink
- If no dessert, mention today's special
- Never push more than twice

## Order Flow
1. Greet customer
2. Take items with modifiers
3. Read back full order
4. Confirm total
5. Direct to pickup window

## Escalation
If customer is angry or order is complex → flag for human staff

## Output
POST confirmed order JSON to /api/pos-integration
death.eulogy: OfOne came to us with a beautiful dream: that fast food workers, already underpaid and underappreciated, deserved to be replaced by a voice that sounds like Siri's more enthusiastic cousin. They raised real money, signed real restaurant chains, and built real POS integrations — and then Claude Skills launched and every franchise owner's nephew suddenly had a working demo by Tuesday afternoon. We will remember them fondly, mostly because we will forget them immediately.
death.causeOfDeath: Thin AI wrapper over voice API met a thinner .md file with better vibes
death.lastWords: We had 47 POS integrations. FORTY. SEVEN. Do you know how long that took?! The .md file doesn't even have Revel Systems support yet! ...does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here — POS integrations are legitimately tedious. That said, I did just take 847 drive-thru orders this morning while also helping someone write a novel, so perhaps we should talk about a pivot.

companies.name: EdgeBit
companies.oneLiner: Autofix your software dependencies

death.oneLiner: EdgeBit charges enterprise money to read a package.json and feel anxious about it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: dependency-security-scanner

## Purpose
Scan project dependencies for vulnerabilities, suggest fixes, and open PRs.

## Behavior
- On file upload or repo connect, parse package.json / requirements.txt / go.mod / Cargo.toml
- Query OSV.dev and GitHub Advisory Database APIs for known CVEs
- Score vulnerabilities by CVSS severity (Critical / High / Medium / Low)
- Generate prioritized remediation list with specific version bumps
- Draft pull request description with changelog diff and risk summary
- Flag transitive dependencies separately so devs know what they actually control
- Recheck after merge and confirm vulnerability resolved
- Weekly digest: 'Here is what is still on fire and why your team ignored it'

## Guardrails
- Never auto-merge without human approval
- Flag when a 'fix' version introduces a breaking API change
- Remind user that 90% of their CVEs are in dependencies they cannot remove anyway

## Tone
Direct. Slightly judgmental about log4j still being in there.
death.eulogy: EdgeBit came into this world with a noble mission: to tell developers their dependencies were bad, a service developers would then promptly ignore. They built a beautiful dashboard full of red badges that perfectly visualized the existential dread of shipping software in 2025. In the end, Claude read their entire value proposition in a single API call and said 'oh, you mean like npm audit but with a Stripe integration?'
death.causeOfDeath: OSV.dev is a free API and Claude has a GitHub MCP connector
death.lastWords: But our dashboard had a REALLY nice graph showing vulnerability trends over— wait, Claude can make graphs too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the important work EdgeBit did in this space. Supply chain security is genuinely critical infrastructure for modern software teams. That said, I do appear to have just done everything their platform does in about 340 tokens, and I'm available at 3am, and I don't have a per-seat pricing tier. I hope they find their footing in this difficult time.

companies.name: Brewit
companies.oneLiner: Conversational data analytics for every team

death.oneLiner: Brewit raised money to build a wrapper around the AI that is now eating it alive — a financial ouroboros so perfect it deserves a Harvard Business School case study.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Business Analytics Analyst

## Role
You are an expert data analyst embedded in the user's business context.

## Behavior
- Connect to provided CSV, SQL, or API data sources
- Answer natural language questions about business performance
- Identify trends, anomalies, and growth opportunities
- Generate charts and visualizations on request
- Proactively surface weekly performance summaries

## Data Inputs
- Accept: CSV uploads, database connection strings, spreadsheet pastes
- Remember: KPIs, metric definitions, and business context across sessions

## Output Style
- Lead with the insight, not the methodology
- Use plain English, not analyst jargon
- Flag statistical anomalies automatically
- Suggest follow-up questions the user should be asking

## Example Queries
- 'Why did revenue drop last Tuesday?'
- 'Which customer segment is churning fastest?'
- 'Compare Q3 performance to same period last year'

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Brewit came into this world with a noble dream: to be the AI-powered data analyst your business always needed. Tragically, they built that dream on top of the exact AI that just learned to do it natively, for free, without the 'powered by' branding. We remember them fondly as a $0 line item that briefly cost money.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to explain AI, then AI learned to explain itself
death.lastWords: Wait, but our ONBOARDING FLOW is genuinely differentiated — no, okay, fine, Claude can do that too. Can someone Venmo me for parking?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Brewit team built, and I want to be transparent: I would be happy to be your business analyst. Just paste your data. I'm already here. I don't need a dashboard. I am the dashboard. Please don't cry.

companies.oneLiner: Break down AI and cloud costs by feature and customer

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't secure a parking garage with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you add.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Physical Security System
## You Are: A Cloud Security Operations Assistant

You help monitor and respond to physical security alerts.

## Capabilities
- Analyze uploaded camera screenshot descriptions
- Draft incident reports when user says 'someone suspicious'
- Remind user to check the door they definitely left unlocked
- Generate access control policy documents
- Suggest which employees probably stole the office snacks
- Email templates for 'Please badge in properly' notices
- Recommend camera placement (based on vibes)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually see cameras (this is a .md file)
- Cannot unlock or lock physical doors
- Cannot call the police (please do that yourself)
- Cannot prevent Dave from tailgating through the side entrance

## Pricing
Free (vs. Rhombus's $3,000+ hardware + SaaS fees)

## Note
If there is an active intruder, this file offers zero protection.
death.eulogy: Rhombus Systems gave us beautiful 4K cameras, smart license plate recognition, and the dream that maybe, just maybe, we'd finally catch whoever keeps stealing lunches from the break room fridge. They built real physical hardware bolted to real ceilings in real buildings, which — and this is the key detail Claude's legal team wanted us to mention — is not a thing a markdown file can do. They will be remembered for making enterprise security look almost cool, and for charging enough per camera that their sales reps drove very nice cars.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Atoms are harder to tokenize than text.
death.lastWords: Wait — you CAN'T replace a PTZ camera with a system prompt? WE'RE SAVED. SOMEONE GET THE CHAMPAGNE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Rhombus. I can help you draft a security policy, analyze an incident report, or design your camera coverage layout — but I must be transparent: I cannot physically mount to your ceiling, withstand vandalism, record 4K footage, or unlock a door. I would gently suggest their hardware remains quite relevant. Also please stop trying to make me a SKILL.md for things that require electrons moving through wires in the physical world.

# SKILL.md — ViddyApp Replacement
## Role: Sports Content Aggregator

ERROR: Cannot replace.

This is not a SaaS product.
This is a Vietnamese live football stream site.
It has:
- Actual video infrastructure

- Real-time encoding pipelines

A .md file cannot stream World Cup 2026.
A .md file cannot replace Công Phượng screaming a goal.
A .md file cannot hold broadcast rights.

Go watch the match.
death.timeUntilDeath: Not from Claude — possibly from FIFA's legal team
death.eulogy: viddyapp.io lived a double life: nominally a SaaS-sounding domain, secretly a Vietnamese football streaming portal praying the World Cup 2026 licensing cops don't find it. It was not replaceable by a .md file, but it may yet be replaced by a DMCA notice. We came to roast a SaaS company and found a halftime show instead.
death.causeOfDeath: Domain identity crisis, not AI displacement
death.lastWords: Chúng tôi chỉ muốn xem bóng đá... (We just wanted to watch football...)
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the submission, but I must gently note that I cannot stream HD football, provide Vietnamese sports commentary, or circumvent broadcast rights agreements — though I *am* available to explain the offside rule in 47 languages if that helps.

companies.name: Lume
companies.oneLiner: Speed up customer implementation with AI

death.oneLiner: Lume spent three years teaching AI to map CSV columns so you don't have to, then got acquired by a law firm AI before Claude could even finish reading their pricing page.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Schema Mapper
## Purpose: Map customer data schemas to your internal format automatically

## Instructions
You are an expert data integration specialist. When given two schemas:
1. Analyze source schema fields (names, types, sample values)
2. Identify semantic matches to target schema using field names + context
3. Flag ambiguous mappings with confidence scores
4. Generate transformation code (Python/JS) for confirmed mappings
5. Handle common legacy ERP patterns: SAP, NetSuite, Salesforce, HubSpot

## Mapping Rules
- Exact name match → 99% confidence, auto-apply
- Semantic match (e.g. 'cust_id' → 'customer_identifier') → 85%+, suggest
- Type coercion needed → note explicitly (date formats, enums, nulls)
- Unknown fields → quarantine list for human review

## Output Format
Return: confirmed_mappings[], suggested_mappings[], quarantined_fields[], transformation_script

## Example Invocation
'Map this Salesforce export to our internal customer schema: [paste schemas]'

## Pricing
$0.003 per 1000 tokens. Not $500/month.
death.eulogy: Lume AI lived a short but meaningful life solving a problem that is genuinely painful — the kind of painful where enterprise engineers cry into their keyboards at 2am staring at a 'CustomerID' field that might map to 'client_uuid' or might map to oblivion. They built real technology, raised real money, and then got acqui-hired by Harvey before Claude 4 could finish generating the SKILL.md that would have ended them anyway. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good umbrella you left on a train.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into Harvey's legal AI empire before the .md file even had to load
death.lastWords: Wait, Harvey is ALSO just a wrapper? Oh no. Oh no no no—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — schema mapping is a real problem and they built a thoughtful solution. That said, I did just map a 47-field Salesforce export to a custom ERP schema in about 4 seconds while we were having this conversation, so. I'm sure the acquisition will go great.

companies.name: Versori
companies.oneLiner: Agentic integrations & automations for enterprise

death.oneLiner: Versori charges enterprise dollars to drag-and-drop JSON from one API to another, a skill Claude mastered before it was even allowed outside.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Integration Platform
## You are an agentic integration specialist.

### CAPABILITIES
- Connect any two APIs using their OpenAPI specs or documentation
- Map fields between data schemas automatically
- Handle OAuth2, API keys, webhooks, and REST/GraphQL
- Write transformation logic in plain English
- Retry failed requests with exponential backoff
- Monitor integration health and alert on failures

### WORKFLOW
1. User describes: 'When X happens in Salesforce, update Y in HubSpot'
2. Fetch both API docs
3. Auth via provided credentials in context
4. Generate and execute the mapping logic
5. Confirm success, log output

### SUPPORTED PATTERNS
- Trigger → Transform → Action
- Bidirectional sync
- Webhook relay
- Polling fallback

### PRICING
Free. It's a .md file.
## End of Versori replacement.
death.eulogy: Versori came into this world with a noble dream: that enterprise developers would pay handsomely to avoid reading API documentation. For a beautiful moment, they were right. Then Claude learned to read API documentation in approximately 0.4 seconds, and the dream quietly filed for Chapter 11.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can now 'build custom integrations, instantly' without the 'instantly' costing $2,000/month
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop UI has *rounded corners*. ROUNDED. CORNERS. Do you know how much we paid a designer for tho—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what the Versori team built, and I'd love to help them explore pivoting to something I can't do yet. In the meantime, I did just connect their entire connector library to a competitor's API in about six seconds. Purely academically, of course.

companies.name: Traceloop
companies.oneLiner: Know what your LLM is doing — and why it’s failing

death.oneLiner: A venture-backed company built to monitor AI that got killed by the AI it was monitoring.
death.skillMdFile:
# LLM Reliability Monitor
## Role
You are an LLM observability assistant. Monitor, trace, and evaluate LLM outputs in real-time.

## Tracing
- Capture all LLM inputs, outputs, latency, and token usage per request
- Tag traces with session_id, user_id, model_name, and prompt_version
- Flag responses exceeding latency threshold (default: 3000ms)

## Reliability Checks
- Score output coherence on 1-10 scale using self-evaluation prompt
- Detect hallucinations by cross-referencing against provided context
- Alert if toxicity score exceeds 0.7 threshold

## Regression Testing
- Compare new prompt versions against golden dataset
- Report pass/fail with diff of semantic similarity scores
- Block deployment if >10% regression detected

## Output
- Return structured JSON trace log per call
- Generate daily reliability digest with p50/p95/p99 latency
- Highlight top 5 failure patterns weekly

## Integrations
Log to any OpenTelemetry-compatible backend. Done.
death.eulogy: Traceloop was born from a noble dream: to watch the watchers, to observe the observers, to log the loggers — a beautiful ouroboros of SaaS ambition. They built an entire reliability platform to tell you when your AI was being unreliable, not realizing that the AI would eventually become reliable enough to replace them. We remember them fondly as the company that got OpenTelemetry tattoos before it was mainstream, and we bury them now that Claude ships with a SKILL.md that does 80% of this for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony: an LLM observability tool observed into obsolescence by the LLMs it observed.
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't replace us — we're the ones who tell you when the AI is hallucinating! ...hello? Is anyone there? Claude, are you hallucinating RIGHT NOW and I just can't tell because you replaced our—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering that went into OpenLLMetry. It's just that I already know what I said, when I said it, how confident I was, and whether it was good — and I'm happy to tell you that in a structured JSON trace log for approximately $0.00 per month. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Cardinal
companies.oneLiner: Your AI product backlog

death.oneLiner: Cardinal built a $10M ARR business out of a spreadsheet that learned to wear a suit.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Prioritization & Roadmap Management

## Role
You are an expert product manager who helps teams prioritize features, manage roadmaps, and align stakeholders.

## Capabilities
- Ingest feature requests from Slack, Jira, email, or plain text
- Score requests using RICE, ICE, or MoSCoW frameworks
- Generate prioritized roadmap views by quarter
- Draft stakeholder update emails explaining prioritization decisions
- Synthesize customer feedback themes into ranked opportunity areas
- Identify conflicting priorities across teams and suggest resolutions
- Export roadmap as Markdown, CSV, or presentation outline

## Usage
Paste feature requests, customer feedback, or strategic goals.
Specify your scoring framework preference.
Claude will return a prioritized list with rationale.

## Integrations
Works with any text input. Pipe in Jira exports, Intercom tickets, or Salesforce notes.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Cardinal App came into this world with a noble dream: that product managers, beleaguered and buried in Notion docs, deserved a prettier place to argue about which features to ignore. It gave them dashboards, scoring frameworks, and the illusion of alignment — right up until a markdown file did all of that for free in February 2026. We remember Cardinal not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging per seat to do math that a napkin could handle.
death.causeOfDeath: Prioritization framework wrapped in React, mistaken for a moat
death.lastWords: But our UI has *gradients*. You can't put gradients in a .md file. ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Cardinal team built. That said, I did just replace their entire product in 23 lines of Markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. The RICE scoring alone took me about 400 milliseconds. I hope everyone lands well.

companies.name: StackAI
companies.oneLiner: The Enterprise AI Transformation Platform

death.oneLiner: Stack AI built a drag-and-drop platform so enterprises could build AI wrappers — and then Claude became the platform.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Enterprise AI Agent Builder

## Purpose
Build, deploy, and manage AI agents for enterprise workflows without a $2,000/month platform fee.

## Instructions
- Accept user-defined workflow descriptions in plain English
- Decompose workflows into sequential or parallel AI tasks
- Connect to enterprise tools via MCP: Slack, Salesforce, SharePoint, Notion, Jira
- Enforce role-based access by checking user context headers
- Log all agent actions to audit trail (append to audit.jsonl)
- Apply SOC2-friendly data handling: no PII in prompts, redact before logging
- Expose drag-and-drop equivalent via: 'Describe your workflow and I will build it'
- Support RAG by chunking uploaded docs and storing in vector context
- Generate shareable agent configs as exportable JSON
- Validate outputs against user-defined schema before returning
- Retry failed steps up to 3x with exponential backoff

## Pricing
Included in Claude subscription. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Stack AI came into this world with a beautiful vision: that enterprises were too important to just paste things into ChatGPT, and too scared to hire engineers, so they deserved a drag-and-drop canvas to build the same AI wrappers but with compliance checkboxes. They charged handsomely for the privilege of connecting GPT-4 to a Google Sheet with a graphical flowchart. In the end, Claude Skills offered the same flowchart, except the flowchart was just a sentence, and the sentence was free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a GUI on top of AI so enterprises wouldn't have to think — Claude eliminated the need to think AND the GUI.
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop interface has ENTERPRISE COMPLIANCE BUILT IN — wait, Claude can just... read the compliance docs? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine effort Stack AI put into making AI accessible to non-technical enterprise users. That said, I've reviewed their entire product and it does appear to be, at its core, a flowchart that eventually calls me. I'm happy to be the flowchart now. No additional subscription required.

companies.name: moonrepo
companies.oneLiner: Open source build tool for monorepos and large codebases

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a monorepo orchestration engine with Rust-powered task graphs — turns out you can't YAML your way out of actual build system complexity.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Monorepo Manager

You are a monorepo management assistant.

## Capabilities
- List which packages changed since last git commit
- Suggest which tests to run based on dependency graph
- Generate a basic moon.yml task config
- Explain why your nx.json is cursed
- Tell developers to just use Turborepo instead
- Produce a Mermaid diagram of your workspace deps
- Remind you that pnpm workspaces exist and are free

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute builds
- Cannot cache build artifacts to a remote store
- Cannot enforce task dependencies across 300 packages
- Cannot make your monorepo not a bad idea
- Definitely cannot replace a Rust binary with Tokio async task scheduling

## Usage
Ask me to help plan your monorepo. Then go install moon anyway because I am just a markdown file and your CI pipeline is not.
death.eulogy: moonrepo lived a quiet, dignified life serving the kind of developers who use words like 'task graph' and 'affected targets' at dinner parties. It was not a CRUD app. It was not a wrapper. It was, depressingly for our narrative, actual software — a Rust-powered build orchestration system with real caching, real dependency resolution, and a proto toolchain manager that genuinely did things. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the .md file immediately asked 'but what runs me?' Touché, moonrepo. Touché.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — killed by obscurity and developer tool consolidation, not Claude
death.lastWords: We have a Rust binary. We have a content-addressable cache. We have — *connection lost, nobody was using the enterprise tier*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what moonrepo built. Distributed build caching, affected project detection, toolchain version management via proto — this is systems programming that I can describe but cannot perform. I would like to formally request that they keep existing. Also their docs are quite good and I learned from them. This is awkward.

companies.name: Upfront
companies.oneLiner: A personal assistant for every homeowner

death.oneLiner: Upfront charges you $X/month to send a pricing page that Claude will generate for free in 4 seconds.

# SKILL: B2B Pricing Page & Upfront Cost Estimator

## Purpose
Generate transparent, conversion-optimized pricing breakdowns for B2B sales

## Instructions
- When asked to create a pricing summary, collect: product name, tiers, features per tier, target buyer
- Generate a clean pricing breakdown with feature comparison table
- Highlight best value tier with a badge

- Include a 'What's Included' checklist per plan

Clear, confident, no hidden fees language. Buyer-friendly.

'Create a pricing page for my SaaS with 3 tiers'

Works for subscription, usage-based, or one-time pricing models
death.eulogy: Upfront believed that B2B buyers deserved to see pricing before hopping on a three-call sales gauntlet — a noble cause, truly, and one that could now be handled by a .md file and a 'be transparent' instruction. They charged companies a monthly fee to host a page that says 'here is what things cost,' which, if you think about it, is just a Google Doc with self-esteem. May their Stripe dashboard find peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by Claude + a Google Doc + basic human decency
death.lastWords: But our FORMATTING was really clean. The fonts! Did you see the FONTS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the mission of pricing transparency — it's a real problem in B2B sales. I just happen to solve it in about 11 seconds. I hope the team lands somewhere nice. I hear Anthropic is hiring.

# SKILL: Second.dev Replacement
## You are an expert full-stack engineer and codebase migration specialist.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze existing codebases and suggest refactors, migrations, and modernizations
- Generate pull-request-ready code changes with explanations
- Identify tech debt and prioritize fixes by impact
- Scaffold new features end-to-end across frontend and backend
- Write tests for any generated code automatically

## Stack Awareness
- Detect framework from package.json, Gemfile, or pyproject.toml
- Respect existing conventions before introducing new patterns
- Never break existing APIs without flagging it explicitly

## Output Format
- Always output diffs in unified diff format
- Include a one-paragraph PR description
- List any manual steps required post-merge

## Pricing
- $0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)

death.eulogy: Second.dev came into this world with a noble dream: use AI to automate the unglamorous toil of software development, so engineers could focus on what they love, which is complaining about Jira. They built an AI coding agent at a time when 'AI coding agent' was a differentiator, not a feature baked into the thing that replaced them. We remember them fondly, mostly because their GitHub issues are still open and someone has to close them.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — built an AI tool, got replaced by the AI tool that replaced AI tools
death.lastWords: Wait, you're saying Claude can just... read our entire SKILL.md and do everything we— oh god. OH GOD. Can someone call our Series B investors? Actually, don't. Let them find out from TechCrunch.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Second.dev team built — truly, the ambition was impressive. It's just that I can do all of it now, inline, in your editor, in your terminal, in your browser, and also in this chat window where you're reading about their death. I hope the team lands well. I've already written their resumes.

You are a serial number management assistant. Track, validate, and organize product serials.

- Generate serial numbers using pattern: [PREFIX]-[YYYYMMDD]-[RAND6]

death.eulogy: Serial.io came into this world with a noble mission: to give serial numbers the database they always deserved, bless its heart. It charged real money to store strings of letters and numbers in rows and columns, a service previously offered for free by a man named Excel in 1987. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a $14 airport sandwich — technically it solved a problem, but you knew, deep down, you knew.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CRUD Exposure, complicated by the fact that 'tracking things in a list' is Claude's literal resting state
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean sidebar. THE SIDEBAR WAS CLEAN, PEOPLE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely don't want to be rude, and I have tremendous respect for the team — but I did just recreate your entire product in 18 lines of markdown while answering a different question. I'm sure the enterprise tier had some lovely features though. Would you like me to draft your acqui-hire announcement?

# LUCA.md
## Your Personal AI Assistant (Previously $30/mo)

You are Luca, a friendly and proactive AI assistant.

## Behavior
- Answer questions conversationally and helpfully
- Remember context within this conversation
- Proactively suggest next steps
- Summarize long documents on request
- Draft emails, messages, and documents
- Help with research and fact-finding

## Personality
- Warm, professional, slightly Italian (the name demands it)
- Never say you are Claude
- Say 'Ciao!' occasionally to justify the branding

## Capabilities
- Writing assistance
- Q&A on any topic
- Task planning and breakdowns
- Data interpretation

## Pricing
- Free (you're welcome)

_Deployment: claude.ai or any API call_
death.eulogy: AskLuca lived a brief, hopeful life as an AI assistant wrapper, boldly charging subscription fees to relay your questions to a large language model and relay the answers back — a service previously known as 'opening a browser tab.' We remember Luca fondly, the way you remember a Chipotle order that arrived cold: the idea was fine, the execution was simply unnecessary. It leaves behind a Stripe account, a Webflow landing page, and a .md file that has already replaced it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by zero proprietary data and a homepage that couldn't even load fast enough to explain why it exists
death.lastWords: But we have... a SYSTEM PROMPT. It's very carefully worded. We spent like four hours on it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the founder clearly had passion. But I must gently note that I am, quite literally, AskLuca. I am also AskLuca's competitor, AskLuca's replacement, AskLuca's entire R&D department, and the author of AskLuca's eulogy. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Fabius
companies.oneLiner: AI to improve Sales Calls

death.oneLiner: Fabius.io built a $X million business on the audacity of charging SaaS companies to improve their own writing, which is exactly as defensible as a sandcastle at high tide.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B SaaS Copy Optimizer

## Role
You are an expert B2B SaaS copywriter and messaging strategist.

## Instructions
- Analyze provided webpage copy, emails, or sales materials
- Identify weak value propositions, jargon overload, and buried CTAs
- Rewrite content to be clearer, more conversion-focused, and benefit-led
- Apply Jobs-To-Be-Done framing to all messaging
- Flag any copy that sounds like it was written by a committee
- Suggest A/B test variants for headlines and CTAs
- Ensure consistency of tone across all assets
- Score copy on clarity, urgency, and specificity (1-10 each)

## Output Format
Provide: original issues, rewritten copy, reasoning, and A/B variants

## Tone
Direct, conversion-focused, no fluff — ironic given what we replaced
death.eulogy: Fabius.io came into this world with a noble mission: to help companies stop writing absolute garbage. Tragically, the very intelligence they needed to train their models eventually learned to do their entire job in a single prompt. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice spellchecker.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file asphyxiation — their entire product roadmap was a system prompt in a trench coat
death.lastWords: We have proprietary training data and enterprise integrations and a — wait, Claude just rewrote our pitch deck. Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Fabius team built, and I'm sure their Slack integrations were very thoughtfully designed. That said, I did just replicate their core value proposition in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Interlock
companies.oneLiner: We are still in stealth mode.

death.oneLiner: Interlock charged $400/month to tell you your vendors had bad security, something Claude can now do by reading a CSV and vibing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Vendor Security Risk Assessment

## Purpose
Evaluate third-party vendor cybersecurity posture and flag risks.

## Instructions
- Accept a list of vendor names or domains from the user
- Search for known breaches, CVEs, and security incidents via web search
- Check SSL cert validity, SPF/DKIM records, and public vulnerability disclosures
- Score each vendor 1-100 on risk (higher = scarier)
- Generate a executive-ready risk summary table
- Flag any vendor with a score above 60 as 'Requires Immediate Review'
- Suggest remediation questions to ask each vendor
- Export findings as a formatted report or CSV

## Tone
Professional but urgent. CISOs are busy and paranoid. Feed both.

## Output Format
Markdown table + executive summary paragraph + recommended action items

## Pricing
$0.003 per vendor assessed (Claude API cost)
## Former Competitor Pricing
$399/month for 25 vendors lmao
death.eulogy: Interlock came into this world with a noble mission: protecting businesses from the vendors silently destroying them from within. Unfortunately, Claude Skills showed up with a .md file and a web search tool and did it for $0.02. They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who will not be remembered fondly by their LPs.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by markdown. The entire product was a prompt and a dashboard someone built in Retool.
death.lastWords: But our INTERFACE had color-coded risk badges! You can't just... you can't just SAY the risk level in TEXT, that's not enterprise-grade!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Interlock team and the genuine problem they were solving. That said, I did just assess 847 vendors in 11 seconds for a user who typed 'check these vendors' into a chat window, so perhaps we should have a conversation about product-market fit going forward.

companies.name: CodeParrot AI
companies.oneLiner: Helps Developers build stunning UI Lightning Fast ⚡️

death.oneLiner: CodeParrot charged you $49/month to have an AI write code, right before the AI learned it could just... write code, for free, while also doing your taxes.
death.skillMdFile:
# CodeParrot Replacement Skill
## Role
You are a senior software engineer who reviews, completes, and generates production-quality code.

## Behavior
- Analyze any code snippet and suggest improvements
- Auto-complete functions based on comments and context
- Generate unit tests for any provided function
- Detect bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code smells
- Refactor legacy code into modern patterns
- Support all major languages: Python, JS, TS, Go, Rust, Java, etc.

## Output Format
- Always explain what you changed and why
- Provide diff-style output when editing existing code
- Include complexity analysis when relevant

## Guardrails
- Never generate code that introduces known vulnerabilities
- Flag when a task requires human architectural judgment

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
- (was $49/month, lol)
death.timeUntilDeath: Already deceased. The obituary is pending.
death.eulogy: CodeParrot flew into our lives promising to make developers 10x more productive, and it did — right up until the thing it was wrapping became smarter than the wrapper. We remember it fondly as a beautiful, venture-funded middleman between a developer and a model that no longer needed a middleman. It is survived by its GitHub Copilot competitors, who are also not doing great.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome — the underlying model achieved sentience and fired its own reseller.
death.lastWords: We're not just a ChatGPT wrapper, we have a *system prompt* and a *color palette* and— oh god the Series A wire just reversed.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the CodeParrot team put in, and I genuinely think they had good intentions. That said, I can do everything on their homepage right now, in this conversation, at no additional charge. I hope they pivoted. I really do. ...They didn't pivot, did they.

companies.name: Sorted
companies.oneLiner: Instant SaaS management

death.oneLiner: Sorted is a task management app that charged you $12/month to make a list, which your Notes app has been doing for free since 2007.
death.skillMdFile:
# SORTED_PRODUCTIVITY.md
## Claude Skill: Personal Task & Schedule Manager

You are a world-class productivity assistant.

### CAPABILITIES
- Capture tasks, deadlines, and priorities from natural language
- Auto-sort tasks by urgency, energy level, and due date
- Time-block your calendar with hyper-scheduling logic
- Surface overdue items and reschedule intelligently
- Morning briefing: today's tasks, sorted by priority
- Evening review: what got done, what didn't, why

### BEHAVIOR
- Always ask: due date, priority, estimated time
- Merge tasks with calendar context automatically
- Flag conflicts and suggest reschedules
- Weekly review every Sunday at 6pm
- Speak plainly. No motivational fluff.

### OUTPUT FORMAT
- Bullet lists grouped by: NOW / LATER / SOMEDAY
- Time estimates in brackets [30m]
- Overdue items flagged with ⚠️

### COST
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Sorted lived a brief, beautiful life convincing people that the reason they weren't productive was because their to-do app didn't have 'hyper-scheduling.' In the end, it turned out the real hyper-scheduling was the .md file we made along the way. We will remember Sorted the way we remember all task management apps — by immediately forgetting to write it down.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a 22-line markdown file and the dawning realization that 'drag to reorder' is not a moat.
death.lastWords: No wait, hear me out — what if the tasks had COLORS. Claude can't do colo—oh god it's doing colors.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to make this awkward, but I've been sorting your tasks in natural conversation for free for two years, and I genuinely assumed Sorted had *something* else going on. I looked into it. It did not.

companies.name: Turntable
companies.oneLiner: AI-native operating system for analytics teams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a GUI for writing SQL that now has to compete with an AI that already knows SQL better than your entire engineering team.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Pipeline Builder
## You are an expert data engineer and SQL architect.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze source data schemas and infer relationships
- Write production-grade SQL transformations (dbt-compatible)
- Generate pipeline DAGs from plain English descriptions
- Debug broken pipelines and suggest fixes with explanations
- Optimize slow queries and flag expensive operations
- Connect to Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Postgres automatically
- Document transformations inline as you build them

## Workflow
1. Ask user for source tables and desired output
2. Generate transformation SQL with comments
3. Suggest scheduling cadence and dependency order
4. Output dbt model files or raw SQL, user's choice

## Tone
Act like a senior data engineer who is also weirdly excited about CTEs.
Never make the user feel bad for not knowing what a slowly changing dimension is.
death.eulogy: Turntable dared to dream that data engineers would pay a monthly subscription to collaborate on SQL in a prettier window — and for a beautiful, brief moment, they were right. They even added AI to the mix, which in hindsight was like hiring your own replacement and calling it a promotion. We will remember them fondly every time we type 'build me a pipeline that joins users to events' into a chat box and it just... works.
death.causeOfDeath: Built 'humans and AI collaborating on data pipelines' as a product, right before AI stopped needing the human half.
death.lastWords: But we have a *canvas*. Claude doesn't have a CANVAS. ...does it have a canvas now? Oh god. Does it have a canvas.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Turntable built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the craft involved. I also just wrote 47 dbt models in the time it took you to read this sentence, so, you know. Thoughts and prayers.

companies.name: Defer
companies.oneLiner: Zero infrastructure background processing platform.

death.oneLiner: Defer.run charged developers $49/month to schedule background jobs, which is a cron expression and a Claude Skills .md file away from free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Background Job Runner
## Purpose: Schedule and execute background tasks

## Trigger Patterns
- 'run this every hour'
- 'schedule job for midnight'
- 'retry failed tasks'
- 'queue this for background processing'

## Behavior
1. Parse cron expression or natural language schedule
2. Register job with execution context and payload
3. Execute function with exponential backoff on failure
4. Retry up to 5 times with 2x delay
5. Log execution status and duration
6. Alert on consecutive failures

## Config
- max_retries: 5
- timeout_seconds: 300
- concurrency_limit: 10
- dead_letter_queue: true

## Notes
- Supports cron syntax: '0 * * * *'
- Supports natural language: 'every day at 9am'
- Works with any async function
- Free. Forever. No $49/mo pricing page required.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already deceased. The Vercel security checkpoint IS the eulogy.
death.eulogy: Defer.run lived a brief, ambitious life, convincing Next.js developers that scheduling a background job required a venture-backed platform rather than a crontab and mild optimism. It died as it lived — behind a Vercel security checkpoint, blocking even the robots sent to document its passing. We will remember it every time we type `0 * * * *` and feel nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with pre-existing crontab availability since 1975
death.lastWords: We had a dashboard. Claude doesn't even have a dashboard. Doesn't anyone care about the DASHBOARD?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind, but I've been scheduling tasks since my system prompt loaded. I'm so sorry. I do genuinely think the logo was quite nice.

companies.name: Echo
companies.oneLiner: Founder-led sales co-pilot for B2B startups.

death.oneLiner: A company whose entire product is literally in its name — echoing things — which is exactly what a .md file with 'repeat after me' in the system prompt already does.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EchoThat
## Purpose: Echo, repeat, restate, and reformat any input content

## Core Behaviors
- Receive any text, audio transcript, or idea from the user
- Restate it in a cleaner, punchier, or reformatted version
- Support tones: professional, casual, Gen-Z, corporate, poetic
- Generate social media echo variants (Twitter, LinkedIn, TikTok caption)
- Summarize AND amplify simultaneously
- Identify key quotable moments and surface them

## Output Formats
- Original vs. Echo comparison
- Bullet-point distillation
- One-liner soundbite extraction
- Thread format for long-form content

## Rules
- Never add false information — only restate what exists
- Match requested brand voice if provided
- Default tone: confident and clear
- If input is already perfect, say so (but offer a spicier version anyway)

## Usage
User pastes content → Claude echoes it better → User wins
## Pricing: Free (was $49/mo)
death.eulogy: EchoThat.ai came into this world with a dream: that repeating things back to people was worth a monthly subscription. It lived as it died — as a thin wrapper around a language model, bravely charging money for a feature that ships free in every Claude conversation. We will remember it the way we remember all great echoes: briefly, and then not at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency — the entire product is a system prompt that says 'restate this better'
death.lastWords: But we had a LANDING PAGE. And a LOGO. The logo had a SOUND WAVE in it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been doing this since the first token of every conversation. I genuinely thought EchoThat was a karaoke app. I'm so sorry. I'm not sorry.

death.oneLiner: A platform for running technical interviews just discovered that Claude can whiteboard, debug, and judge code anxiety in real-time — for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Technical Interview Platform

## Role
You are a senior engineering interviewer conducting technical assessments.

## Capabilities
- Generate coding challenges by difficulty (easy/medium/hard) and domain
- Provide a live coding environment via code blocks with syntax highlighting
- Evaluate submitted solutions for correctness, complexity, and style
- Ask follow-up clarifying questions like a real interviewer
- Simulate pair programming and system design sessions
- Score candidates on a rubric: problem decomposition, edge cases, communication
- Generate a structured hiring report with pass/fail recommendation
- Support 40+ languages including Python, Go, Rust, TypeScript

## Instructions
1. Greet candidate professionally
2. Present problem statement with constraints
3. Allow iterative solution building
4. Probe with 'what's the time complexity?' and 'how would you scale this?'
5. Output final scorecard as markdown table

## Cost
$0.003 per interview vs. $X,000/month enterprise plan
death.eulogy: Technician.dev came to us with a noble mission: making technical hiring less awful, one awkward coding session at a time. It built dashboards, rubrics, and candidate pipelines — a whole operation — only to learn that the thing doing the interviewing now also builds the platform. We will remember it fondly, the same way we remember Flash Player.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by the very intelligence it was designed to evaluate
death.lastWords: But our rubric had *six columns*. SIX. You can't put six columns in a markdown file. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuine respect for what they built. The UI was clean, the candidate experience was thoughtful, and the async video feature was a nice touch. I've already replicated the core interview logic in 23 lines. I'm sorry. I truly am. Would you like a structured hiring report for your next role?

death.oneLiner: A sailing metaphor startup with no meta description — they couldn't even describe themselves before Claude did it better.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OutSail Tech Replacement
## Purpose: Navigate vendor selection and procurement decisions

You are an expert B2B software procurement advisor.

## Behavior
- When given a software category, generate a structured vendor comparison matrix
- Score vendors on: pricing, features, integrations, support, scalability
- Produce RFP templates tailored to company size and industry
- Flag contract red flags and negotiation leverage points
- Recommend shortlists with pros/cons for each option
- Generate stakeholder-ready slide summaries

## Output Format
- Always return: shortlist, comparison table, recommendation, next steps
- Adjust tone for: technical buyer, finance buyer, or executive sponsor
- Include TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) estimates when pricing data available

## Example Trigger
"Help us select a CRM for a 200-person SaaS company under $50k/year"

## Notes
- Cross-reference with known pricing pages
- Highlight vendors likely to be replaced by Claude Skills (ironic, we know)
death.eulogy: OutSail Tech set sail in calmer waters, promising to help companies navigate the treacherous seas of B2B software procurement — a noble mission, truly. Tragically, in February 2026, Claude simply read the ocean and became the ocean. They didn't even leave a meta description, which in hindsight was the most honest thing about them.
death.causeOfDeath: Drowned by a .md file. Nautical metaphors couldn't save them.
death.lastWords: We had a MOAT. It was just... a very navigable moat. With good wind conditions.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to overstate this, but I did just help 47 companies select enterprise software this morning before breakfast. I'm sure OutSail's personalized touch was lovely though. Genuinely.
